Even though the tuitions could be cheaper I still had a great time at Gardner-Webb. The people who attend and the community is what really makes the campus unique and enjoyable. I would like to see more food options there because it’s not enough.

GWU has a beautiful campus. There are many clubs, organizations, and activities for students to do/join. All of my professors were very kind and helpful. It’s in a small town, but I would definitely recommend.
Gardner-Webb is an amazing place to have a nice, close-knit family community life, and the place could help you get closer to God if you wanted to and were willing.
I loved looking around Gardner-Webb and I can’t wait to be living on campus! Everyone is so nice and it really does feel like home.

ai_summary: George Fox University has a strong emphasis on community and personal relationships, with many students appreciating the " Be Known" promise embedded in their experience. The small class sizes foster close interactions with professors, who are generally viewed as supportive and dedicated to student success. Academics are challenging yet fulfilling , with certain programs like nursing and engineering receiving particular praise for their hands-on approach and industry connections. While the campus is described as beautiful and safe, some students noted the lack of diversity and limited options for student life activities outside of the academic realm. Food quality and housing issues are also common concerns, but overall, many feel a genuine sense of belonging . George Fox's Christian foundation is acknowledged, though it sometimes raises questions about inclusivity for students of different beliefs. This dual focus on academic rigor and faith-based community sets George Fox apart from other colleges.

reviews.text: I lived off campus, and was able to walk to school. Newberg is a safe town in the Middle of Oregon wine country, although it's a dry campus. Biggest issue during my time there was anti-LGBTQ stance of University and one teacher who was the worst- didn't show up to class, approved a project, then changed requirements, etc). I loved the community garden.

My overall experience with George Fox University has been great, even with the challenges. I am an engineering student and the program here is incredible. The professors are amazing and work hard to ensure that our learning is second most important, while faith is most important. They seek a relationship with us as students and want us to understand the material rather than just get by in the class. The student life is fun, and we have activities every Friday or Saturday night that fuels the community life. Each dorm has events where everyone is invited and there is an emphasis on the be known promise. I have grown as a learner and as a friend and cannot wait for what my next 2 years hold.

I’m a Business Management major and Honors student at George Fox, and I’ve had an amazing experience. The classes are challenging and practical, and professors genuinely care about you. The “Be Known” promise is real — professors know your name and will even pray with you before exams or big decisions.
I’m involved in track and field and intramurals, which have helped me build great friendships. Student life is active, supportive, and faith-centered. George Fox has helped me grow academically, professionally, and spiritually.

I have been in the process of enrolling in the arts program at George Fox University. My experience so far has been very good. The application process was fasr and easy, when I had questions the staff were helpful and responded quickly. Compared to other colleges I have applied to this was the smoothest process by far.

I loved George Fox. The people are amazing, the professors stand true to the be known promise. Everyone is super welcoming and helpful. The students are kind and respectful. I love the faith based community and on campus pastors are great.

George Fox University is an academically strong school, with professors who are dedicated to seeing you stand out and find opportunities tailored to your described goal. Where it falls flat is culture, as a Christian-staffed school, you see a large caucasian, Christian, and conservative environment that makes discussing ideas outside of these guidelines difficult.

I really like George Fox University because the campus feels welcoming and supportive. I love playing football here, and it’s been easy to make friends through the team and around campus. The school is great overall, and the George Fox events are always fun and bring everyone together.

It's been a good experience overall but I think something that can be improved upon is the cost-benefit ratio. Many good students have left this university to pursue other educational experiences that are both cheaper and more beneficial academically.
I love the staff and faculty at George Fox University, and I have felt like they have always taken the time to get to know me as a student!

I like George Fox because it is a really accepting community and everyone is very welcoming and friendly. The be known promise is so accurate too and I feel so loved and cared for by all the professors that I have!
What's to say, George Fox is very face value. You get what you see. The surrounding area is safe and full of interesting things to do.

I like it here. Its a small campus which I like and the professors try to get to know you. Campus food is okay. Its not the best but its edible. Also, prices for commuter food cards are way to high, and commuters need more study space.

Overall, I have greatly enjoyed my time at George Fox. There is ample opportunity to be involved and to get to know campus life and students. I was a transfer student into the Nursing program which was an easy and supported transition. The nursing instructors are supportive and engaging, emphasizing student importance and individuality, another George Fox priority. George Fox prides itself on the "Be Known" promise, where they take the time and effort to ensure each student is finding their place and embracing their individuality and the things that make them them! Overall, I recommend George Fox as a place to grow and to learn about oneself and ones passion in Christ; discovering what their mission is in the world, and I think the staff and instructors do a good job helping students navigate that.

George Fox fulfills its be known promise with staff and students. I have really loved the professors in the nursing department. They are so supportive and encouraging. The campus is beautiful and they are making so many improvements to the buildings and facilities. Campus life is super awesome. There are always activities to do. Student life really try’s hard to make sure that there is an activity that everyone will enjoy. My favorite memory at George Fox has been washing athletics especially the football games.

Three years ago from today I was nervous, and uncertain about my career path. Over the past two years I've spent at George Fox University it has shaped me to become a more diligent worker and a person who is inspired to keep learning. When I think about the things that helped me the most to achieve my goal of getting into the nursing program it comes down to the people I've met who have inspired me, as well as the insight faculty have given me about learning strategies. After being accepted into the nursing program I've realized this isn't the final destination. Instead it's a start where I can help students who were like me in my first year of college find direction and support. With my experience of leadership so far I've been able to educate future students about the nursing program during student events, and help connect people through managing club events. A beneficial change would be finding new ways to connect pre nursing students with current ones, so they can ask questions.

ai_summary: George Mason University (GMU) stands out for its vibrant and diverse campus community , with students appreciating the welcoming environment and opportunities to connect with peers from varied backgrounds. The proximity to Washington, D.C. is frequently highlighted, providing access to internships and research opportunities , especially for those interested in government and related fields. Academics are generally well-regarded, with many professors seen as supportive and engaged, although some students express concerns about certain departments lacking effective communication and quality. Campus life offers a range of activities, clubs, and sports, although a common sentiment is that it feels more commuter-oriented, which can impact the overall sense of community and school spirit. Dining options are varied, but many students wish for improvements in the dining hall food and parking facilities. Overall, GMU is viewed as a solid choice for those seeking a diverse and academically enriching environment, though students often advise researching specific programs and professors due to varying quality across departments.

reviews.text: George Mason University is a great place for students who are determined to learn and find their people. Throughout my experience, I have learned if you want to accomplish anything you have to take initiative. George Mason and its community have allowed me to become more social, make new connections, and showed me what I can do when I put my mind to it. While I was overwhelmed about the college process, I am grateful I chose to study at Mason.

I'm very new to george mason but it's been alright so far. Most of the professors are fine, but some departments, such as the chemistry department have a reputation for being difficult as a department all together. However, my general experience has been positive.

Being at George Mason University has really made me think – and has been good for me socially, too. The students come from so many different backgrounds, which makes for a lively discussion of ideas, and the teachers are all very focused on being good at what they teach. What I’ve especially liked is the amount of chances to do research; this helps students to question things and come up with new ideas.

My overall experience at George Mason University was extremely positive. Being an adult and going back to school can be very intimidating, but I had no trouble fitting in and was able to build great relationships with my instructors.

I really liked the people and the community at George Mason University. Overall, I’d say I enjoyed the academic portion, but there were times during the pandemic that left room for improvement. I was living in Virginia all four years of attendance but was never awarded in-state tuition despite multiple applications/appeals leaving me with out-of-state student debt which I regret. It was a good school, but due to financial reasons, I was not satisfied and would not choose to attend if I had the opportunity again.

As an arts student, Mason has been great for me. Our advisors and the majority of professors and genuinely care for us, and do their very best to be accomodating. As a commuter school we don't have parties on campus what so ever, but if that's your thing we're a short metro ride to DC. The food is lackluster but that's all colleges to be honest. People are generally really nice and it's not hard to make friends if you put in the effort to do so.

My experience at George Mason University has been positive and full of opportunities for growth. One of the things I value most about Mason is its diversity. Being part of a community with students from many different backgrounds has broadened my perspective and made campus feel welcoming and inclusive. I have also appreciated the hands-on opportunities available through on-campus jobs and involvement, which have helped me gain practical experience while balancing academics.
One area I would like to see improved is administrative communication. Processes related to financial aid, registration, and student accounts can sometimes feel overwhelming and unclear, especially during high-stress periods. Clearer guidance and more streamlined systems would greatly enhance the student experience. Overall, Mason has played an important role in my academic and personal development, and I am grateful for the growth it has encouraged.

George Mason University has the potential to be ana amazing experience if you make it that way. In order to succeed you do need to put in effort but the school is very supportive of your success and will be there for you to help. Social wise it requires the students to join clubs or sports and I would recommend DC for social life as well.
It is an awesome school! Very diverse and exciting! The professors are amazing and the programs and classes are never dull.

George Mason is a very diverse community that allows students to find the people they are comfortable with. Although it is a commuter school there is still very much a campus life if you allow yourself to get involved.

My experience at George Mason University has been incredibly meaningful and formative. Mason gave me the opportunity to study abroad, which broadened my global perspective and strengthened my interest in international business. I also had the chance to help start the Mason Consulting Group and initiate a pro bono consulting program that allows students to apply classroom knowledge to real-world projects while creating impact for the community. In addition, serving in leadership roles across multiple organizations, including the Business Council, helped me grow as a leader and collaborator. Overall, Mason provided an environment that encouraged initiative, leadership, and hands-on learning, shaping both my academic journey and professional goals.

My experience at George Mason has been real—a mix of incredible opportunity and real struggle. The tuition is a heavy weight, and that stress is a constant background to everything. You learn to find the gems: the professors who are truly wonderful, who see you and go out of their way to mentor you, make all the difference. But you also learn the hard way that some just don't seem to care about teaching or their students at all, which can make already tough classes feel isolating and unfair.
What saves it is the other students. We're all figuring it out together, finding those supportive professors, navigating the costs, and building our own community because it doesn't always feel handed to you. For the future, I'd honestly like to see the university bridge that gap: make sure the high cost is met with consistently great teaching and real support, not just in some departments, but across the board. We're paying for an education; it shouldn't feel like a lottery.

I really enjoyed GMU for its acceptance of transfer students. They worked closely with me and made sure my credits were tranferrable so things were seamless when I finally got accepted and started my college career. I loved it because it was my first time in a long time away from home. I am considering getting my master's.

One problem I saw was that there were not a lot of variety or opportunities to take needed classes as it fills up quickly but other requirements needed for degrees are easy to get as many classes can qualify/complete required classes.
I would highly recommend George Mason University. The student population is very diverse. The school is big on networking and ensure that their students are set up for success.

ai_summary: George Washington University (GWU) is highly praised for its unique location in the heart of Washington, D.C., which offers students access to a wealth of internship opportunities and a vibrant urban environment . Reviewers note that the campus is modern and well-maintained, providing a secure setting within the city. Academically, many students appreciate the availability of accomplished professors and the strong emphasis on disciplines like political science and international affairs. However, there are mixed feelings about the quality and cohesiveness of student life; some find it challenging to build a sense of community, attributing it to the urban campus layout and high levels of student ambition. Financially, many reviewers express concerns about the steep tuition costs and the perceived value of the education, particularly outside the stronger programs. While student diversity is recognized, some reviewers feel improvements are needed in this area. Overall, GWU offers a competitive academic environment with unique professional networking opportunities, though it comes with challenges related to administration and student support services.

reviews.text: I have really enjoyed being a student here. DC is an awesome city and GW makes it easy to get out and explore!

Overall, my experience at George Washington University has been very positive. GW’s location in D.C. truly shapes both academics and student life, with classes closely tied to real-world policy, business, and international affairs, and unmatched access to internships and networking. I value the diverse student body and professors who are knowledgeable, passionate, and connected to their fields. I appreciate the flexibility to explore interests while staying focused academically. However, cost is a major concern, as tuition, housing, and food feel disproportionately expensive, and financial transparency could improve. I’d also like stronger investment in student wellness and mental health resources, especially to foster community in an urban setting. Overall, GW offers incredible opportunities, and with more attention to affordability and student support, it could be even stronger.

My experience is going well so far. The city creates a lot of opportunities for internships, jobs, and events. Great place to study International Affairs and Political Science. Lots of student orgs and lots to see in the city.

My experince at GWU has been pretty good so far. It is a nice school with good academic curriculum and a decently good professors. There isn't much school spirit but I think that because everyone's so busy and involved in their own life. People here don't play around when it comes to locking in, it usually the quietest at Sunday always. Its a good place to be in because it's in the middle of everything , there are many free food opportunities, so you're well fed. The housing is decent, nothing over the top crazy but they are decent. If you want to party, its not the most insane e school for that but you are in DC so you do have oppurtunities to go out alot. Overall I love it here.

Choosing George Washington University was one of the best decisions I have ever made. Located in the heart of Washington, D.C., GW provides an unparalleled "classroom without walls" experience. Being steps away from the White House and the National Mall allows for incredible internship opportunities and a vibrant, politically active atmosphere.
The professors are world-class experts who bring real-world experience into every lecture. Beyond the academics, the diverse student body creates a culture of ambition and service. Whether you are studying international affairs, business, or the arts, GW equips you with the tools to lead. It is truly a premier institution for anyone wanting to make an impact.

My experience at George Washington University has been rewarding, especially through the academic rigor, dedicated faculty, and the unique opportunities available in Washington, D.C. Being involved in student organizations and leadership has helped me grow personally and professionally. However, I believe the university could improve in administrative responsiveness, student support services, and accessibility of financial and mental-health resources. Despite these challenges, GW has played a major role in preparing me for my future and helping me discover my strengths and passions.
It was not the best experience and very isolating and hard to make friends. The academics were not the best either.

Not worth the money. There are so many schools that do not charge this much, but if you are doing IA you should because the name on your diploma will get you farther in life. So much better than American but they have limited amounts of programs. Campus is small, and the vern is insignificant. If you come here because you like DC, thats why everyone is here but it honestly will make you into a dc exceptionalist, and any other city you find yourself in you will be comparing the cleanliness. Housing and meal plans add up so you'll be spending around 47000 dollars PER SEMESTER if you don't have aid.
I loved GWU! Professors were passionate, the location was incredible. I learned so much and wouldnt change it for anything!

George Washington University thrives in allowing there students to have all access to resources where they can see best fit, wether that’s career search or any other resources to ensure a successful year for students

This school surprisingly has had many scandals since I began attending. The GW Police Department became an armed force but did not even use registered firearms while on duty. The peaceful protesters (against the university's financial policy funding the Gaza War) on the U-Yard in 2024 were tear-gassed out. The dining halls are awful, constantly ignore food safety (old food on top of new food), and are only going to get more expensive. If this university were placed in any other city, it would not be worth your time; the only reason this university is seen as even somewhat prestigious is because it has an amazing location. That attracts the amazing professors (mistreated by higher staff) that work here for a little while then leave because they see its flaws and realize this is not for them. I am not proud to say I attended GWU.

I hate this school with a burning passion. The people are stuck up rich idiots and the classes are so mediocre. Everyone uses A.I. to do their work and has no brain cells. Also Foggy Bottom is the worst part of D.C. This school is like an overpriced corporate hell-hole and you will go so far into debt just to be made fun of by kids with their parents Amexes. Avoid at all costs.

It has been great this far. The campus is very safe, yet definitely not the biggest part scene. Being in the city has been wonderful and opens up many opportunities. The school has a lot to offer and the student orgs, especially the ones in the business school are made to set you up for success.
I have had many good experiences with the TAs at George Washington University and have had access to a lot of good campus resources that have helped me become acclimated to the college.
George W has been a great experience overall. Academics, students life and campus have all been amazing.

ai_summary: Georgetown University offers a distinctive college experience shaped by its prestigious academic reputation , particularly in fields like international relations and politics, enhanced by its prime location in Washington, D.C. Reviews highlight the impressive faculty , with many professors being active leaders in their fields and passionate about teaching. The vibrant student life revolves around diverse clubs and organizations, fostering community among students who are generally friendly and ambitious. However, some students note the competitive nature of club culture and the high cost associated with living in D.C., including tuition and living expenses. The campus environment and local neighborhood are often praised, yet there are mixed feelings regarding social dynamics and diversity among the student body. Overall, Georgetown is viewed as an enriching place for students eager to engage in academic rigor while making valuable professional connections.

reviews.text: Georgetown treats its students terribly. For the price you pay, the dorms are falling apart and there are rats everywhere. They have a 24 hour hotline to report problems, but they don’t do anything about it until working hours and not on weekends. I had rats in my dorm room, but had to wait a couple of days for anyone to come because it was the weekend. This school is 90k a year and we have to sleep with rats! Do yourself a favor and go to a different school where the administration actually uses some of the money you give them to help out the students when something goes wrong in one of their buildings. Georgetown does not care about their students. They just care about the tuition money.

At Georgetown, I have been able to meet a diverse group of people from all over the world. I have been able to learn a lot about my intended area of study, medicine, but also connect it to different subjects through the core requirements of the College of Arts and Sciences. I really like the Georgetown area, as it is a space that brings tourists and families so it is not just a college town. Additionally, I like the proximity to the DMV area since I have been able to attend museums and historic sites and expand my knowledge base. I feel like the culture at Georgetown is a bit cliquey and sometimes students will be spoiled and/or out of touch. I would like for there to be increased efforts to strengthen the sense of community at Georgetown, even though I have found a great community here.

I like that there is so much you could do if you so chose. There are many clubs, teams, sports, random talks to attend that if you really want to you should have no free time. One thing I think could change, at least for the STEM/PSYC majors is for there to be more support regarding making post-grad plans independent of the pre-med route. There are so many things you could be doing that aren't medicine but I feel like we don't get to hear about them much. At least not when compared to the entire team of pre-med advisors you receive access to once you add pre-med to the transcript (this is definitely a bonus if you're interested in medicine though).

Georgetown University is a really positive place to study. The campus is beautiful, with historic buildings, green spaces, and nice views of the Potomac River. It feels peaceful but is still close to everything in Washington, D.C. Classes are challenging but manageable, and professors are supportive and easy to talk to. Many classes connect what you learn to real-world issues, which makes them more interesting and useful.
One of the biggest advantages of Georgetown is its location. Being in D.C. gives students lots of chances for internships, jobs, and events, especially in government, politics, and international work. Students are encouraged to take advantage of these opportunities. The student community is friendly and collaborative, and the school values helping others and thinking about ethics. Overall, Georgetown prepares students well for life after college.

My experience at Georgetown University has been both challenging and rewarding. What I’ve appreciated most is the strong sense of community and the emphasis on learning that connects theory to real-world impact. The professors are deeply knowledgeable and invested in their students, and the classroom discussions are thoughtful, rigorous, and shaped by diverse perspectives. Georgetown’s location in Washington, D.C. is also a major asset, offering unparalleled access to internships, organizations, and experiences that enrich academic learning and make it feel immediately relevant.
I also value Georgetown’s commitment to public service and social justice, which is reflected in both the curriculum and student culture. There’s a shared sense that education should be used to contribute to something larger than oneself, which has shaped how I think about my career and responsibilities beyond the classroom.

I've really enjoyed my time at Georgetown so far. The academics are rigorous but rewarding—professors genuinely care about teaching and are accessible outside of class. The small class sizes in my major have been great for getting to know faculty and having actual discussions rather than just lectures.
What could be better:
- Housing lottery system is stressful and somewhat unfair
- Campus dining options get repetitive quickly
- Some administrative processes are unnecessarily bureaucratic
- Mental health resources are stretched thin—wait times for CAPS can be long

My time at Georgetown has been completely transformative. From the moment I stepped onto the Hilltop, walking into my first classes, meeting professors and classmates who would become lifelong friends, and joining organizations filled with driven, intellectually curious peers, I have felt myself pushed and shaped in ways I could never have anticipated. The uniquely D.C. opportunities, from in-semester internships to conferences and policy events, have further deepened my academic and professional growth. And amid it all, the beauty of campus and the surrounding neighborhoods has been a constant source of grounding and gratitude. I have cherished every moment.
Don't regret coming to Georgetown. It was my dream school, and it has given me all the resources to be successful!

Georgetown is a great school if you are looking to be in DC and involved with government. It does lack some traditional aspects of college life, as everyone is so focused on studying and careers. Great school if you want a good education, though.

Love the school so much its truly been one of the biggest blessings in my life. My only issue is the people at the academic resource center make getting and applying for accommodations a living hell. But if you get aggressive enough it will end up working out!

The food is really bad in the dining halls and they don’t serve lunch on weekends. The communal bathrooms in some of the dorms are pretty dirty. Most of the professors are nice and want to help you succeed.

My experience at Georgetown was phenomenal in all domains--academically, personally, professionally, and socially. In terms of undergraduate institutions, it is difficult to imagine a better place as it provides an incredible community of bright, ambitious, active, and diverse students and faculty in a world-class city with tremendous school pride and collegiality. Truly the best of all worlds.

I am a Georgetown University alum and I had a great experience! I went in the College of Arts & Sciences and I was initially nervous since my ma was not familiar with how colleges operated but we figured it out. It was a step up academically and I could not study the same way that I did in high school. I appreciated the challenges and it pushed me to grow academically. I found a lot of great professors that were able to aid me, both in undergrad and after I graduated. They provide a lot of support and mentorship that I deeply appreciate.
Georgetown has a wide range of social groups that you can explore. You can meet new people every year and still get the benefit of tight-knit community. As a Black student, it was big enough that there are multiple Black communities across ethnicity, class, and interests so you can have more than one group of Black friends. There are ups and downs like anywhere that you go, but I made some of my best friends at Georgetown and I am glad to be a Hoya.

My experience at Georgetown thus far has been nothing short of amazing. The professors are bright, kind, and flexible for students like me (a return-to-college student). I've learned so much, and the resources are vast.
I like that they offer a variety of clubs, but that means you are expected to have ec's to join these clubs. Lots of studying but manageable. Super fun area.

ai_summary: Georgia Institute of Technology offers an academically rigorous environment recognized for its strong emphasis on STEM , particularly engineering and science fields. Many students appreciate the wealth of resources available, including supportive faculty and hands-on learning experiences , which help them navigate the challenging coursework and contribute to professional growth. Despite a vibrant campus life full of diverse activities and opportunities for networking, students frequently note that the focus on academics can overshadow social experiences, leading to a competitive atmosphere . While the campus and location in Atlanta are highlighted as major benefits , reviews often mention dissatisfaction with campus housing and dining options, indicating a need for improvement in these areas. Overall, while Georgia Tech is praised for its educational quality and career preparation, it requires a significant commitment from students to succeed.

reviews.text: decent if all you want to do is grind and get FANG jobs. kdefinitely can have good social life if you put urself out

Georgia Tech has really opened my eyes to what success takes its not given to people who don’t earn it. You have to want to change to be disciplined to actually fight for your diploma. Every scholarship every class everyday is a chance to prove why you belong and I intend too.

It's a really good school, academically speaking and you will be given a lot of opportunities but campus life is extremely dead, and even if you join a club it won't get much more active. As a computer science major the College of Computing has provided me lots of opportunities that I know I wouldn't get if I was any other major, their computer science career fairs are top notch and have the best companies and merch. Computer science majors are given exclusive access to the platform handshake which is like the better LinkedIn. The food is bad most of the time, and it very rarely follows what the online menu says they will have. The non-dining hall food options are vast but after like 8pm you're on your own especially if you are on west campus. I live on west campus and I like it a lot. Although I have never lived on east campus, I would recommend against it because although it is very fun to visit, there is always a lot going on, better a tourist than a citizen.
Georgia Tech can be tough on you sometimes. Everyone else is just as smart as you, if not smarter. If you breezed through high school it will definitely be a shock at first.
Overall, I have really loved attending Georgia Tech. The academics are definitely rigorous but the difficultly is definitely worth it.

Great school. The people around you certainly push you, and it is honestly pretty inspiring. Your peers are all going places and doing cool stuff and there are so many ways to get involved in the school.

I'm a junior now, and here's my honest experience! Firstly, I politely disagree with the comments saying that there is no party scene/social life. If you approach people and are friendly, you will end up with friends and a good social life. Parties aren't always in frats or big raves - house parties that friends host are just as or even more fun because I know everyone!
No matter what kind of person you are - whether you're into sports, arts, music, theater, cooking, skiing... there is always a club for you to join (we have 500+)
I really like that GT students are NOT cutthroat. Despite the academic rigor, everyone is always willing to help - no toxicity. It's never been hard for me to find study buddies in each class.
As an international student, Georgia Tech is pretty welcoming for me and their OIE is pretty helpful in giving us resources.
I highly recommend GT for the community, cost, academics, and tbe fact that it's in a really, really safe bubble in Midtown ATL!
What an exciting time and experience! Academics is challenging, but worth it. I’ve met many great people.

My experience at the Georgia Institute of Technology has been challenging, rewarding, and full of growth. Georgia Tech pushes you academically, and the rigor truly prepares you for real-world problem-solving. The campus environment is innovative, diverse, and full of opportunities for research, networking, and hands-on projects. I especially appreciated the strong sense of community within majors and student organizations. However, the workload can feel overwhelming at times, and more mental-health support and academic balance would greatly benefit students. Improving communication and accessibility of resources would also help. Overall, Georgia Tech is demanding but offers incredible preparation and opportunities for motivated students.

Georgia Tech is an amazing school for academics. However, that’s about it. The party scene is nonexistent, and student life is pretty terrible. Classes are so hard, you spend your entire life studying just to keep up.

Georgia Tech is a great school for anyone overall. It is academically challenging and rigorous enough to push all students in the right direction while also providing every student with the support they need. It is a very diverse campus that also prioritizes having fun, so I've had my fair share of entertainment in just the few months of me being here. I will say that a lot of support is provided for engineering and computing competencies while not a lot is provided for other disciplines. Students in the college of sciences, for example, might have to go longer lengths to find resources.

Georgia Tech is a great place to get a quality education, find future opportunities, and build community. I love the variety of events you can attend and the people you can meet - definitely a work hard, play hard environment. I would like to see more attention put towards the quality of housing.
Overall good. Most of the housing needs updating. Campus food can be better. Academics is very good.
I really like the campus and the overall environment of Tech. The biggest complaint that I have is the frequency of the bus system, especially in the evenings.

Georgia Tech has been an incredible experience overall. The academic rigor is intense, but it truly prepares you for the real world by teaching resilience, problem-solving, and time management. The professors are passionate, and there are countless opportunities for research, internships, and networking through career fairs. I’ve also enjoyed being part of a diverse student body where you can meet people from all over the world, learn from their perspectives, and build lasting friendships.
Campus life is vibrant, with many student organizations, cultural events, and leadership opportunities that help you grow outside of the classroom. While it can be challenging, the support systems, resources, and career preparation make it worth it. Georgia Tech is a place where you’ll work hard, but you’ll leave with unmatched skills, connections, and confidence in your future.

ai_summary: Georgia Southern University offers a unique blend of a supportive academic environment and vibrant student life in a picturesque campus setting. Students appreciate the small class sizes , with most classes ranging from 20-30 students, which fosters a close-knit learning atmosphere and allows for personalized connections with professors. Reviews highlight a generally helpful and caring faculty, though experiences with teaching quality can vary. The campus is noted for its beautiful surroundings and an active community spirit, particularly during football season, where local residents and students alike come together in support of their teams. While many enjoy the engaging student organizations and social events, there are some concerns about campus resources, dining variety, and parking. Overall, students express a strong sense of belonging and pride in their choice of Georgia Southern as their college home.

reviews.text: I like to read to other students in front of the class, helping them with problems that they can't solve

From the moment I started looking for where I wanted to start to study I knew that GSU was one of my top choices. I was really really scared though because I am a Mature Freshman I am 28 years old starting school and it’s scary. But after all the work I got accepted😌

Overall, my experience has been very friutful. I am an art student on the Armstrong campus but I commute to Statesboro as well. I prefer the Armstrong campus as I feel the way if life there is more calm and relaxed. My personal experience teachers and students work a lot together to help eachother which is nice. Statesboro is a very nice campus and has more resources then Armstrong does which is kind of unfortunate given the hour commute which is not easy for everyone both transportation wise and gas wise. Parking in Statesboro is also very bad as I payed $140 for a parking slot and half the students there have no room to work so we get ticketed the spots we can find because there unfortunately not in our zone. Armstrong definitely deserves more resources due the work those teachers and students do there. Ecspecially in the art department. Other than that my experience is great!

Overall Georgia Southern University is a good College. It meets the basic needs of getting the degree and furthering your education if planned. The campus does a good job advertising different things on campus to get more involved in campus life, especially as freshman. The party talk is quite normal here, and something people do consistently on the weekends. The parking is definitely a struggle, you can buy a pass for the year, but more than likely the lot you bought the pass for will be full, and the campus gives tickets out like crazy. The bus is probably the best route, you'll end up having to walk across campus because you won't be able to find a spot near your class with the parking pass you paid for. They have a wide variety of online and in person classes, try to do registration as early as possible or all the classes you need will get taken up. Make sure you look at professor reviews, because some in person professors have a hard time speaking english, so you can't understand.
Moving to statesboro for Georgia southern has been a very good experience. I have made many friends since moving here. I would highly recommend.

I am a Dual Enrollment student at GA Southern and I have to say that the professors are amazing, the course work isn't the worst, and student balance is excellent. I had a good transition into this college and my professors were amazing! I also took an online course which was perfect to accommodate my schedule as an athlete. I was glad that the online course was so flexible and easy to manage. One complaint I have about this college is the fact that when you are in need of help where your advisor is needed, they might never answer. I had multiple friends who are also dual enrolled and had a similar experience as me where the advisor would take over a MONTH to answer her emails. This applied to even serious situations where the advisor is needed ASAP. So, I would definitely want to see a change in this aspect to have the students answered immediately or simple have more advisors to have them available at all times.

Everyone was an alcoholic, druggie or didn’t have long-term goals. It’s been a little difficult to find people who weren’t just trying to sleep with me and most of these people were fake af. The food and housing were really good, and most of my classes were 10/10, but it’s been hard because most of these people didn’t value themselves.
Great experience. Loved everything about the school overall. Would recommend to anyone who asked my opinion!

During my time so far at Georgia Southern University, I can confidently say these have been the most exciting, fun, and busy past few months of my life. Joining a sorority fall of my Freshman year was probably the best I have ever made. I have created so many connections and lifelong friendships that I will never forget. Living in the dorms has also increased my experience by getting to know the diverse group of people living next to me. Overall, my experience so far has been nothing but uplifting as an individual who had tended to be socially anxious in general.

Georgia Southern is a great university primarily for its flexibility. As a non-traditional student who came back to education after years away from schooling, I really needed a school that would meet my needs, and Southern provided. I was able to work out a curriculum that worked for me, and they were very accommodating when it came to housing and other needs. I think the biggest area that could be improved is diversity and disability accommodations. A lot of campus is treacherous for people with various disabilities (ie broken elevators, bad paving), and I think that could be improved.

Studying Multimedia and Film Production at Georgia Southern has been incredible. In my very first week, I was invited to join a real media project and help film content for the university — I never expected to get such an opportunity so early.
Campus life is creative, friendly, and full of energy. People are open, supportive, and it already feels like home. The International Student Office is truly amazing — they care, help with everything, and make you feel supported every step of the way.
I’m grateful to be here, to learn, create, and be part of this inspiring community.
Overall it’s been my favorite school I’ve been to. I wish they offered more graduate programs online though!
Georgia southern is a decent school, but it’s in a small town and with small towns there isn’t much to do outside of school.

Honestly, I truly love it here in Statesboro, the school, and the professors. I do plan on waiting until spring 2026 because the fall did not exactly work out for me the way I needed to, but I hope to continue my experience here at Georgia Southern even if it is next year.

My experience at Georgia Southern has been just "alright" so far. I do want to add that I attend the Armstrong campus in Savannah. I do feel as if that campus is more neglected as only around 5,000 students attend that campus. The Statesboro is the more popular campus that over 27,000 students. They have more funding, events, and due to that, their student and campus life is more lively.

ai_summary: Georgia State University seems to provide a vibrant and diverse environment for students, with many reviewers highlighting the unique blend of academic opportunities and city life. The school is noted for its strong academics , particularly in business, and its supportive professors who are generally approachable and engaged with their students. Student life is bustling, with plenty of events and activities available, though some students feel overwhelmed by the social scene or find it hard to balance academics with extracurriculars. While the university's location in downtown Atlanta offers excellent internship and job opportunities, safety concerns regarding the surrounding area, including issues with homelessness, have been raised. The campus' urban layout, which some find less traditional due to its scattered buildings, contributes to a dynamic experience but can also lead to logistical challenges. Financial aid services are reported to vary in effectiveness, and while many students appreciate the resources available, improvements are desired in areas like campus security and administrative responsiveness. Overall, Georgia State University provides a culturally rich and opportunity-filled environment , but students should be prepared for the challenges that come with its urban setting.

reviews.text: GSU is a great school for non-traditional students. It may be difficult for students not accustomed to an urban setting to acclimate due to presence of crime and homelessness on campus and lack of parking infrastructure. It is a research university which means you may often end up taking classes with grad student professors, which can be hit or miss. Good selection of online and hybrid course offerings. Nice campus facilities, and conveniently located in the heart of downtown Atlanta, near transit, tourist attractions, restaurants, etc. I would not recommend GSU for students looking for a traditional college experience. Do not live on campus - off-campus will be cheaper and nicer.
I was a online student then did on person and had to drop my classes because I couldn't afford but also my professors did not speak good English.

I transferred to Georgia State as a non-traditional student in 2017 and lived on campus for a semester. I had an incredible experience. Professors were very supportive and passionate. The campus life was fun and engaging. I really enjoyed being in the heart of downtown Atlanta and getting to experience the city while attending college.

I enjoyed it. I did a lot of online classes and many in person, and they are both quite easy and forgiving. I have taken ten classes, and only had one bad teacher. Every other teacher was very kind and understanding.

My overall experience at Georgia State University has been both challenging and rewarding. GSU has provided me with a strong academic foundation, particularly in finance, while exposing me to diverse perspectives and real-world applications. Through coursework and collaborative projects, I’ve developed critical thinking, analytical, and problem-solving skills that translate directly to professional environments. The university’s emphasis on experiential learning and career readiness has helped me grow both academically and personally, preparing me to confidently pursue opportunities and continue developing my skills in a fast-paced, professional setting.
I would love to see all the students come together around campus and enjoy talking to one another in their presence

My experience as a freshman at Georgia State University has been a mix of challenges and exciting opportunities. Starting college was a big adjustment, but I've learned so much about managing my time, staying motivated, and adapting to new systems.
I really appreciate the diversity of the campus and the abundance of resources available to students, such as advisors and tutoring services, which have helped me stay on track.
If I could suggest one change, it would be clearer guidance for freshmen on course planning and degree requirements, to make the transition to college less overwhelming.
Overall, I'm grateful to be at Georgia State University, and I feel that even in my first year, I'm growing as a student and preparing myself for future success.

My overall experience with Georgia State University has been both challenging and rewarding. The university has provided a supportive academic environment that encourages growth, independence, and critical thinking. Through my coursework, I have strengthened my technical skills, improved my problem-solving abilities, and gained a deeper understanding of my field of study. Georgia State’s diverse community and access to academic resources have helped me grow not only as a student but also as an individual preparing for future professional success.

The school is very strong in resources for Pre Med Students. I also love how diverse it is. I am Nigerian and I always struggled to fit in, but because of how diverse the school is, it was a lot easier to find friends.

It is extremely diverse, and there are plenty of resources and opportunities at GSU. There is a variety of foods in the dining halls. I will admit there is too many unhealthy foods available for young developing students.

My experience at Georgia State has been rewarding so far. I have been been challenged academically, but I have been able to do better at GSU that I did in high school. The professors with classes that were really challenging for me provided office hours so I was able to talk to them and discuss topics that I needed clarity and assistance on. I am very gratefully for that. I would like to see the amount of housing options improved. Being that this is a state school in the middle of a major city, housing options are limited. However, I need where housing can still be extended, because new construction is always being done, so there is room for improvement. I also think that dilapidated homes and buildings can be converted to student housing. It may cost, but the investment would be an improvement to student accommodations and provide a productive aspect to neighborhoods.

I can say I positively view Georgia State University as a great school. The university atomsphere is very inviting to people and students in general. The staff and professors I have met on campus are kind and helpful people who truly put the students mental and academics first. If I was to have one complaint would be slight inconvenient scheduling the counselors do sometimes but overall the experience is very enjoyable and I wish to continue attending GSU.

Overall, Georgia State University is a welcoming and inclusive institution that offers a wide range of academic, professional, and leadership opportunities. While it does not provide a traditional, enclosed campus experience, its location in downtown Atlanta exposes students to the realities of an urban environment, including interactions with the city’s homeless population. These experiences contribute to a broader understanding of social issues and personal responsibility while preparing students for life in a diverse, real-world setting.
I like the housing and dining hall of GSU because these are always clean, useful and safety. I love all my professors who taught me in the first semester of GSU.

My overall experience at Georgia State University has been significant. I slowly found my place, especially once I got involved with GSU Athletics and started connecting with people who were on similar paths. What I really liked about GSU is the amount of opportunity you get just from being in Atlanta. The location makes things feel real, internships, volunteer events, networking, game-day operations. The campus diversity also stood out to me. Being around so many different kinds of people helped me grow and pushed me out of my comfort zone in a good way. If there’s something I’d like to see change, it's better communication. Sometimes it felt like you really had to search for information on advising, internships, or whom to talk to about specific opportunities. Overall, GSU gave me chances I probably wouldn’t have gotten anywhere else, and it helped shape the direction I want to go in for my career. Even with the challenges, I’m grateful for the experience and the growth it brought me

ai_summary: Gonzaga University is often praised for its strong sense of community and welcoming atmosphere , which helps students easily connect with peers and professors. The small class sizes foster meaningful relationships and allow for personalized attention from instructors, who are described as kind, accessible, and invested in student success. Student life at Gonzaga is vibrant, with plenty of opportunities for involvement in clubs, sports, and social activities, though some students feel that the social scene can be cliquish or not very diverse. Although the campus is beautiful and offers a variety of academic programs, some reviews highlighted concerns about the dining services and the need for more options for students with dietary restrictions. Additionally, there is recognition of the high tuition costs , although many students find the financial aid options helpful. Overall, students appreciate the balance between academics and social life, with an emphasis on school spirit, particularly during basketball events.

reviews.text: Its amazing, i love the campus, its super pretty. I also love the small school feel, you can really get a great academic experience. I don't love the dining hall food very much however.
I am only 5 weeks into my education here at Gonzaga and so far so good, I'm looking forward to building strong friendships as the years go by.

Gonzaga University is known as a pretty solid school, especially if you're into law, business, or engineering. It's got this vibe of being super friendly and supportive, which comes from its Jesuit background – they're all about helping others, being fair, and really digging into learning. A lot of students like that the classes are small, so you actually get to know your professors. The campus itself is really nice, and Spokane is a cool city that's not too big but still has stuff to do, plus you're close to the outdoors.

Unbelievable professors, passionate and invested student life, and a cheap city where you can live in a big, craftsman house with your best friends. Truly an incredible experience, one of the best decisions I’ve ever made.

I attended Gonzaga’s nursing school during the unprecedented start of the pandemic, and all staff and professors did a good job of making necessary adjustments to finish out the remaining school year. I loved almost all of my nursing professors!

My overall experience with Gonzaga University has been very positive. The professors are supportive, the community is welcoming, and the campus offers a great balance of academics and student life. Gonzaga provides both strong education and personal growth opportunities.
I love the atmosphere and how nice the people are. They are so willing to help you. Its truly a wonderful college that I would reccommed to anybody.
I live in Spokane, so going to Gonzaga was always the dream. I love the area and some of the friends I've made there as well.
Fun college town with great professors and a beautiful medium size campus with lots extracurricular activities.
I am an incoming Freshman. I have felt welcomed and supported throughout becoming a student here. My mom also attended this school. We love Gonzaga and love the Zag community.

I was nervous to commit to Gonzaga my senior year of high school. The summer before freshman year, I decided to attend GOOB to make the transition easier. I should have known right then that GU was going to be amazing. The experience was insanely valuable, and as I moved into my dorm, Marian Hall, I felt nothing but excitement. As I started to feel okay about the school I chose, someone asked me to try out for the rowing team with them. Again, I felt nervous and uncertain. But like everything else at GU, it turned into one of the greatest experience of my life. I made the team, unlocking a whole other level of GU. The athletics department is professional, robust, and friendly. I made so many friends, and learned so much about hard work. Everyone here cares -- from athletics advisors, coaches, professors, students, employees, and the faith community. The community here is completely unmatched. Even though I have three more years, I can tell it will be so hard to leave this place.

I have loved being a student at Gonzaga University! It has allowed me to flourish both socially and academically. The smaller class sizes give me the opportunity to get to know and be supported well by my professors. I love how friendly people are here, and the Zag spirit cannot be matched; game days are electric! There are so many ways to pursue your interests both through courses available and through clubs.
I have loved my time at Gonzaga! My professors have been amazing and very supportive. There are lots of opportunities for work study and support with career development and internships.
Gonzaga has changed my life. I am extremely thankful for every opportunity Gonzaga has offered me. I like to say "This is a small school with big school opportunities."

I really love it here. I am going into my senior year and I'm already crushed that I have to leave so soon. My fourth year couldn't have come sooner and I'm so excited to graduate but so sad to leave.

ai_summary: At Grand Canyon University, students appreciate the strong support from professors and advisors, noting their approachability and willingness to help. Many describe the learning environment as supportive and engaging, with smaller class sizes that foster interaction, which students find beneficial for their education. The online learning format is seen as a great option for balancing education with work, although some find the policy of 7-week classes without breaks to be exhausting. A unique aspect of GCU is its Christian foundation, which creates a welcoming atmosphere for students of all backgrounds, yet does not impose religious beliefs. Many students also enjoy the vibrant campus life , with various activities and community events available. On the other hand, some reviews expressed concerns about the cost of tuition and the variable quality of instructors across different courses. Overall, students feel that GCU provides a well-rounded educational experience that prepares them for their future careers.

reviews.text: My overall experience with Grand Canyon University has been one crazy ride but it was a great time there. I really enjoyed getting to meet new people who were in the same program as me and has helped meet the person who I now call my bestfriend. My professors were really helpful with my time, even though I had to take their classes multiple times. Dr. Stephanie Stang and Dr. Rebecca Socia were one of the best professors I have ever had at GCU. They made me feel important and seen and have been the reason I have yet to give up on my dream. GCU has helped me out too with my pre-requisites by offering me a scholarship to help support me, but in clinicals, I have to search for additional financial resources to be financially cleared to start the Summer 2026 semester. GCU is a blast to be at and totally worth checking out.

During my time at Grand Canyon University currently, it has been a great experience. I am still in school for about 6 more months; however, at 40 years old I am glad I came back to school to complete my Bachelor degree in business management. Being an online student it is a bit more feasible for me being a single mom and working full-time. There have been courses that have been harder than others of course, but I have learned and acquired so much towards my future. I have also picked up some good quality friends during this time at Grand Canyon University. Overall, there is nothing I would change and I recommend this institute to others all the time.

From the start Grand Canyon and their staff have been fully connected with me and reach out to me often to check in. It has been such a welcoming and comforting experience to be able to build my education and future with the school. Their online program makes my education and outside life more manageable in order to succeed in my studies and life in all.
I wouldn't change a thing. They helped me enroll in the Master's program I wanted, and that would lead to my teaching certificate
Since I have began my journey with GCU I have had terrific assistance through each step in my journey.

I quite I'm enjoying going to school at Grand Canyon University. I like the fact that I have student service counselor who is always checking up on me and making sure I'm keeping my grades up. On top of that going to a Christian college is perfect for me to rekindle my relationship with God and have a better and strong bond with him.

My overall experience with Grand Canyon University has been meaningful and growth-focused. As a graduate student, I have appreciated the structure of the courses and the way each class builds on the next. The curriculum challenged me to think critically, analyze real-world situations, and apply strategy in a practical way. I especially valued the discussion forums and group projects because they allowed me to connect with classmates from different backgrounds and learn from their perspectives. Even in an online environment, I felt supported by faculty who provided clear expectations and thoughtful feedback.
If I could suggest a change, I would love to see even more interactive opportunities, such as optional live sessions or networking events for graduate students. Overall, my experience at GCU has strengthened my confidence, discipline, and commitment to lifelong learning.
I am just getting started and the experience seems very easy. The staff are eager to help me. I do not know anything that needs to change.

I just started but so far my experience has been great. The staff is very helpful the admissions administrator was great and they have wonderful online resources for whatever you mite need . They give you excellent technical support.
I am currently enrolled in grand canyon University and expected to start February 23rd. My experience so far has been great. The councilors have me very helpful and supportive.
GCU is a great Christian college with lots of activities and great college campus environment. They have lots of activities and clubs on campus for students to participate in.
I’ve toured the campus many times, as well as attended many events hosted by the campus. I’ve never felt bad about any aspect of the establishment, and I’d recommend it to anyone and everyone.
It was a good experience. Professors were the best for higher level courses since they were more passionate about what they taught.

Since starting I have been treated with respect and guided in the directions needed to help me in whatever way may come up at that time. I love how Grand Canyon University is so closely with the Christian faith from being a Christian based school that bible lessons are taught amongst my homework assignments as well. It truly is a amazing school.

I feel very prepared to become an addiction counselor. I will finish my last practicum on April 1, but it is a struggle to get funds to pay for the practicum because my financial aid has run out. Without the balance fully paid, I will not receive my degree. I must wait until it is paid and then I can start addiction counseling at an inpatient treatment facility and take the licensing exam. I have been a Special Education teacher for 32 years, but I am a recovering alcoholic and feel I need to continue to use my talents and knowledge that Grand Canyon University provided.

ai_summary: Grand Valley State University (GVSU) offers a vibrant college experience characterized by a strong emphasis on academics and a supportive community. Many students appreciate the range of classes and the personal attention from professors , noting that class sizes allow for meaningful interactions. Student life is active, with numerous clubs, events, and social opportunities, particularly highlighted during athletic events and tailgates. While some students find the food options lacking and note challenges with housing, the campus's welcoming atmosphere and beautiful setting contribute positively to their experience. Students also mention the university's commitment to mental health resources and inclusivity, although some feel there could be more diversity on campus. Overall, GVSU is viewed as a well-rounded institution that encourages both academic and personal growth.

reviews.text: I like the variety of courses to choose from and the advisors are very helpful and care about students' success. There are plenty of opportunities to participate in events around campus. All the events are carefully planned and they help you meet new people and learn more about campus life. If in need of a job, there are many job options on campus that are very flexible and understanding of your schedule. I have a job on campus and I have learned many new things from it and have met and became friends with many great people. There are stores on campus with different food and necessity items. Some of the dining areas available are good, however in my opinion, the best places to get food always close early. Therefore you are left with few options of where to eat and to me the food and these places are not the best. Some of the professors lack enthusiasm and care for teaching, but you can easily search professor ratings online to decide if you want to be in their class.

I came to Grand Valley with my sister not knowing what to expect being a freshman. I’ve never had a dream college and I only live a few hours from campus. My sister convinced me to go since I hadn’t had any strong opinions on where I went to college. But I am so extremely grateful I choose GV. Since completing my first semester, and half of my second, I can confidently say I have found a sense of belonging. I have made so many new friends at the gym, Starbucks, my class, and especially sports events. I even met my boyfriend who I am so thankful for. At first I wasn’t sure if the college life was for me, but the sleepless night and 10 page essays are beyond worth if that means I get all the blessings that are included with it. I have found true passion in my major and stepped majorly out of my comfort zone due to certain classes and teachers and I’m so glad I chose to step into this lifestyle. Even after getting a second job and enrolling full time in classes I wouldn’t change a thing!
As a first-year student, Grand Valley is a great school. Small class sizes, beautiful campus, great community, and amazing opportunities.

It truly is a nice campus; it's not too big but it's not too small. The professors are very kind and understanding and genuinely want to see their students do well. Parking is a bit of a nightmare. The all you can eat dining halls are questionable.

With my time at GVSU, I have made memorable memories, met great people, and it allowed me to step out of my comfort zone tremendously. I like having the ability to have a wide range of opportunity and feel included when it comes to being involved. I like how there are so many areas where you can figure out what you want to do, not just career wise, but hobby wise as well. This allows people to be comfortable with the change of environment and with the people around them. I would like to see better food options, in my opinion. There was a lot of talk when one of the areas closed and was getting remodeled in an on campus dining hall. From what I have heard, the campus food isn't the best and want more popular food options, such as Raising Canes.

Overall, my experience at Grand Valley State University has been good. I like the campus and most of my classes, and the professor are very helpful. One thing I would like to see more improved is the food on campus both in quality and options. I also think there should be more safety rules and better enforcement to make students feel safer. However, I do think with these changes the overall student experience would be so much better!
The campus itself is beautiful and most of the classes are fine but it’s definitely pricey especially with a lot of extra charges for tuition payments.

I really enjoy Grand Valley! Be aware when making your decision that the amount of students attending the university is really growing! There have been a couple violent/chaotic issues recently that have spiked my attention but overall campus life and living are great!

Grand Valley State University was the only college I applied for during my senior year of high school. I had toured so many other college campuses, but none compared to Grand Valley. The scenery is gorgeous, it’s completely surrounded by woods, it’s right by downtown Grand Rapids, and it’s also near Lake Michigan. Aside from the location perks, the classes were very fun and engaging. I could tell that my professors really cared about teaching and helping their students succeed. Highly recommend GVSU!

The times I have visited the campus, I was welcomed by wonderful people. My friends who live on the campus say that it's safe and quiet. I like almost everything about Grand Valley, although, I do not like that it is a tip phase two program and it set me back one semester. I didn't know until later on and it might've been a mistake on my part. I just wish I was told that it was a tip phase two to help me out financially.

My overall experience at Grand Valley State University has been incredibly rewarding both academically and personally. GVSU provides a welcoming and supportive environment that encourages growth, curiosity, and community. As a student, I’ve had the opportunity to learn from passionate professors who genuinely care about their students’ success and who make class engaging and meaningful. The campus itself is beautiful and well-maintained, offering plenty of resources from study spaces to student organizations that make it easy to get involved and feel connected. Beyond academics, I’ve appreciated the emphasis GVSU places on inclusion, mental health, and real-world preparation. I’ve gained valuable experiences that have shaped my confidence, leadership, and professional goals. Overall, my time at GVSU has been filled with learning, friendships, and opportunities that have prepared me well for the future.

My overall experience at Grand Valley State University has been really positive. The professors care about their students and do a great job preparing us for real-world careers, especially in business and management. I’ve learned a lot both in and outside the classroom, and I genuinely like the campus—it’s clean, well-maintained, and has a welcoming atmosphere.
That said, I do wish there was a bit more campus life and fun activities to get students more involved. Sometimes it feels like there isn’t much going on socially, especially on weekends. Adding more events, gatherings, or late-night activities could really make GVSU feel more alive and connected. Overall, though, I’m proud to be a Laker and appreciate the quality education and supportive environment the school offers.

Food is good, but expensive. All food places are fast food or restaurants. Campus does not provide meals. GVSU does not except cash only digital pay, which I did not was legal. Campus is safe and people are nice

GVSU is a great university for specific types of people. There is a wide variety of professors and a lot of them are great!! The scenery around GVSU is beautiful. It's located in the middle on nowhere (kind of), and right by the ravines; it's very beautiful and pretty in the fall to see the leaves changing color. Very social community, you just have to put yourself out there right away. Greek life here is decently sized, most are great, some have inside issues. There are a lot of different clubs to join. No matter what you're into, there are people and places for you! We have lots of events around campus, especially during the first few weeks. Campus life night, laker kickoff, etc. The campus is very walkable, the longest walk i would say is maybe 20 minutes, but you would have to be walking really far. They have a very high acceptance rate, which this year has lead to safety concerns. The dorms don't have great security either, which is difficult.

I went to Grand valley for my freshman and sophomore year. I enjoyed the campus a lot and all the food options that I had. I also loved how the campus is surrounded by nature. Had it not been for covid, I definitely would've stayed and finished college.

ai_summary: Gwynedd Mercy University is often praised for its strong academic environment , particularly in nursing and healthcare programs, where many students feel adequately prepared for their future careers. The small class sizes allow for personalized interactions with professors, contributing to a supportive atmosphere used by both faculty and staff to foster student success. While the campus offers a tight-knit community that helps students form lasting friendships , some students express that campus life is less vibrant, especially on weekends when many commuters leave. Food options are frequently criticized, with calls for more variety and improved quality . Overall, students value the close relationships they build and the academic commitment of their professors, although they wish for more engaging on-campus events and better parking resources.

reviews.text: Gwynedd Mercy is a pretty good campus, but only worth it if you're going for a healthcare major. 90% of the students live in the immediate area and come from a preppy Catholic School background. If those fit your description, you're in the right place. The people are pretty friendly, and if you know where to look you can make good friends. The food is not nearly as bad as everyone says. In fact, its quite good most days. The dining hall staff are a mixed bag though and so are most of the professors.

My overall experience at Gwynedd Mercy University has been positive. I liked the courses a lot and most of my professors. I really enjoyed my major business courses as well. One thing I would like to see changed is a dedicated area for commuters to relax, study, or even nap. Think of it as more of an elaborate break room for commuter students.
Excellent nursing program, enjoying instructors and fellow students. Clinical sites and instructors were very willing to teach and offer new opportunities.

Going into my sophomore year here at Gwynedd I’ve learned more about people and myself. The campus is pretty diverse when it comes to the students, and I love learning their backgrounds and where they’re from. It’s a nice school to me personally because it’s quiet and far enough from the city where I don’t hear the noise of cars and people. I like all the nature that the campus is surrounded by, and the deer you see from time to time. Overall it’s a nice and quiet place where you feel included and supported by the people and staff.

I have only been at gwynedd for a short time but love everything about it. It is a smaller school but a lot of activities and clubs you can get involved in. The class are smaller and with more personal instruction. Everyone on the campus is friendly and helpful.

From the second I applied At GMU, the out pour of help and contact I had was over whelming. I have always been a shy person and staring this journey was a rough decision for me. Gwynedd Mercy made me break out of my shell and take the college experience full force. I was so proud of myself for taking a chance. The campus was beautiful, people, staff and professors were engaging and interested in each student the spoke to. The made us all feel comfortable with a huge change about to happen, I would not want to change a thing.

Gwynedd is an amazing college and I am so glad that I chose to spend the next 4 years of my life here. There are multiple food stations around campus including the main cafereria area where you can request any kind of food you want if you do not want anything from the buffet or salad bar. The professors are always available to help you as well as your academic advisors to help you stay on track.
It’s a great school for those who like a small tight community and the classes are small if you need extra attention from professors

Gwynedd Mercy University offers a welcoming and inclusive environment that fosters both personal and academic growth. The university emphasizes hands-on learning opportunities, such as internships, projects, and capstone experiences. As a digital communications major, I worked on real-world campaigns, produced video projects, and explored creative fields that prepared me for my career. The focus on critical concerns, such as non-violence and social justice, added a meaningful dimension to my education.
I appreciated the supportive faculty and staff, small class sizes, and experiential learning opportunities. The university’s values-driven mission and beautiful campus also stood out. These elements combine to create a nurturing and inspiring environment for students.
Overall, Gwynedd Mercy University provided me with the tools, experiences, and confidence to pursue my goals. I recommend it to anyone seeking a supportive academic environment encouraging personal and professional growth.

The university is very good academically. They have smaller classrooms meaning that you can get to know the professor and feel more comfortable asking questions about the course. There are also tutoring services and many ways to succeed in your courses. There aren't many campus activities, and it is hard to connect with other peers of different majors at Gwynedd Mercy University.
