I really like the school and how it makes me feel safe and there’s tons of resources academically and health wise as well. If I would like to see anything change I would say more shoutouts or events for students living off campus to join on campus students.

I was on the women's soccer team at St. Ambrose and I had a great experience and enjoyed playing soccer. I have also had great professors in the education program that have helped me become a teacher some day!

ai_summary: At St. Catherine University, students often appreciate the supportive and engaging community fostered by small class sizes and dedicated professors. The campus offers a safe, welcoming atmosphere, with a mission focused on empowering women and promoting social justice. However, some students express concerns about communication from staff, limited extracurricular activities , particularly during weekends, and the quality of food options. While many feel encouraged and well-supported academically, there are criticisms regarding the administration's handling of financial aid and diversity issues, particularly concerning the experiences of BIPOC students. Overall, St. Kate's is portrayed as a good fit for those pursuing a close-knit educational experience , especially in the health sciences, but it may fall short for those seeking a vibrant social life.

reviews.text:
St. Catherines University keeps students' life in mind with very comfortable dorms, safety, and higher learning.
I started the beginning of January 2026, and it has been a smooth transition moving into my dorm and starting my full load of classes.

Honestly, dont come unless you are used to being separated by "cliques" and ethnic groups. I'm from the Houston area, and I hated being here. I joined clubs, senate, and go to a lot of events but unless you already have friends, it's practically impossible. The school rlly pushes their left-wing political views, while saying we have a "voice". Not a big Catholic scene, compared to LGBTQ+. Im pretty sure they have more staff and leadership positions than they do professors. It seems so good on paper, but actually being here feels like im a couple decades early. Heavy commuter school as well. Unless you are doing pre-med, nursing, humanities, or business admin., youll most likely have to do ACTC or directed study. Idk why they offer these majors, if you cant even take the classes on campus. Most people that did talk to, said they came here for the scholarships so dont be fooled.

I really love the campus, it is very beautiful and relaxing. There are lots of study spaces in all the buildings. There isn't much for parties, but everybody usually just goes to the clubs in Minneapolis. Because there aren't parties, the campus is really quiet which is great for sleeping and studying. Most of the professors are great and just want to genuinely see you grow. We have great fashion Design, ASL, and Nursing programs. There are many leadership opportunities such as Senate. The chapel cares about several social justice issues and open about sharing religions that aren't catholic. We have issues with public safety since they don't do their job half the time. If something happens on campus you have better luck calling the police than public safety.

It has been great! I am a transfer student and my experience here has been so impactful. Professors really care about you and want you to succeed. The university goes above and beyond to make sure that their students are seen, heard, loved, and cared for.

I was hoping to feel a strong sense of belonging here at St. Kate's, but throughout my 2.5 Years here, I didn't. It has been so hard to make friends because every friend group already feels established. All of the staff and the professors there are kind, and I've had great professors in my first year; however, their teaching just wasn't the greatest for me. Everything was so hard. It's honestly such a depressing university. I wouldn't recommend this university to my friends and family. If this university is your only option, try to reconsider. If you can't, I hope you'll enjoy it as much as I wished I did.
I am currently enrolled in my first semester working towards an OTA degree. The program is 80% online and 20% in person labs/internship. i am enjoying my experience and doing well in my classes.

Overall, I love the campus, and it is a really great school. There are a variety of courses to choose from, and class sizes are relatively small, so it really gives you a chance to bond with your classmates and professors.

St. Catherine University has a beautiful campus and a strong focus on women’s leadership, social justice, and the health sciences. The small class sizes and supportive professors make it a welcoming place to learn. It can be a bit expensive, but the community and mission-driven education are valuable.

St. Catherine University was personally not the place for me. They always talk about inclusivity and creating a sense of belonging but never once did I feel like I belonged here.
On my first day, I was called a "whitie" by another girl.
In my "The Reflective Women" class that they make every single first year take, my professor made me feel like I had to apologize for all the racism and prejudice that is in the world.
If you haven even the slightest conservative opinion, I wouldn't suggest coming here. They tell us to voice our opinions but it feels like we can't unless it's in agreement with their views. Not to mention it's expensive and that some of the admission counselors will tell other immigrant students that if they can't pay then they shouldn't be here.

St. Catherine University is an excellent school for health education. The courses are rigorous and highly informative, offering students a strong foundation in their field. One of the most valuable aspects of the university is its small class sizes, which allow students to receive personalized attention. The professors are caring and committed to student success. If you’re struggling in a class, they are more than willing to help you until you fully understand the material. Each student is truly seen and supported in the classroom environment.
I haven't started school yet, but based on what I've seen during registration, the campus life seems nice.

My experience in St. Kates MSNEL has been disappointing and falls short of the advertised commitments. Seeking students should be aware of systemic issues such as chronic disorganization, understaffing, and reliance on overburdened faculty, leading to inconsistent curriculum delivery. Course materials often deviate from standardized nursing education frameworks, raising alignment concerns with industry benchmarks. The program fails to meet criteria set by ACEN with inadequate simulation-based training limiting essential hands-on skill development. Clinical rotations often occur in non-traditional settings like summer camps or correctional facilities, lacking comprehensive exposure to acute care environments. Outdated facilities and insufficient access to modern equipment further hinder preparedness. Grading inconsistencies, especially in final semesters, suggest a lack of transparency. Prospective students should research alternative programs with proven reputations to ensure success.

My experience at St. Kate's has been exactly what I was hoping for in my college education. I do wish there were improvements within the food options, as it's very repetitive, and also that the dorms were more updated. Otherwise, the enclosed campus is a very safe feeling with many options for food, shopping, and other places to walk to.

Overall, my experience at St. Catherine has been pretty good. I have had many good experiences through clubs and activities on campus through Resident Life. The staff and professors are very helpful and would recommend it as a good school to consider applying to.

St. Catherine University has brilliant professors. Small, women-only classes provide for an excellent opportunity to deepen learning and engagement. The campus is quiet, but in a great local area and is close to other university campuses.

ai_summary: St. John Fisher University receives strong praise for its close-knit community and welcoming atmosphere , making students feel like part of a "Fisher Family." The campus is often described as beautiful, with modern housing and excellent dining facilities. Academically, small class sizes allow for personalized attention from professors, particularly in well-regarded programs like nursing and education. However, some reviewers noted a desire for greater diversity among the student body, as well as a more vibrant social life beyond academics. While many appreciate the support services available, a few voiced concerns about the limitations of advisement and some student services. Overall, Fisher is characterized as a solid choice for those seeking a supportive educational environment , particularly in specific programs, while noting areas for improvement.

reviews.text: I have had a good experience so far. I transferred in as a junior and everyone was very welcoming. The school is very small, so there are very limited classes, which is my only negative. The food is also very good!

The academics are poor. Everyone is a nursing major---hence, the nursing program is "good". Everyother major, however, is severely lacking. Classes are still quiet large despite the schools size, and the opportunities are slim. They talk about their food before they discuss their academics, and the food is mediocre. They have a main line with tasty options, but very limited pickings outside of that. It's not healthy food, either--It still makes you sick from time to time

I'm going there in Fall 2026! I've toured the campus, met the coaches, and the admissions staff plus tried the food and stayed a night in the dorms. I can't wait to go here, all I heard it rave reviews about the place!

Although I had some difficulties regarding individuals that impacted my academic success along the way. The school does still have good individuals with good hearts. Now, it might get difficult at times, & one way or another you'll find yourself in the loop. However, it gets better, especially when you create an environment you want to be in. Fisher is a great campus; I do feel as if there could be a lot more improvements regarding the campus, but I feel like it's the people that make this campus feel more at home. It doesn't hurt to say hi, especially if the person is a stranger. Small campus, but everyone know everybody & everyone deserves the opportunity to get to know one another.

Everything is great about the school except for their career services and support for students. After going to Academic advising and career services for 2 years neither helped me find an internship. Financial services also has no idea what they’re talking about because they said I didn’t qualify for any scholarships but after checking the website one day reminiscing on my time there, it turns out there were several I qualified applying for. Also didn’t announce Cum Laude distinction and no cord for the club I was apart of at my graduation. So basically 4 years of effort not recognized.
Great academics, terrible everything else.
Love everything about this school! I am thriving due to the incredible professors and the overall amazing community
Overall a good experience and a nice school to attend. Good social life and keeps everyone involved constantly.

I absolutely love it here! Perfect for those who prefer small campuses. Everybody knows each other and it’s easy to find people. The professors for nursing are probably the best and most caring people I know! My only issue is it’s mainly caucasians and not a lot of diversity.
I start at Fisher soon and I already love the school. It feels so homey and welcoming. Everyone's so nice and inviting.

Although I haven’t started the semester, I took a tour of the university before applying and was amazed. The professors in my program seem very well educated and personable. The campus is beautiful and easy to navigate. My program specifically, Mental Health Counseling, seems like it will help prepare me tremendously for the career I see myself having in the future. The diversity could definitely be better but not to the point where I feel it would be an issue with furthering my education. Very excited to start and gain more knowledge.

I did enjoy the campus it is not as big as I expected when I arrived. The campus shuttles really helped me get to class on time during my first year. Also, the professors always had your best interest when it came to taking actual courses that will help guide you to your major.

Overall, my experience at St. John Fisher University was positive and rewarding. The university offers a supportive community, small class sizes, and professors who genuinely care about student success. I appreciated the personalized attention I received, which made me feel valued as an individual, not just a number. The campus is beautiful, well-maintained, and provides a welcoming environment that encourages learning and personal growth. Academically, I felt challenged but supported. The faculty are knowledgeable and accessible, and many bring real-world experience into the classroom. That said, there are a few areas where I’d like to see improvements. More diversity—both in the student body and the curriculum—would make the experience even stronger. Additionally, expanding mental health resources and support services would benefit the well-being of all students. Some campus facilities, like residence halls and dining options, could also be modernized.

St. John Fisher has an incredibly engaging and interactive campus life that makes students instantly feel at home. There are diverse clubs for students allowing the to feel connected and a sense of belonging.
I loved that fisher was a campus that offered so much in terms of fun and student life. There was so much for everyone to do and find their niche. I also enjoyed the food and hibachi station.

Overall, I feel that I learned a lot at Fisher. I feel like I learned a lot about myself, as well as how different the world can be only a few hours away from home. Last year, I would've told you to completely avoid this place if you had any interest in taking part in extracurriculars that involve music or performance, don't come to Fisher. However, in the last year, they've made strides towards making the campus feel a bit more equal for arts and athletics students. There's so much I can't write about this place, but it's so much better to experience it. Take a tour, I'm sure you'll find that Fisher could be exactly what you're looking for.

ai_summary: St. John's University - New York receives mixed feedback from its students, particularly regarding its academics, student life, and campus environment. Many reviews highlight the helpfulness and dedication of professors , citing their willingness to support students and provide engaging classes, although experiences can vary significantly by individual professor. The university is praised for its diverse student body and vibrant campus culture , with numerous activities, clubs, and events that cater to various interests. However, some students express concerns about high tuition costs and the adequacy of student services, particularly around financial aid and internship opportunities. The social scene is viewed as limited by some, especially for commuters, and there are calls for improved facilities and resources . Overall, while many appreciate the well-rounded experience and supportive community, others feel there are areas needing improvement, particularly regarding campus engagement and accessibility.

reviews.text: Can’t wait to go, I’ve seen online tour’s, events and life at this college and it’s all be astonishing to with great everything seems

I am a sophomore Nursing major at St. John’s University, and I am truly grateful for the supportive professors and strong sense of community that have shaped my experience so far. I enjoy campus life and spend much of my time in the library studying and completing coursework.
While I love my journey at St. John’s, the cost of tuition presents a significant challenge. I have two siblings who also attend the university, and although we each receive financial aid, it does not fully cover our expenses. My mother is the sole provider in our household, and my father is unable to work due to a heart condition. This creates a tremendous financial strain on my family.
Receiving this scholarship would help ease that burden and allow me to stay focused on my education and my goal of becoming a pediatric nurse. This support would make a meaningful difference for my family and help me continue working toward a career dedicated to caring for children and giving back to my community.
They do not honor st. John or Christian beliefs. They are as woke broke as people feared they would become. Do not recommend

During my first semester at St John’s University as a freshman student I was welcomed by many people who were enthusiastic about helping me find my place in this school. There’s always activities, internships, mentorship’s, and opportunities to get involved in and they encourage you to do so at this school. There’s definitely versatility and diversity of students and everyone is comforting to that matter. You’ll never feel like you don’t belong in this school. I feel as if there is not much of anything that needs to be changed in this school, it’s just your opportunity to adjust to a new scenery and create opportunities for yourself.

As I approach the second semester of my freshman year as a finance major at St. John's University, Peter J. Tobin College of Business, I want to reflect on my experience so far.
Living in the dorms has been fantastic. My suitemates and I have formed a bond and created a supportive environment, which has greatly enhanced my transition to college life.
Academically, my classes have been engaging and informative. My professors are passionate and genuinely care about our success, making it easy to approach them for help. Their support has been invaluable, and I've performed well academically.
While I appreciate the quality of education, costs for tuition and living expenses are my only concern. Increased financial assistance would help alleviate the burden, to focus more on my studies.
So far, freshman year at St. John's has been positive and fullfilling. Despite the financial concerns, I am optimistic about the future and look forward to embracing the opportunities that lie ahead.

I enjoyed my first year of undergraduate studies. There was structure within my biomedical science program, and I enjoyed having multiple classes with the same people. It was when the pandemic hit that made my studies difficult. It took me some time to adjust, and I wish some professors had been more accommodating when we were online. Online courses were not conducive to my learning style. Some professors valued active learning, but most used passive teaching, so I had to teach much of the material myself. Besides academics, I loved the university's athletics department. The gym was great, and we had access to really cool group classes like boxing and pilates/yoga. There were also events like Stormin Loud and Winter Carnival. These evetns brought my friends and I closer.

My overall experience at St. John’s University has been meaningful and growth-oriented, as it offers a supportive academic environment with professors who are knowledgeable, approachable, and genuinely invested in student success. I value the diverse campus community, which provides opportunities to learn from different cultures and perspectives. The Vincentian values are clearly reflected through the emphasis on service, leadership, and social responsibility, contributing to my academic and personal development.
I also appreciate the academic resources available, including tutoring services, career development support, and student organizations that encourage involvement and leadership. However, I would like to see improvements in financial support, particularly for international and continuing students. Increased transparency, flexibility, and availability of financial aid and scholarships would greatly benefit students who are academically committed but face financial challenges.

I liked the general vibe the school campus brings; it is very clean for the most part and organized in a manner that I prefer. Everyone on campus is also very nice and caring for the students' safety.

What I liked about my time at St. John's is that I had the opportunity to start working on aspects of my TV and film major, including editing, creating screenplays, storyboards, and producing my own sound. They were all really fun to work on. Any change I would like to see is a broader range of dining services, allowing the food options to expand, as I'm a picky eater. I would like to see more healthy choices, such as spinach, asparagus, and bananas, from the dining services, so that I can get my vitamins when I stay on campus late.

As a freshman here rn do not come here I am transfering, theres literally NOTHING to do, the frats don'e even throw and besides that there are literally no parties near bye and if they are they are so bad, they took away the biggest event Tip Off, there is barely space in the dorms at all and it gets dirty so fast. Some of the classes you have to take to graudate are the dumbest classes of all time. The people don't even care about anything and they don't look to make friends either everybody wants to stay to themselves and be in the dorm. This was so boring wasted my whole year here and money. They have little events and thats it and people barely even show up. You also literally see the same people everyday just don't come here if you want a real college experince and life.

St John's is a decent university, though I would say that the tuition is definitely overboard, even with a good scholarship. The professors are nice people, for the most part, and are helpful for when you need extra help or you just have questions.

My overall experience at St. John’s University has been incredibly positive. I’ve grown academically, professionally, and personally through supportive professors, strong community values, and opportunities like research, service work, and leadership programs. I really appreciate how the university emphasizes service, diversity, and student support, which has helped me feel empowered and prepared for my future. I have worked as a SOAR (Sexual Violence Outreach, Awareness and Response) Guide for two years as a student worker. I am appreciative that I have had the opportunity to give back to my community and making a difference on campus.
I like the environment that surrounds the school, it feels more natural yet modern. The students and teachers are friendly and encouraging.

So far, things are going well. I faced some early challenges with one of my professors, but I’m working through them, staying focused on completing my assignments, and preparing for my next set of classes. I would like to see more opportunities for tuition assistance, as that would make a big difference. Overall, I don’t think much needs to change right now—I’m still new to campus life and learning to adjust to my growing independence.
My mom always said how she made a lot of friends while there and also that was very much a lot of diversity.

ai_summary: At St. Thomas University - Florida, students appreciate the strong sense of community and supportive faculty who are often approachable and invested in their students' success. Many reviews highlight small class sizes , which facilitate personalized learning experiences and foster close relationships between students and professors. The campus is described as beautiful and easy to navigate, though some students mention limitations in campus life and extracurricular activities, as well as high tuition costs . While the athletics program is a point of pride, some express a desire for better support, particularly for less prominent teams. Food quality and dorm conditions receive mixed reviews, with some noting inconsistencies. Overall, the university is seen as a welcoming environment that offers diverse cultural experiences and academic opportunities, although there are calls for improvements in organization and communication in certain services, particularly financial aid.

reviews.text: Like most Universities, St. Thomas is a school that will make or brake you depending on your major. I was a Poli- Sci Major and therefore it was worth every tear and literal crash out because I actually learned something. I use my degree in the work I do now which is hard for many kids my age to say unfortunately. Though I did not attend every single party on campus, it is know that events happen on campus almost everyday and you do not necessarily have to be apart of the hosting major or club to have fun (8 out of 10 times). I did not live on campus so I can not really speak to dorm life, however I can imagine its pretty average like many other universities. The difference comes with the fact that there are a few newly constructed dorms so that's a plus. The school is very commuter friendly which helps with cutting the cost of attendance down significantly. Lastly, I would warn that parking is terrible but it comes with the territory as the school does not sit on much land.

My experience at St. Thomas University was very positive and well-rounded. Rugby played a big role in my time there, helping me develop discipline, teamwork, and leadership while balancing academics. The fellow students were friendly, supportive, and easy to approach, which made campus life enjoyable.
The professors were excellent—knowledgeable, approachable, and genuinely invested in students’ success. Overall, St. Thomas University provided a strong academic environment, great sporting opportunities, and a welcoming community that supported both my personal and athletic growth.

My experience at St. Thomas University has been truly amazing. What stands out the most is how personalized everything feels. The professors and staff genuinely care and take the time to understand you, your goals, and what you need to succeed. It never feels like you’re just another student—they really create a supportive environment where you can grow academically and personally. I’ve been pushed to challenge myself, explore new opportunities, and build meaningful relationships. STU has helped me gain confidence in my career path and has given me a strong sense of community. Overall, it has been a valuable and uplifting experience.

ai_summary: At Stanford University, students frequently highlight the beautiful campus and the vibrant, diverse community, which fosters academic and personal growth. Many appreciate the strong academic rigor and the quality of professors, who are generally approachable and passionate about their subjects, although some express a desire for better support in the humanities and more consistent teaching quality across departments. The extracurricular opportunities, including sports and various activities, enrich student life, though some feel the social scene could be limited and that finding a sense of community can take time. The financial aid offerings are often commended, making a Stanford education more accessible, but navigating administrative support can be challenging. Overall, students find that the collaborative environment and stunning surroundings contribute to a unique and transformative experience at Stanford.

reviews.text: I love how diverse it was and how I was able to fully immerse myself in the student culture, classes, and community at Stanford over the summer. The campus was a perfect mix of nature and city. There were a lot of options for food, activities like sports, the gym, swimming, or just hanging out with friends. The professors were really nice and they were great at providing support and help in my classes. I also really enjoyed the TA's in my classes, they served as an extension of the professors and they were always available. They defiantly helped me out when I was confused on classroom material.

Evil undertones from the hoover institute etc. I think there is something uniquely discreet & sinister about Stanford, which is currently suing its own students to protect its commitments as Silicon Valley's academic branch of empire.

Overall, most of what you hear about Stanford is true. It's a great school filled with amazing professors and teachers. I would like to see more from the administration about taking care of the school and it's students who are using their first amendment rights and who's rights are under fire from the current president.

An incredible place to learn and live! While there's not much going on in downtown Palo Alto, there are always things to do on campus. 99% of my experiences with professors and classes were wonderful. So many inspiring classmates to learn from. Research opportunities galore. BEAUTIFUL campus!!!

It's really eye-opening to meet and spend time with all these amazing people. There are extremely motivated students with various backgrounds and interests, well-established faculty with lots of research opportunities, and various faculty to help with anything from academic advising to career planning.

All things are good but i request more oppotunities of scholarships if not the food and life campus and local area is ok
Thank you
Stanford limited a lot of fun social experiences, specifically parties and large social gatherings. This made it difficult to build friendships

Stanford University is an amazing university. There are so many incredible professors, students, and opportunities. There is not much of a change I'd make as far as academics, though I believe student life such as housing and food could be better for the students.

I'm incoming student at Stanford university and I am very inspired. However, as someone from Ukraine, my financial situation has been affected by the ongoing war, making this next step both exciting and uncertain.
I’ve recently been accepted to one of the world’s top programs focused on design and innovation. Thanks to Stanford’s support, I have already secured funding to cover half of my tuition. Right now, I’m trying to find the remaining amount - at least enough to cover my first term - so I can stay in the program and become eligible for a teaching assistant position, which will help me support myself going forward.
I definitely mde the most of it! I appreciate all of the people within the institution who make themselves available to mentor and uplift students.

Stanford University offers the world's best education to prepare students for the unexpected challenges of the future. The students are brilliant, and each one brings something unique and exceptional to the table. The professors are among the brightest minds on the planet, and the conversation is enlightening at the highest levels. Stanford students are the forward-thinkers of their generation. There is no education like it on this earth.

My time at Stanford was amazing! The motto "the winds of freedom blow" perfectly encapsulates the expansive educational opportunities from world class professors to accessible professionals to vast research opportunities. The flexibility of the curriculum made it easy to be explority at any point in my college experience. I also had great opportunity to expand and explore socially and in hobbies with so many clubs and interest groups! I joined an acapella group, grew in my faith in Chi Alpha Christian Fellowship, and developed skill and endurance with the Pickleball Club! I would love to see greater opportunities for upperclassmen and lowerclassmen to interact in fun social, non party related, ways. Having a central campus hangout spot with games and activities would be amazing!

I enjoyed my time at Stanford overall but my main issue is with the administration. Their actions show they truly do not care about the students. I never felt supported by them and knew that any chance they had to disregard the student opinion or voice they took it.
Stanford is undoubtedly amazing, for the incredible people, beautiful campus, and boundless opportunity.
Vibrant and fun culture, intellectually diverse and rigorous place, clubs and people are friendly but local area is very suburban/wealthy/boring

ai_summary: Stevens Institute of Technology is highly regarded for its strong emphasis on engineering , technology, and business programs, with a rigorous curriculum that prepares students for successful careers. Many reviewers appreciate the knowledgeable faculty who often integrate real-world experience into their teaching and the supportive environment created by dedicated academic advisors and career services. Location is a significant advantage, with the beautiful campus in Hoboken providing easy access to New York City, offering students numerous internship and networking opportunities. However, there are notable criticisms regarding food quality and housing conditions, along with concerns about the administration's effectiveness and the school’s social dynamics, particularly related to student engagement and inclusivity. Some students feel the institution is heavily focused on career outcomes at the expense of a more vibrant campus life, leading to mixed experiences among the student body. Overall, students who engage actively with the community find their time at Stevens rewarding.

reviews.text: The academics at Stevens can be incredibly difficult based on your major due to accelerated math courses and such. It feels easy to fall behind but the majority of professors are understanding and willing to spend extra time so you don’t fail.

Stevens is the best school, in my opinion! Most of the staff members here are very helpful and friendly. Most professors are really helpful if you have any question and problem. Since Stevens is next to NYC, it is very convenient and easy to find places that you want to hang out and have fun! I am not forgetting to say, Stevens is still pretty expensive. However, I spent my money well to use all the students' benefits and reaching our professors for research and make my own club. So it is what you paid for, but you have to work for it!

Although Steven's Tech is an amazing college every international student would love to be. The intercultural diversity, international students' relationship and academic excellence and the cutting-edge research facilities is a top notch. I will never regret ever choosing Stevens Institute of Technology. In my next life, I'll choose Stevens again and again. I'm so proud to be an international student of the prestigious Stevens Institute of Technology

The campus life is eh, many of the clubs meet very late at night and as a commuter student, going to them is almost impossible if I'm not sleeping over at a friend's place. So far the professors have had a ton of past experience/ credibility in my chosen major, no one too nasty or mean and generally fair in their teachings. The area can be quite expenses, but food credits can be used at most of the neighboring food places using doordash.

Stevens has an amazing location right by New York City, including school-sponsored events throughout the city we get access to. Hoboken itself is a peaceful town with a good amount to do and good food. The professors range from good to genuinely infuriating, but the coursework is rigorous and I feel a lot more capable in my field of Computer Science as a result. There are a lot of opportunities for furthering my career as well. For example, this summer I am conducting summer research involving AI, a burgeoning field that I am glad to work in.

Amazing school full of creative and competitive intellectuals. If you're looking for an academic challenge, but also some fun then Stevens is for you. Being in Hoboken and not even five minutes from the city provides you with so many opportunities and activities. You never find yourself bored on a weekend or completely locked in the library. There are restaurants everywhere in Hoboken, so you can always find a good place to eat especially right next to campus on Washington street.
For the school, there are clubs and sports teams for every type of interest. There is a community and friends to be found for everyone. The view from most of the academic buildings shows the New York City skyline in all its glory. A sight you'll never grow tired of. Ever need to take a break from studying? So many places to sit and relax outside on campus or go for a walk by the Hudson. Dog parks are everywhere in Hoboken and it can be friendly sitting with some furry, four-legged friends.

The school is very supportive of its students and has many resources available to encourage a healthy balance of personal life and academic life. The community is great and is very welcoming which helps in being able to make connections with others pretty quickly. Something I would like to see changed at the institution is having more affordable living arrangements as it is already is expensive as a private university.

My experience of Stevens has overall been positive, however, there are many areas that can be improved. Academics can be very overwhelming with professors that are very smart, but arent the best at teaching. In most classes, students end up having to teach themselves the material because the professors do not do a great job at explaining information. Additionally with the load of work we have, there is not much time to pursue hobbies or research projects of our own. I'd like to see the curriculum change so that students have more time to take on extracurriculars without feeling like they are drowning in work. I would also like to see more interaction between clubs and more of a "Stevens pride" around campus. Students should feel proud to go here, and yet most times when asked about their school, Stevens students do not have this certain pride about Stevens.

I am a freshman at Stevens Institute of Technology this year. I am very happy with the academic programs. I feel like the professors are well versed in the areas they teach and the education I'm receiving is outstanding. I however, I would prefer to see changes made to student life, especially the quality of the food. I am also from a very rural part of NJ and am having trouble adjusting to city life. Overall, it's an excellent institute with highly qualified teaching staff and I am confident I will graduate with a dream job lined up.

The people at Stevens really care about making sure students can graduate and do well in the future. Academic advisors and the career center are extremely helpful resources at the school, and they give very good advice. Moreover, study plans are given so that students know when and which classes to take so that they're not behind. However, food is not great, and the housing is mediocre. But, those issues are alleviated by its proximity to New York City and the Hoboken area.

I like the people and the friends I have made. As a commuter, it is harder to find a nice group of friends and feel connected with others, but at Stevens it has been easy to make friends. The classes are small and concentrated so you can get to know your professors and they teach interesting material that is applicable to the outside world.

Stevens has been the best choice I have made so far in my life. There's something for everyone here, whether that in academics, student life, athletics, activities, etc. You can be as involved as you want without feeling pressured to do more or less. Everyone fits in somewhere. Plus you can't beat that view. Professors in the Mech Eng dept are great, and the ones outside of that department that I have interacted with are good too. The people here feel like a community, and students are always open to helping each other out. Hoboken is a great spot too, there is never a shortage of things to do. The reputation this school has goes a long way, even outside the NJ/NY area. Campus never feels empty, but you're also never overwhelmed like some large state schools. The athletics dept has incredible staff, and coaches especially understand the rigor of classes and are willing to work with their athletes to be the best they can be.

My experience at Stevens Institute of Technology has been largely positive. The professors are passionate about their subjects and genuinely care about student success. The campus is beautiful, with great resources like labs and study spaces that support both academic and personal growth. I especially appreciate the strong emphasis on real-world application of knowledge, particularly through internships and research opportunities.
However, there are areas for improvement. The social scene could be more inclusive and varied, with more events or spaces that cater to different interests.
Stevens is a fun place and the people there seem driven and excited to be there while the Professors and staff are eager to help you out.

I have gotten everything I wanted out of Stevens. Although I was accepted to a few more brand name schools, I chose Stevens because of its focused Engineering programs, excellent location, and friendly student body. It has not disappointed in any of these areas. The professors in my program know me well. I have had the chance to study and research abroad two summers in a row - paid for by Stevens Pinnacle Scholars program. I have done research in a lab and taken on leadership roles in campus clubs. All of these experiences have led to me getting a highly competitive internship this summer in Manhattan. The students at Stevens are kind, diligent, and very driven. I like being with peers who take school seriously and are focused on the future. At the same time, they are not elitist or pretentious, which makes it easier to collaborate and learn together. Stevens is a great place to get an education. Hoboken is an awesome college town - safe and fun with great food choices.

ai_summary: At Stockton University, students appreciate the warm , inclusive atmosphere and the close-knit community fostered among both peers and faculty. The academic experience is generally highlighted by small class sizes and dedicated professors who genuinely care about student success and provide ample support, including academic advising and resources for financial aid. While students find the campus visually appealing and enjoy the outdoor environment, concerns about parking availability, food options, and limited social activities on weekends are common. Many students feel that being engaged in campus organizations or clubs enhances their overall experience, and while there is a strong sense of camaraderie , some express a desire for more vibrant campus life and diversity. Overall, Stockton is noted for its academic strength and commitment to student welfare, although some logistical challenges remain.

reviews.text: I think the university in general is a decent. The isn’t huge, which is great if you don’t like too big of crowds. They also have smaller class numbers, as in the class sizes range from 15-35 most of the time.

At Stockton University, almost everyone you encounter is friendly and cheerful. It is fairly easy to make friends, and the professors are not only helpful, but willing to make your college experience as smooth as possible. The dorms are kept fairly clean, but I do wish they were cleaned more often. However, they are larger than I anticipated and feel home-like. The campus is beyond beautiful and there is usually not a speck of litter outside or in classrooms. I would say my experience at Stockton thus far has been great but my favorite aspect, besides academics, is definitely the friendly community.

Stockton has been very good experience for me as a new transfer student. All the staff have been very helpful in this process. The professors are excellent, challenging, and encouraging! The campus is well diversified and offer many activities for students to engage in. All the amenities that make academic life at Stockton beautiful are truly valued.

Stockton U. has a calm, focused atmosphere that’s ideal for students who prioritize academics and the gym. The campus is surrounded by nature, which gives it a quiet, grounded feel and makes it easier to stay organized and mentally locked in. Classes are smaller, so professors actually know you and notice your effort, which makes taking school seriously feel worthwhile. There’s a strong study culture—seeing students working in the library or common spaces is normal, and the environment encourages consistency rather than competition. The gym is a big part of daily life and fits naturally into a productive routine. The fitness center is clean, modern, and active without being intimidating. Students regularly work out between classes or after long study sessions, and the gym culture is more about discipline and balance than showing off. Overall, Stockton feels intentional and steady, a place where you can focus on school, stay consistent in the gym, and grow without constant distractions.
Stockton has been a very enjoyable experience. The best part is definitely the campus life. The people are great, the food is diverse, and the clubs are fun.

Science professors are amazing along with the surrounding area. It is a suitcase school, so if you’re living on campus it is dead on the weekends. The housing is expensive for the mediocre living experience.
I like being close to Atlantic City for clubs and bars. I also like the different housing options, specifically the on campus apartments in AC with separate bedrooms.
Idk but I hope there’s more options that I would hope to see in the futre and that is just it now I guess.

Stockton has been great for my premed post bacc. I have great advisors and professors. The visiting professors are noticeably worse than the full time ones. The STEM courses are very through and allow a student to do well with hard work. There are many campus events, although I have not taken part in many.

I love it because no matter where you walk around, people are always so helpful and sweet to you. Everyone is so welcoming and I was very nervous to start in a new environment but once people spoke to me I knew I was calm and safe.
There’s nothing to do on the weekends and most of the food places are not open. Also there is no football team so the only games to go to are like soccer or basketball.

Being a freshman at Stockton has opened my eyes in so many ways. This environment and place are very different from where I grew up. Being away from my family and my culture has definitely been hard, but it has also been fun. The events and people make it worth it.
My overall experience has been really good there is a lot resources for everyone and lots of clubs to join

My experience at Stockton has been overwhelmingly positive and enjoyable. The professors are excellent in their respective fields and are very approachable when additional assistance is required in certain courses. I am also a member of Delta Epsilon Mu, which has increased my involvement on campus and facilitated my interactions with others. The only suggestion I have is to improve the parking situation and add more quiet study rooms. As someone who requires accommodations, I prefer to work in a quieter environment, but unfortunately, there aren’t many quiet spaces on campus.

Stockton University is a great university for those who are in-state. They have a ton of resources that can help you succeed for your future. The professors have an outstanding understanding on each individual's journey, and help them throughout their experience.

ai_summary: Stony Brook University has a beautiful campus and offers a rigorous academic environment , particularly strong in STEM fields, with many opportunities for research and involvement in various clubs. However, students note that the academics can be quite challenging, especially in STEM majors, and class sizes for introductory courses can be large, making personal interaction with professors difficult. Student life seems to be a mixed bag; while there are many activities and a supportive community , some students feel that the social scene can be lacking, particularly on weekends when many commuters leave campus. Dining options also receive criticism for their quality and variety . Overall, Stony Brook provides a solid education at an affordable price, but students must actively seek out social opportunities and be prepared for a demanding workload.

reviews.text: My daughter went to Stony Brook for summer school. The professors behaved like children wearing their favorite revolutionary leftist buttons. We aren't conservatives, but when did it become ok for teachers to voice their opinions on subjects that have nothing to do with their areas of expertise? My daughter was in stem science camp, not leftist boot camp. If you believe in Communism, or believe that terrorism is a legitimate means of political discourse, perhaps you should be teaching those subjects in a country that believes in those ideas. I'm pretty disgusted with what I witnessed at Stony Brook and I'm sorry to have given them a penny in tuition.

The school itself is very diverse with great research opportunities. However, though it isn't advertised as a "STEM/Tech" school, that is what it caters to most. This can leave those studying the humanities or even soft-STEM fields feeling overlooked. The location of the school is nice, especially if you have a car. You get the beach and are a train ride away from NYC. Many students are commuters, and though they are friendly, they don't stick around long and leave right after their classes, which can make for a divided campus feel between those who live on and off campus. If one wants to go into medicine, engineering, or some other STEM-related field, Stony Brook has many opportunities both in and beyond the classroom.

Stony Brook university is a great school. They provide students with everything they need in order to succeed academically. Stony Brook gives you the perfect balance of student life and high achieving education. The professors are great, however some are better than others, which is to be expected. However, please know that all professors are there with your very best interest in mind, and are always willing and able to help you out. Overall, Stony Brook is a great school and lives up to it’s name of the number one Public University in New York.

ai_summary: Suffolk University offers a distinctive urban experience as it is located in the heart of Boston, providing students access to city life, internship opportunities, and a diverse community . However, the reviews reveal mixed feelings about academics, with some students noting a lack of academic rigor in certain majors and inconsistencies in teaching quality. While many appreciate the small class sizes and the chance to connect personally with professors, others feel that the academic services and quality, especially in STEM, are lacking. Housing appears to be a point of contention, with some students facing issues like mice infestations and limited options for upperclassmen. The campus atmosphere is described as less traditional, making it harder for students, particularly commuters, to engage socially, leading to a perception of a weak sense of community and school spirit. Tuition is frequently mentioned as high, with many expressing that it feels unjustified given the overall experience. Overall, Suffolk seems to attract students who prioritize career-oriented education in a vibrant city setting, even if it lacks some classic college experiences.

reviews.text:
Suffolk offers a strong academic environment, but what draws me even more is its place within Boston’s larger intellectual community. Being surrounded by over fifty colleges means being immersed in a city where ambition and curiosity are part of everyday life. I’m someone who is motivated by the people around me—I want to be in an environment where conversations push me, where opportunities are constantly within reach, and where success is not unusual but expected.
This sense of support and engagement was clear during my initial visit. My academic advisor rearranged his schedule to meet with me personally and worked to apply my previous credits in a way that would allow me to enter with junior standing. That experience stood out to me because it reflected a level of individual attention and investment that I value in an academic setting. We need more educators who are truly passionate about education.

If you are looking for a unique opportunity to gain experience and network with organizations from around the globe while getting a great education for your money, then Suffolk University is somewhere to consider. The overall environment is fantastic for students to explore their academic possibilities while seeking for themselves in this early stage of adulthood.

I'm a commuter student, so I'm really only here to go to class and then I usually go home right after. I will say there are a lot of other commuters at this school so that is nice. All the train lines are close by, so the commute is easier. I'm an interior design major so my classes are 2.5 hours long, which isn't ideal, but it's required. The classes aren't bad, definitely harder than I thought. Some of my classes feel really pointless for my career and not what I plan on doing when I finish school. The ones that are more of what I do want to do are fun. The professors are okay, some are really good and helpful, while others are the opposite, but that's kind of something that would happen at any school. I'm not in any clubs but I know some people that are and they always talk about how they like it. I'm not in a club because it's hard to have time when I commute and my classes are 2.5 hours long. There is always little events going on for clubs that are fun.
It was amazingly diverse. Meeting international students of many kinds, and having the opportunity of traveling abroad myself.

I'm a transfer student and it wasn't the college experience I had in mind. there is no campus, you have to go out of your way to make friends by joining clubs and such, but the location is good as it s right in the middle of the city
I really liked being in the big city. It was a huge change of pace from the usual boring neighborhoods I used to live in. As for changes, it would nicer if the dining hall food was a little cheaper.
I want to apply there, it's been my dream college for many years now. I'm trying to apply for scholarships so I can afford my tuition there.

I began at Suffolk in Fall 2025 as an Undergraduate Freshman. I am currently majoring in Marketing. So far, it's been an okay experience. The professors are really nice and helpful. The workload hasn't been that bad. The location of the school is amazing since it's in a big part of Boston. The only thing I dislike about the school is lack of campus/student life, especially cause Suffolk doesn't have a set campus. I would recommend Suffolk for students who plan to major in Business or Law related majors. But other than that, I like the school and glad I came here.

If you want to get involved in local or state government in Massachusetts or you love the city of Boston, there is no better place on earth for you to do that. So many opportunities to get involved in politics and government in this city, you will be showered with internship opportunities if you know where to look. And if you want the fun that comes with living in an incredible location in downtown Boston, go here and dorm! But if you arent going for those things or you want a traditional college experience this is a very middling choice. Hence 4 stars

Suffolk University has been an incredible place for both my academic and personal growth. Known for its strong law and business programs and its prime downtown Boston location, it offers a dynamic city-centered campus with multiple buildings woven into the heart of the city. The small size fosters familiarity and belonging; I know professors, staff, and even dining hall and security personnel by name, sharing friendly conversations that make campus feel like home. Opportunities like Free Activities Period, dorm events, and student clubs create meaningful ways to connect with friends, regardless of schedules. These experiences have helped me build lasting relationships and a strong network. The only improvement I hope for is more affordable dining options, as current prices can be challenging for students. Overall, Suffolk has given me a supportive, inspiring environment, rich connections, and a solid foundation to pursue my academic, creative, and career ambitions.
I had some bad experiences with some of the professors in the journalism program. I feel like I left with great connections, however.

I have been attending Suffolk for a year now, going into Sophomore year, and i could easily say I love suffolk. It’s such an amazing school with really helpful people and i’m so happy i chose this school! You’ll make lots of friends and have tons of fun experiences at the never ending events the school holds for the students. It’s a very diverse school and very inclusive as well. I can’t stress it enough but i am eternally grateful for choosing this school, for all the professors that go above and beyond to help you, and all the faculty that are always there to make sure everything is accommodated for you. Love love love Suffolk University! Go Rams!
Suffolk is a very welcoming community, that has something for everyone. When you show up you automatically feel like you're part of the family. Everyone there is very honest and friendly.

I love how diverse this university is. When I was initially applying to schools one of the main features I was looking for was diversity. The students truly do make campus what it is. It's always a great time to go to campus and the professors are very knowledgable in the biology and chemistry department. They want their students to succeed and care deeply for their students.

My experience at Suffolk University has been incredibly rewarding. I’ve grown academically stronger and more confident in my abilities, thanks to challenging coursework and supportive professors. I’ve also developed a better sense of connection with my peers, both in and out of the classroom, and I truly appreciate the inclusive and welcoming environment Suffolk fosters. Although I’m a commuter student, I still feel part of the community, and I’ve heard nothing but great things from students who live on campus. Overall, Suffolk has helped me grow both intellectually and socially, and I’m grateful to be part of this university.

ai_summary: Syracuse University showcases a vibrant campus atmosphere characterized by strong school spirit and an active social scene , particularly for those who enjoy partying and attending sports events. There's a wide variety of academic programs, with students particularly praising departments like Newhouse and architecture for their quality and networking opportunities . Although most professors are noted as supportive and engaging, some students express concerns about consistency in teaching quality and access to academic resources, like advisors. The campus is beautiful and offers many extracurricular activities but comes with a hefty tuition price tag , which many find challenging. Some reviews highlight issues with safety in the surrounding area and a perceived lack of diversity among the student body. Overall, Syracuse University offers a mix of academic rigor, community involvement, and spirited campus life.

reviews.text: Literally the best school ever! Quintessential college experience with a beautiful campus, great party scene, great academics, and fun athletics.

I loved the campus community that I’ve built during my Syracuse University! This community was only possible due to the numerous organizations and clubs that are available on campus! Though the food quality has been kind of mid, it seems like the university is trying to make adjustments based on feedback from the students.

Syracuse has been a great experience so far. I have met some great people and enjoy participating in extracurricular activities around campus. There is always something to do on the weekends outside of studying.

I have loved Syracuse. I am a student in the business school. I will say at the beginning of my college education it was a lot to get used to they foster competitiveness and finding a on campus job is not that easy. However the academics were perfect they were challenging but provided the materials for a student to succeed in life, being a student here I was able to push myself to want to receive a higher education. I have always loved the social life and academic ridge it provides.
I am very happy with my time here at Syracuse. I enjoy my classes and the professors here at Syracuse. Everyone is so helpful here.

Overall, Syracuse is a great school. I do see them following the trend of cutting humanities, which is very disappointing. I think Syracuse needs to focus more on academics and less on sports. I've heard that they are starting an "influencer" track in the communications school, which I think is bizarre, and would prefer they focus more on humanities that always trying to keep up with the latest trends.

This is my first year at Syracuse University as a transfer student, and I have overall really enjoyed being on campus so far. The academics are rigorous and challenging, but the professors are very engaging and helpful, and resources like the career center have helped me build my professional network and prepare me for the real world. The reasoning for my current 2.7 GPA reflects my adjustment period to a different academic environment, and since then, I have become more comfortable and dedicated to improving my cumulative GPA for the current semester and beyond. Overall, I would not change anything about Syracuse University.

I have really enjoyed my time here at Syracuse, and would recommend it to anyone who can afford it and thinks that they can brave the freezing winters. I feel like I have been able to find community within my dorm, the organizations I have joined, and through Greek life. However, one should note that the food could use improvement, the older dorms have a lot of problems, including wasps, and we often get emails from the Department of Public Safety informing us of something weird occurring on campus.

Syracuse University is an exceptional school. From an environment that is welcoming to new students, to cutting-edge research opportunities, to outstanding housing and on-campus amenities for students, and excellent instructors, staff and educational programs, this is the university kids dream of. After a day of lectures from experienced, caring professors, heading to the state-of-the-art Barnes Center at The Arch to climb, swim or take a yoga class to decompress is something great to look forward to. Beautiful architecture on campus, D1 sports events to attend, an in-house art gallery, representation of every spiritual group and hundreds of student organizations to choose from- what more could we ask for?! The safety here is top-notch, transportation is made easily available and overall this school is run so efficiently. If you want an outstanding education and a real, well-rounded college experience, look no further than Syracuse University.

As a single mother studying part time while working, continuing on a straight academic path is not always easy. Syracuse University has provided a supportive environment that made it possible to keep moving forward. The professors and advisors were always helpful, understanding and willing to work with my schedule and individual needs. They treated me as a whole person not just a student which made a significant difference. I appreciate the flexibility, and encouragement I received, which allowed me to stay committed to my degree.

I liked Syracuse University for the opportunities that were given to me. The Sports Analytics program is very niche, as very few colleges have it. In addition, the professors all pushed me to do my best and I've been in very good company with my peers.

This is an incredibly large school and I think that's amazing because you get to come across and meet so many new, great people, even if you have one encounter and never see them again. I also came from a small high school so it can be a little intimidating but there's so much this school has to offer when it comes to resources, opportunities, and chances to connect with others.
The courses, I find, to be very thought-provoking and depending on the course, it can definitely challenge students to think more critically and be more engaged with the material. It's definitely important to improve your time management as soon as possible because the work can get a little rigorous (depends on your major).
I also commute, and if you've been in Syracuse for a while, you know how bad it can get in the winter season... But don't let that stop you from applying! This is a great university in so many ways and I'm proud to call myself a student from here.

Moving across the country to come here was my biggest risk ever taken. But i'm literally so happy I did. This university is perfect for me and I've learned so much just in my first semester. I feel that I am getting a quality education from some of the best professors. One thing I would like to see changed is the food to be fixed. I know campus food isn't going to be the best but I feel like it could be better quality and more diverse among the week. I don't want to have the same meal for 3 days straight.

I really like it here at Syracuse. The campus is super diverse, and as a Caribbean student I’ve met a lot of people from different backgrounds, which is really cool. The food is honestly pretty mid, but you can make it work. Biggest warning: the snow is crazy. If you hate cold weather, this is not the place for you. Overall though, I’m having a good experience.
It is not a very walkable campus as there is a lot of hills, and some residence hall are further away from campus.

ai_summary: Tarleton State University offers a warm , welcoming atmosphere that many students appreciate, often describing it as a " home away from home ." The campus is noted for its small size, making it easy to navigate and fostering close relationships between students and professors, which is a highlight of the academic experience. Students have mixed feelings about the quality of teaching, as while many praise the dedication and approachability of the faculty, others mention inconsistencies in teaching quality across different departments. The university has a strong sense of community and tradition on campus, with plenty of opportunities for involvement in clubs and activities. However, some students express concerns regarding diversity and parking issues, as well as the organization of student services. Overall, Tarleton is seen as a solid choice for students seeking a supportive and engaging college experience in a smaller, rural setting.

reviews.text: Well for it being my first year as a college freshmen it was ruff just as anyone would assume. I did like the church they partned with called paradigm and that really got me to stickk it through the semester i was there. Expeciallly my one Kine professor Ms.Mckay i also enjoyed her teachings and classes as well

Tarleton State University has been a very welcoming and supportive place from what I have experienced and learned so far. I appreciate how the university focuses on helping students succeed academically while also creating a strong sense of community. The campus environment feels friendly and encouraging, and there are many opportunities for students to grow, get involved, and prepare for their future careers. I especially like that Tarleton offers strong programs and resources for students who want to make a difference in their communities. One thing I would love to see continue improving is even more support and awareness around student mental health and wellness. Overall, Tarleton seems like a place where students can feel supported, challenged, and inspired to reach their goals.

I am currently still in my freshman year here, honestly I am loving it! Of course there are some of the usual downsides with housing and dining hall food but most every one of my classes has been going well, I don't regret ever coming here, looking forward to the rest of my time here.

It has a vibrant community, but you will really only fit in if you are familiar with country folk. I liked nearly all of my professors, I just struggled with the community as I am very used to a culturally diverse environment. nearly all students here are white.

My overall experience at Tarleton State University has been really positive. I’ve enjoyed the supportive environment and the way professors are willing to help students succeed. The smaller class sizes make it easier to ask questions and really engage with the material. I also appreciate the opportunities to get involved on campus, which have helped me grow both personally and professionally. What I like most is how the university encourages students to pursue their goals and provides resources to help us succeed, especially in programs like education, where hands-on experience is so important. The campus community feels welcoming, and I’ve made meaningful connections with professors and classmates. The only changes I would like to see are adding more parking and updating housing to allow for more upperclassmen. These improvements would make life on campus even better for students who are continuing their education.

I have greatly enjoyed my time at Tarleton State University. I interact primarily with the staff, due to being online. The staff has been so helpful. I am a first generation, former foster care student. I need my "hand held" in a lot of areas regarding scheduling, registering, and choosing classes. They have been wonderful. I have went to campus quite a bit of time for meetings and in High School for extracurricular activities. It is always a nice time being there and mingling with other students!

Im a senior at Vista Ridge High School. I had taken a tour of Tarleton State university to see the campus and learn more about their Pre-vet course they had to offer their. As I was walking though with the tour guid I really enjoyed the campus and the people that were their. It was diverse with all different people which made me feel welcomed. Later after my tour I applyed to the school and got in. This school has became my number one top school that I want to get into. Even though I didn't get to see the dorms I heard good review from the students their and from past graduates that I know from working/interning at a vet clinc.
I like that I can pursue my degree from anywhere and I can work at my own pace. I would like to see more programs such as dance and film

My experience at Tarleton State University has been meaningful and rewarding. From the start, I felt welcomed into a supportive community that values student success. One of the things I liked most was the smaller class sizes, which made it easier to connect with professors who genuinely care about helping students learn and grow. I never felt like just a number, and that personal support made a big difference in my confidence and motivation.
I also appreciated the variety of campus resources, organizations, and events that encouraged growth beyond the classroom. These opportunities helped me develop important skills and feel more involved on campus.
One change I would like to see is clearer communication about campus resources and events so students can take better advantage of what is offered. Expanding study spaces would also help support the growing student population.

Tarleton is such a fun campus, and even on a random Tuesday you can still find so many things to do. The professors are amazing, and the only things I have to say is make sure you wear purple on Thursdays, watch out for the poo, and don’t EVER step on the grass!
I'm having an amazing time at the university. The learning experience has been fantastic and the atmosphere is wonderful.
Great experience so far. I have been there several times over the years to watch football games and to watch my niece on the dance team. It was a great experience.
I like the different amount of people at Tarleton and how others get along well not knowing each other before coming to the university and the campus is nice and simple

Almost flunked out twice due to my mental disability. Instead of the college try to help me on it, felt like they wanted nothing to do to me. Made sure they were aware of my mental disability as well. They (unsurprisingly) didn’t seem care and didn’t seem to think it was important that I knew I could get accommodations for it either. Guess they figured a student almost flunking out twice and dropping out because he gave up was easier than helping someone to succeed. Also I almost starved to death while going here at least twice. Was nothing but hell here. Would sooner shoot myself in the foot then ever step foot in even the town this college is in.

Some of the things I love about TSU is the people, student engagement, and football season. Some of the thing i don’t necessarily like about TSU is the food is a little iffy sometimes, campus hours are weird, and getting around was a little confusing at first.

ai_summary: Temple University offers a diverse and vibrant campus experience , situated right in the heart of Philadelphia, which adds a unique urban flavor to student life. Many reviews highlight the variety of majors available and the quality of academics , particularly praising some professors for their dedication and experience. The school's extensive resources , clubs, and events provide numerous opportunities for engagement and networking. However, students have expressed concerns about safety in the surrounding neighborhood and the quality of student services, particularly financial aid and administrative support. On the social front, while some students found it challenging to make friends in a large school with nearly 40,000 students, others appreciated the campus community and found ways to connect through dorm life and activities. Overall, students appreciate the blend of city life with campus amenities , although opinions on safety and administrative responsiveness vary significantly.

reviews.text: I am a freshman here at Temple and I love it. There is a lot of things that happen around the campus so its a little overwhelming but I got used to it pretty fast. Temple is very diverse and as a black man, it makes me feel more comfortable to be around more people that are just like me.

Temple University is a massive school with a wide range of majors to study. As an engineering major in Philadelphia I felt that Temple was trying, and falling short, of competing with other more technical universities in the same city. It felt like their student base was lacking and the professors cared more about their research/just getting by instead of their teaching. While I did have some excellent professors, I didn't get to them until late in the degree. This school is massive; it provides you with the opportunities to try any hobby, club, or sport you may be interested in. However, because of it's size you will likely end up living off campus which kills the classic campus vibe you might be looking for.

My experience with Temple University has been awesome, I've made a lot of connections when it comes to my major, I am a film major, so connecting with other film majors has been a huge highlight of my experience. Grasping a sense of where I belong and my identity hasn't at all been questioned when attending this university. Life on campus feels welcoming, though sometimes I do get worried when danger hits campus.. But the school does a good job at keeping students safe for the most part. Overall, I've had my fair share of an experience here at Temple, and I can't wait to continue my journey here at this school.
My temple experience was fun. Touring campus and researching makes the university seem very interesting.
I loved it. Philadelphia is a wonderful city. Temples professors were phenomenal. I made a lot of friends.

I had a good experience at Temple University. The walk between classes gave me a wonderful idea pf the vast majority of students as well as the student life that involved people interacting with each other and navigating around campus.

If you're from PA and looking for good in-state schools to go to, I wouldn't recommend Temple. I am in my last semester here and can say I wouldn't recommend anybody come here. There are so many better schools in PA to go to, where people don't get shot and killed in the restaurants on campus. Its very rare that you ever hear any positive news about this school. It feels as if any news that comes out about Temple is almost always negative news regarding campus safety. Some of the few pros of Temple are things that have nothing to do with the university itself. Philly has great places to eat, and if you're a Philadelphia sports fan its obviously fun to get to live so close to all of the action. However, if you are not already in love with Philadelphia, I would not recommend going to Temple. Besides safety, there are so many terrible professors here; we are constantly getting new deans, and it's just so depressing to walk outside every day to trash flying around the streets.

I love the School. I have meet great people here and all the teachers and staff are very helpful. I enjoy all of my classes because how nice and great their teaching style is. I would love to stay long here.
My begining at Temple was many different things, scary, fun, enlightening. the people who welcomed me was the best part.

Temple University is a great university for those looking to experience a more urban campus and are okay with being close to the city. The professors can be strict and the classes are difficult, however I haven't met a single person who wasn't kind and thoughtful. It's also nice being in a community that is very proud of their athletics department and the performing arts programs. Coming into the school know that you will soon bleed the cherry and white and most students at Temple do.

A lot of people, new to the north so in my opinion the perfect medium between being in the middle of a big city while still feeling safe because it is easy to walk on campus but there is a lot of security, so it is hard to get in anywhere on campus. I really like the opportunities to join clubs, and if you advocate for yourself apply for jobs, internships, etc. I occasionally find it hard counseling wise to really understand how much I'm doing "right", and finding the right people in my field of performing arts without being able to declare a concentration is hard. But, what was ideal and what I wanted from a college is the fact that I wanted somewhere I would get valuable training without being squared into a box of just performing arts which Temple allows plenty of room to do all theatre related stuff of mix up my time, and has led me to pursuing a minor in Spanish which despite my experience in the language would have never crossed my mind until I was given the chance at Temple.
