While many people say that the student and social life is subpar, I believe that this is simply an academic environment that requires students to dedicate more of their time to their studies rather than friendships. The housing and dining situations are not always what I'd like them to be, but I find that compared to other elite colleges and universities, we fit exactly in the middle in terms of quality.

While studying at the University of Chicago, I have come to notice and understand what growing and learning are, and when I say growing, I mean getting to know myself better and interacting with other people, understanding how they may perceive certain interactions differently, and how I can adjust to things in different contexts. Thanks to my school not being a party school, there isn't much to focus on except for education, really, and being athletic and posting whatever makes a kid look "aesthetic." The food may need work, but the workers are worth it every time because they make you laugh, as well as the teachers on this campus.

My experience at the University of Chicago was intellectually intense and deeply rewarding. The academic culture is rigorous, with passionate students and professors who genuinely value learning. I appreciated the Core Curriculum for broadening my perspective and the many research and career opportunities available. The campus is beautiful, and once you find your community, the friendships are incredibly strong. However, the fast-paced quarter system can be overwhelming, and administrative processes sometimes feel slow or confusing. Overall, it’s a great fit for curious, driven students who thrive in an academic environment.

Attending the Open House Weekend at the University of Chicago was a positive experience.
Through touring , sitting in on classes, and listening to first year seminars, it became clear that the culture encourages both individual growth and collaboration, preparing students for success in their fields. The department’s commitment to interdisciplinary research stood out, as students and faculty worked together to tackle complex scientific challenges. Observing students engaged in projects reinforced my belief that the University of Chicago provides an environment where both academic rigor and personal growth flourish.
The combination of cutting-edge research, academic excellence, and a diverse, inclusive community makes it an ideal environment for students who are eager to push the boundaries of science. I look forward to the opportunity to contribute to such a dynamic institution that values academic rigor, diversity, and collaboration.

The University of Chicago offers a rigorous academic environment with a strong emphasis on intellectual curiosity and critical thinking. The campus is beautiful, and there's a vibrant, diverse community of students and faculty. The resources for research and the opportunities for interdisciplinary learning are outstanding. It's a place that challenges and inspires.

As a data science major at the University of Chicago, I’ve found the program both exciting and rapidly evolving. Since it's a new major, there's a sense of being part of something being built from the ground up. The faculty are passionate, and the program is designed to accommodate the multi-disciplinary nature of data science, with many classes cross-listed in statistics, computer science, and public policy, allowing students to develop a well-rounded skill set. Resources like the Data Science Institute and opportunities to work on real-world problems are a huge plus. The flexibility and UChicago’s rigorous core make it a great place to explore data science and other areas of study in a broader and diverse intellectual context.
The University of Chicago is a wonderful university with amazing academics. Located in Chicago, students can also explore the large city.

Chicago's reputation as being the place where fun goes to die could not be further off. As a member of a fraternity at University of Chicago, I can truly say there is any amount of fun if you put in the effort to find it. The Greek life at the school is genuinely pretty solid, and parties are pretty frequent. Not to mention being so close to downtown Chicago, you can go out as much as you want. The education is absolute top notch, and I have had some of the best teachers of my life.

Overall, University of Chicago stood up to the title 'second home'. Although I had some challenges to overcome, they maintained my scholarship throughout the years, were home to all 6 years of scholarship experience (2 high school, 4 undergrad), and had experienced, dedicated professors. What needs work is the advisors. The college advisors assigned to me knew essentially nothing about what was the best path for me to earn my major. They overall served as more of a roadblock. The best resource I delt with was the student advisor in the department of the major I was interested in. The general advisors helped me put together an undoable class schedule, while the department specific advisor knew what courses were best to take earlier vs. later, and what courses not to put together in the same quarter.

ai_summary: The University of Cincinnati (UC) is known for its vibrant campus life , featuring a mix of social activities, clubs, and sporting events that engage students. Reviewers mention that the campus is beautiful and offers a unique blend of modern and historic buildings , which creates a pleasant atmosphere for studying and socializing. The co-op program is a standout feature , particularly for engineering students, as it provides valuable work experience and helps students secure employment after graduation. While many reviewers praise the caring and supportive professors , experiences with faculty and advisors can be inconsistent; some students report difficulty in obtaining responses or support. Additionally, reviews mention that while there are abundant resources on campus, dining options and some accommodations could use improvement. Safety concerns are noted, particularly regarding crime in the surrounding area, but many feel secure on campus due to active campus safety measures . Overall, UC offers a solid educational experience with unique opportunities, especially for those looking for a lively collegiate environment combined with practical career preparations.

reviews.text: Overall Cincinnati is a good school. Campus life is very lively and exciting to live in. The dining hall food could use a change.

I've had an overall positive experience with them, though the College of Arts and Science and the College of Engineering and Applied Sciences could not be more different. My A&S experience has been fantastic. I would love to see a change of leadership within CEAS though, as their way of running the college and its courses is quite detrimental to student success.
overall, have had a great experience. The housing is pretty nice and they’re adding a bunch of new units for the 2027 school year.

I really enjoy the specific classes for my major and I like that it was easy to make friends. The food at the dining halls is not the best quality and not all high in protein or fruits and veggies, so I would like to see that changed.
University of Cincinnati it’s a good college they are welcoming stuff nice and it’s a good environment.

My overall experience with Cincinnati has been okay. I thought I would like it coming in as it seems like there's a lot to do, but without a car on campus, it feels very repetitive. The campus is compact, so it is easy to walk to classes but because UC is on a hill, it can be treacherous sometimes. I've had a hard time finding my people and my community, but overall, people are nice and try to make conversations, you just have to be willing to look for them, too. I would like to see more togetherness when it comes to clubs with new members because many clubs that I have joined have made me feel like an outcast since I wasn't there from the beginning. Overall, it hasn't been too bad! There is always something new to learn and new people to meet, and I think communication is key on campus.

I attend the University of Cincinnati (UC) as a graduate student and also attended the university for my undergraduate degree. The academics and the professional opportunities they provide have helped me feel prepared whenever I gain hands-on experience at an internship or co-op. UC has opened many doors and opportunities for me to grow in my education, professionalism, and leadership skills. The opportunities I have gained through my time at the University have made me a competitive candidate for professional roles.

I am currently a student here and I love it. I am a transfer from the university of Kentucky and I have to say I like Cincinnati a lot better. I love the environment and the campus is beautiful. There is also so much to do around here.

My experience with University of Cincinnati has been a pleasant one. I have been able to grow a strong connection with both of my roommates and makes friends with people in my classes. Along with that, my teachers for the most part have been helpful.
I love everything about it, don’t change anything.the classes are nice the teacher well just u will have a lil sleepless nights,but that’s oky.
I think the University of Cincinnati is one of the best college institutions in the state of Ohio. The campus life, the education, and the price is outstanding!

My experience at the university is limited, as I am only a freshman entering my second semester. However, the University of Cincinnati has already shaped me and contributed to my growth as an individual in many ways. I love the city's community and culture. Networking is easy given the faculty and staff's approachability. In terms of change, there has to be a way to improve campus safety. Neighborhoods around the campus are likely to harbor dangerous situations for students. For example, there were countless shootings during the weekend of Halloween last year.
Great college with a diverse range of studies. The social life is fun and the athletics are competitive.

I have only been a student at the University of Cincinnati for one semester, but have loved my time there. This college is very diverse and has many opportunities for students. I have joined many clubs and programs, each tailored to different interests and personalities, making it easy to get involved and connected on campus. I also love the Learning Communities within your major; it made it easier to connect with students in similar academic paths. A lot of colleges don't do this, but it truly makes Cincinnati stand out. Something that I would like to see change on campus is the ability to live on campus longer than your first year. While it is technically permitted, it is strongly discouraged, making it challenging for first-year students to fully establish friendships and feel settled before needing to secure off-campus housing. I had to sign a lease for the next year before Thanksgiving, and was very privileged to have known people from high school whom I could live with.

I started my freshman year in the fall and I really enjoy it here. I have liked how many clubs there are on campus there is something for everyone. The thing that sucks is parking passes the website crashes every time the portal opens and then it’s impossible to get one. And when contacting the parking office, they don’t answer the phone and don’t respond to emails until a week later. I don’t like the avoidance from the parking office. I enjoy the students there. I also like how the professors do truly care about me. Even if it’s hard for them to show. A lot of them provide plenty of extra credit and office hours to make sure that each student succeeds in their course.

ai_summary: At the University of Colorado Boulder, students report a mixed experience that highlights both the vibrant campus life and academic opportunities. Many appreciate the beautiful location and access to outdoor activities , with Boulder being described as a great place for adventure and relaxation. Academically, the professors often receive praise for their dedication and willingness to support students, although experiences can vary widely, particularly regarding class sizes and the degree of individual attention offered. Some students find it challenging to connect with peers, citing a lack of diversity and difficulties in student interactions, especially for commuters. Additionally, concerns about the high tuition costs and limited scholarship opportunities for transfer students are common, alongside critiques of campus housing and administrative inefficiencies. Ultimately, while CU Boulder offers a rich college experience, students express a need for improvement in certain areas to enhance their overall journey.

reviews.text: Dorm has mold. Heater in dorm doesn’t work. Roommate selection is super haphazard. I was targeted violently by my ex roommate. Teacher in the Native American class is racist.

Overall, so thankful for my time here! I have found some of my absolute best friends, but I also had to grow a lot. I am so happy with my program being in elementary education and having amazing professors!

Academically, the business school curriculum and professors have exceeded expectations. Professors are committed to teaching students and setting them up for successful lives and careers. Dorm housing is alright, most of the dorms are very old and not well maintained, many people have maintenance issues.

The University of Colorado Boulder has allowed me to delve into experimental learning with classes focused on applying conceptual understandings to real-world problems. The learning environment is empowering.

I'm loving my experience at CU Boulder. Excellent facilities, services and academic support. The setting of campus at the base of the Colorado Rockies is awesome. The campus is beautiful, with a great lay out and is easy to navigate walking. The classrooms are very nice and well designed for comfort and well-lit and a welcoming vibe. The food available on campus is really good with a lot of variety of cuisines like American, Mexican, Mediterranean, Italian, and others available at several locations. I've had positive and pleasant interactions with the academic advisor, registrar, financial aid office, health and welfare office and other support offices. The teaching staff is excellent and accommodating. My residence hall is very nice with many safety features and my dorm room is comfortable and cozy. I highly recommend CU Boulder as a place for a great undergraduate experience.
I love the culture and the academics are very good. The campus life is good and it is easy to get involved.

I hope to pursue a career in nursing, a field that combines science, empathy, and service. I plan to begin my studies here by majoring in Integrative Physiology .Seeing how nurses comfort and heal people inspired me to follow a path where I can
support others physically and emotionally. I want to help patients feel seen and cared for, not just
treated. The strong physiology and biology programs at the University of California, Boulder, along
with its collaborative academic environment, will fully prepare me for my future nursing research. I
was also attracted by Boulder's beautiful campus and inclusive community. I believe that studying
here will give me the confidence and foundation to succeed in nursing. In the future, I hope to
become a registered nurse who can provide compassionate care and promote health in diverse
communities. CU Boulder will be the first step in turning that goal into reality.

I really enjoyed the feel of the campus. Where at first you feel intimidated but the more you are there the more it feels familiar and like a second home. While there are a lot of people as well, you can find your own community for the range of events they offer. Along with finding new friends with in different class you take, the same goes for clubs, sports, and just going out and doing things within school life.

My overall experience at the University of Colorado Boulder has been very positive. I really enjoy the strong sense of school spirit and community on campus, as well as the beautiful setting that makes being here feel energizing and inspiring. I’ve appreciated the range of academic opportunities and the supportive learning environment created by many professors and peers. There are also many ways to get involved on campus, which has helped me feel engaged and connected.
One thing I would like to see change is increased diversity on campus. As a predominantly white institution, CU Boulder has room to grow in creating a more diverse student body and ensuring that students from all backgrounds feel fully represented and included. Continued efforts to support diversity, equity, and inclusion would make the campus experience even stronger and more enriching for everyone.
Excellent undergrad and grad programs. Bvsd schools' college readiness will get you there. .......

The single most important thing CU Boulder has going for it is the city of Boulder itself. Without that backdrop, this university would struggle to attract the same student body. The location lets CU get away with far too much—outdated dorms, disorganized administration, poor advising, and ongoing safety concerns - including fire alarms on dorms that cannot be heard!
For out-of-state students especially, it’s simply not worth the cost. Put this university anywhere else, and it could never justify the price it charges. Very disappointing overall—there are too many issues to list.

I transferred out of CU Boulder after my first semester. CU Boulder remains the best decision in regards of growing socially and as a more interesting person. People here in STEM related fields care about what they are doing and are some of the most interesting people I have ever met. However, the reason I left is because the professors here across all ranges feel like they put in the bare minimum effort the majority of the time. It would take months, sometimes until 2 days before the final, for assignments to be graded and handed back. Every single building seemed to be under construction or not fitted for the size of the class held in it. Leeds is overfilled with students and the heating and cooling systems for the majority of buildings breaks down and takes weeks to fix. There simply is too many students here and money is the key reason for it. CU Boulder hires unqualified professors and then expects you to not learn so they get more money out of you retaking classes.

As a transfer student at the University of Colorado Boulder, I have truly found my home here. I am currently studying Marketing and Business Analytics in Leeds School of Business and am an active member of the Women's Club Soccer Team, Boulder Freeride, and the Women in Business Club. Outside of school, I enjoy hiking in the nearby mountains, skiing in the winter, working out at the campus rec center, and studying at the Moxy Hotel. One change I would love to see at Boulder would be a stronger emphasis on fostering connections among transfer students. Although I have found my place here, the transition was not easy. Adjusting to the new environment presented challenges, and I believe creating more structured opportunities for mentorship, collaboration, and inclusion within each specific school would help every student feel a stronger sense of belonging and community.

CU Boulder is an amazing school where I feel like I belong and fit in well. Although the greek life and parties can be a bit much, the academics are challenging but not stressful and I feel like my goals are supported. The resources and events, academic or not, are amazing. The food is great too.
Cheaper Housing, I would like for my University to be able to provide better housing with, with better transport

ai_summary: Reviews for the University of Colorado Denver highlight several key themes. Academically, the university is praised for its knowledgeable professors who are often noted for their industry experience and commitment to student success; however, some reviews mention inconsistencies in teaching quality across different departments. The campus environment is described as diverse and welcoming , making it a solid fit for students seeking inclusivity. While many appreciate the urban campus and its proximity to various resources in downtown Denver, the student life feels limited, particularly due to the commuter nature of the school, which leads to a lack of a traditional college experience with sports and social events. Tuition costs emerge as a common concern, with many students wishing for more financial aid opportunities and expressing frustration with the financial services’ responsiveness. Overall, CU Denver is seen as a place focused on education and professional preparation, particularly for those pursuing degrees in social sciences, business, and healthcare.

reviews.text: Experiencing my first year in college helped me understand why people say it requires determination and hard work to keep going. There were times when coursework felt overwhelming, but I learned that staying committed and not giving up makes the results worth it. Based off my experience in my first year of college, I'd say theres a lot of resources that many students don't know about so bringing more awareness to those online and campus resourcers would be helpful
University of Colorado Denver is an excellent choice of school for any student looking for a diverse, memorable, and overall supportive environment.

I love attending CU Denver, and I hope to continue if I can afford it. All the people and teachers are helpful and kind. I've learned a lot over the year and I'm getting closer to graduating everyday. I hope to learn even more and improve my skills so I can earn a degree and start my career with a head start. CU Denver helps me with this because it has plenty of resources for me to utilize and so many professors wanting to help and encourage success.

I’m choosing CU Denver to continue my educational path because they are famous for science majors, which contribute to the solid foundation for medical school. Therefore, experiences I have been through are meaningful.
I like the atmosphere of the setting it takes place at, near the home the broncos and rockies. Packed but pretty because of the Rocky Mountains hovering over the city.

It is a big campus shared with two other schools. The three schools usually have a lot of events in the main student common area (Tivoli) and any student with an ID can have access to the fairs/events.

As a bilingual early childhood educator and aspiring leader in family engagement, CU Denver has offered me more than just academic growth it’s been a space where my values and lived experience are honored. I’m currently pursuing my Bachelor’s in Early Childhood Education: Human Development, and I’ve found the coursework deeply relevant to my work with toddlers and families, especially in inclusive, multilingual settings.

My overall experience with the University of Colorado Denver has been both challenging and rewarding. As a first-generation student, I entered college unsure of what to expect, but CU Denver quickly became a place where I felt supported and motivated to succeed. The professors have pushed me to think critically and apply what I learn beyond the classroom, while also being approachable and willing to guide me when I needed help.
What stands out most about CU Denver is its diversity and inclusive community. Being surrounded by students from so many different backgrounds has broadened my perspective and made me feel that my own experiences are valued. I have also appreciated the flexibility of programs and resources that make it possible to balance school with my personal responsibilities.
I love this school so much! The campus is so pretty and there are so many different kinds of people to get to know!

Transferring from another college, that was supposed to be more affordable, to CU Denver has been the best choice I have ever made in my academic journey. Other than books and transportation, I did not have to spend a single dollar at CU which has given me the opportunity to explore different interests without worrying about payment.

As an older returning/transfer student, I have different priorities than I did as a new college student. My focus is on academics and courses that will best prepare me for entering industry. I think the career fairs are difficult for students who don't have extensive background in *utilizing* career fairs, ie. first-generation students who have no experience appearing "professional" -- I think coursework or mentorship in this area would do wonders.

I am a first year student at the University of Colorado Denver, but I was involved in two programs throughout high school. I was in the pre-collegiate program, and the scholars program. Both of the programs included monthly meetings, events, and 5-week summer academies with the staff and other students from the same grade. The TA's and professors teaching at the summer academies provided guidance, and supported students to help them succeed in their classes. Aside from the programs, I'm on my first week of classes! Everyone is welcoming; there are "JAM (just ask me) tents" to help students guide themselves through campus, and to answer any questions students might have. Overall, I love how UC Denver shows up for their students, whether it's through their support system, or the amount of activities, and events held over the year; it shows that they want their students to succeed. Although, one thing I would like to see change would be the food. There aren't many options to choose from.

The University of Colorado Denver is more of a commuter school so there wasn't much campus involvement as most other universities. Overall, I felt the academics were good I liked most of the professors and felt the education was worthwhile. The campus is also shared with two other schools which I thought was a unique feature of the school. I also really liked the location as it was super close to downtown (I didn't have a car my first year and felt I had everything I needed within walking distance).

This school is mainly a commuter school with very little campus life. There is one dorm and one apartment style housing and they are currently building another dorm. If you are looking to simply take classes and get a degree, this school could work for you. However, if you would like to be involved in the school, make a lot of friends, and have a campus life this isn’t the school for that. Some of the professors are great and some of the professors just aren’t the best—unable to explain concepts, bad lecturers, etc.—but I believe that could be true for any school. The location is great if you like city life! Overall, the college experience is what you make of it, so just make informed decisions and you’ll be fine!

My experience at the University of Colorado Denver has been very good. The professors I have had have been truly amazing, and have shown that they care for the education of their students. The campus is very safe as well, and the diversity makes the school feel very comforting as a whole.

ai_summary: At the University of Colorado - Colorado Springs (UCCS), students appreciate the strong academic focus and dedicated professors , particularly in STEM and health sciences, who provide valuable resources and support. Many reviews noted the campus's beautiful setting and accessibility, though some students found the social scene lacking , mentioning that it can feel disconnected as a predominantly commuter school. While there are opportunities for clubs and events, the frequency and engagement level of these offerings left some wanting more. Additionally, parking issues were frequently mentioned as a point of concern. Overall, UCCS is recognized for its welcoming community, smaller class sizes fostering closer student-faculty relationships, and the support available for both academic success and personal growth. However, some students expressed that tuition can be high, which may limit its attractiveness to those seeking affordability.

reviews.text: Overall the experience was good. The student life on campus isn't very strong, but it was started as more of a commuter school, so thats to be expected. Most of the professors from College of Business were great! But the ones that were bad were really bad.
My experience at UCCS has been pretty great. With an amazing staff and beautiful scenery it offer so much opprotunity.

Because I was in a very specialized program, it made us a tight-knit group, which was an enormous support for each of us throughout our courses since we all took the same courses in the program. UCCS is located in a fairly conservative area, so that can be challenging in the greater environment for those who are not so inclined.

UCCS is generally a good commuter school for those in the area who were looking to gain an education close to home for a relatively inexpensive price compared to other universities in the area. However, the lack of robust student support and generally poor employment prospects certainly make my perspective on it quite mixed. I hope that in the future the administration will take employment placing and student opportunities more seriously.

Uccs has great accessibility for students in high school who are interested in learning college credit while earning high school credits. This is a great way to get ahead in your college journey. I liked the smooth transition of my grades from my high school transcript into my college transcript.

The University of Colorado Colorado Springs has one of the kindest staff of educators I have ever come across. The English department is full of bright, understanding, and interested educators who really care about the content they are teaching. As a commuter student, I never felt cut off from the community.

I have loved it here at UCCS, I love the professors and variety of classes and majors. The campus is absolutely beautiful! The only issue I would say is the parking, a lot of class times are the same so it's either really hard to find parking or its a mess to try to get in and out of.

I transferred to UCCS from a local community college. From the beginning of my time at UCCS to graduating, I felt like I was fully supported and set up with the tools I needed for my success. The administration office was extremely helpful with helping me navigate which classes to take. I had an advisor that I met with each semester who helped me decide which classes to take for my degree audit. My classes were not overly packed, I felt like fellow classmates shared the same effort to learn which made group projects enjoyable where applicable. My professors were engaged and easy to reach, willing to help in anyway they could. I've decided to apply for my master's in clinical mental health counseling at UCCS due to my initial experience while I was there completing my undergraduate degree. I have full faith in this university and education they offer for my future endeavors and road to success.

Beautiful campus in a pretty good location and a good amount of programs to pick from. However, knowing most of my professors were just Pikes Peak community college profs I just came to a realization why am I spending 3-4 times more to go to UCCS when the education level I'm getting is the same exact as a community college that's way more cheaper and I could just be going there instead. Overall, certain programs like the medical programs are definitely worth it and others are just better to do somewhere else for way less money.

UCCS was an excellent learning environment. I was a commuter, and I enjoyed the balance of in-person and online courses available. I was able to complete my entire MBA online during COVID which was amazing and has jump-started my career. The professors care about you and your success, smaller class sizes mean each student gets personalized attention and you are not a face in the crowd.
UCCS, so far, has been really well to me. The classes are engaging/enjoyable and everyone in student life are active in trying to get students involved.

I am a transfer student who recently enrolled in my first semester at UCCS. As such, my experience with the college is somewhat limited currently, however what I can say is that at least for the program I've entered (that being the music program), the professors are great. The music specific classes so far are all very interesting and taught very well. As for a sense of community, I feel the dust kicked up by the beginning of a new semester beginning to settle, and in the short time I've been here, I have been able to make at least a couple connections. The process of enrollment for me had a number of issues, whether it be missing materials that weren't actually missing or some details of my class levels or tuition being off. It was very stressful for me, although I know some of it may be of my own doing. At the end of the day, (especially from the music department, I can't stress this enough) I received plenty of help to assist me in getting started properly.

Something that I really like about University of Colorado at Colorado Springs is that all of my professors seem to genuinely care about me as a person. I have felt very welcome in every classroom I’ve stepped in.

I'm a new student here at UCCS and I am starting in the fall. I'm transferring from PPSC, so it's definitely a bit different. I'm still not used to Canvas and navigating things online is difficult. They also have a housing requirement for first year students which is definitely annoying because I have to sign an exemption form for it. It's also difficult to schedule advising appointments too. I just hope the classes are better.
the staff and personnel on campus are really committed to their jobs and goals of getting students to feel connected to UCCS and fostering community

ai_summary: The University of Connecticut offers a vibrant campus life that combines strong academic programs with numerous extracurricular opportunities. Students appreciate the diversity of classes and the generally helpful faculty, although there are some concerns about large class sizes making personal connections with professors more challenging. The sense of community on campus is often highlighted, with many students noting the friendly environment and the availability of various clubs and activities. However, critiques focus on the rural setting, which limits off-campus entertainment options, and some dissatisfaction with housing quality and dining variety . School spirit, especially around sports, is highly valued, but there are calls for more diversity among the student body and faculty to enhance the sense of belonging for all students. Overall, UConn presents an enriching experience, though continuous improvements in certain areas are desired.

reviews.text: UConn Storrs is a great place to be. The campus is beautiful and always full of energy, especially on game days. Cheering on the Huskies alongside thousands of passionate fans is an experience like no other. There's always something to do, from exciting sports events to clubs and activities. The professors care, the people are motivated, and the crowd is electric. I honestly can not wait to make more memories here!

U Conn is great. The food was uneven at different cafeterias but in all cases much better than at other schools. My biggest complaint were the dorms. There are just way too many triples and honestly triples are awful for student learning. It is inevitable you will have some roommate who is up gaming all night. The library could also be loud which was frustrating. Those are my only complaints (besides the wind). The support for students with disabilities was OUTSTANDING, I had so much more support than any other high school classmate. The classes were rigorous but there was so much support, tutoring centers, TAs, study aids, just endless. There is even support if your computer breaks down- they have computer support and will lend you one- what public school does that? Plus of course the sports, the recreation facilities and the school spirit.

Overall a great school with many opportunities to find something you’re interested regardless of what it may be. There are great communities for involvement, and for education. Plenty of research opportunities.
There is a lot more than great basketball teams, but the place is electric during basketball season.

My experience at the University of Connecticut has been great overall. I appreciate the supportive professors, variety of resources, and opportunities for involvement. Large classes and some confusing administrative processes could improve, but I’ve grown a lot academically and personally here.
Couldn't imagine myself anywhere else! Super big school with huge student culture but there are so many smaller groups to get a more tight knit community!

My experience at the University of Connecticut was pretty good. I got a lot of access to mental health resources, along with a lot of help, and lots of fun events. I like the classes and the professors are nice.
UConn was a better fit for me than I expected. I like the academic programs offered and the opportunities to get support. I would like to see more effort put into student housing.

I really think my experience can be better if I lived on campus which I will do for my final year as living off campus is boring and a hassle with the driving and moving in/out. I would like to see easier transportation to down-town. I would also love it for there to cheaper rates for parking.

I love the University of Connecticut. They provide you with so many opportunities to build and enhance your career, meet new people, get involved with organizations on campus, and more. I have met so many amazing, helpful people at this school and I am excited to see where it will lead me.

The University of Connecticut is such a great place to be! It’s vibrant and enjoyable, with plenty of opportunities to grow academically. You’ll find plenty of opportunities to explore through research, work, hands-on projects, and building resumes that really catch the eye of employers. Plus, there are lots of clubs and extracurricular activities to choose from, making your experience truly memorable. UConn blends respected academics and research opportunities with a vibrant campus life and strong community spirit, which can help you grow both personally and professionally.

Great school with so many on-campus opportunities to get involved. Amazing campus life with thriving sports, Greek life, and campus clubs. Great facilities such as the business school, student union, rec center, and certain dorms like South, Connecticut Hall, and Werth. If you make the most of your time on campus, you will 100% get a lot out of UConn, but if you fail to apply yourself and get involved, you will feel isolated and lost in a large student body. Overall, a great experience so far.

UCONN!!! As an out of state student, I feel like people underrate UConn. It is a traditional college experience!! It's incredible. The people are so nice, greek life is so welcoming, and it's easy to get involved! Such a great place and so proud to be a Husky!!!
I love my college, I feel like I belong here and I am constantly trying new things. The Husky spirit is absolutely unmatched, and I have excelled in every class I've taken.

My overall experience at the University of Connecticut has been positive. Some of the complaints I have about the school relate to the distance between classes and the cost of attendance. The main positives are the academics, athletics, party scene, and food.

ai_summary: The University of Dayton is frequently noted for its strong sense of community and welcoming atmosphere , with many students emphasizing how friendly and supportive their peers and faculty are. The campus life is vibrant, featuring a range of activities and events, and students appreciate the smaller class sizes that foster close relationships with professors. However, academics can be challenging, and some students have voiced concerns about difficulties with course registration, particularly for popular classes. While the university provides significant financial aid opportunities , tuition costs are often cited as high. Overall, UD is described as a place where students can thrive both socially and academically, although some international students have expressed feelings of being unsupported.

reviews.text: *Read before deciding to go to Dayton* I am a senior here at UD and for the most part I have had a great time. Unfortunately, the Dayton that students are attending now is not the Dayton of 10 years ago. The "community" that the school loves to promote as a main selling point is dying. The beauty in UD was that students felt like they were always welcome no matter who or where they were. In my opinion administration has strategically shifted their focus from fostering a special environment to trying to fit in with every other midwestern school. Administration has made a point to limit weekend life. They shut down student bands and disrupt weekend life. As of next year athletes will get priority to prime lowes housing as an attempt to even further kill what fun that street once was. I'm not even a huge partier but the happy neighborhood vibe is fading away.
Best school,,,let it be your choice over Dayton university were education build's a better career for the betterment of tomorrow.
Everything about you UD is what I wanted in a college, it has a big college atmosphere with a small college connection to others which is a nice blend.

I really like the University of Dayton. It offers great opportunities for academics, degrees, and other areas to just fit in. Athletics at UD whether it is official sports or just intramural sports, is so welcoming and so much fun to either play in or be a part of. Some things I would like to potentially see change is the safety of campus. There was one point within the fall semester at UD where there was a shooting at a university house, and a student who committed suicide. I just wish that the options for better safety or better resources were there. Because I have been in a bad mental state before, and when I tried to reach out for help at the student health center and meet with a psychologist, I had to attend 3 stupid zoom meetings before I could get matched with a personal psychologist to meet with regularly. People don't want to do that; that's like more homework. And even I didn't do that. I did 2 of the 3 zoom meetings and then quit and realized that this isn't worth it.

University of Dayton is an institution that so clearly cares about their students. Their resources are endless (and free!) and if you’re struggling at all so many people are there and willing to listen. The professors have been so kind and have shown so much care for me and my academics. Everyone is so friendly, they don’t make that up. Everyone holds the door, waves hello, asks about your day, and smiles as you walk past. I have struggled making friends, but I figure that would apply to me at any campus. Dayton’s counseling and my advisor have helped me work through it and joining organizations has been so easy!

I enjoyed all parts of my University of Dayton experience. One factor that some students may forget about is the exceptional Dayton Flyers basketball program. Those athletes are the "heroes" of the region and they continuously have spectators in the arena for every game.

I love the professors, all of mine have PHD’s, and they care a lot about the students. Downtown Dayton has a few nice locations, but it’s not great. My advice is to drive down to Centerville, the Greene, or the beavercreek mall (Round 1) as those will provide a better experience and better entertainment.

University of Dayton offers a vibrant academic environment with strong programs in engineering, business, and arts & sciences. Students benefit from hands on learning, research opportunities, and a tight knit community feel. Campus life includes diverse student organizations and a commitment to social justice and service. Overall, it's a nurturing place for personal and academic growth with a strong Marianist spirit.

When I first started at the University of Dayton I was terribly homesick and having a hard time adjusting to college life, but I ended up having very supportive teachers and staff that got me the help I needed to thrive. During my time I found that not only were my teachers supportive of me, but the overall staff was too. There was always help and support around, not just for academic but emotional and social support too. Through the COVID-19 pandemic and many personal issues, I was able to thrive and find my passions.

The University of Dayton has been home for me. It provides me with several opportunities to explore things outside of my channeled education and create new experiences as well as share ideas. The school is a private school however it does not push this on others which is nice allowing people to show up as their true and authentic selves. The change I would like to see is not from a school level but as a community as a whole where people are free to be themselves and share ideas without judgement but to simply grow.
The University of Dayton has been amazing in providing resources and communication constantly throughout my time here.

I have really enjoyed my time at Dayton so far. Even though freshman year I didn't branch out as much as I think I should've, I still found some friends, joined a sorority, and had new experiences. I'm excited to have more next year! There is so much to do and you don't need to rush it there.

The University of Dayton has become home. This school has molded me and helped me become the person I didn't even know I was striving to be. The community has offered me support and a life time of memories — I will forever look back at my undergraduate years and smile. The academics have pushed me to become successful, motivated, and bold. I have nothing but good words and memories for the University of Dayton.

UD is filled with a ton of activities to keep students connected like intramural sports, tons of clubs, and plenty of areas to meet or hangout with friends. Plenty of professors are involved with their students and are helpful if you are able to ask questions. A small downside is that the city of Dayton is a bit scary. There is a hand full of homeless outside university grounds and some neighboring areas are quite shady.

The University of Dayton has provided me with lifelong friends, unforgettable memories, and has truly shaped the person I am today. From late night study sessions and campus events to service opportunities and meaningful conversations, my time at UD has been filled with experiences that I will carry with me for the rest of my life. The supportive community, dedicated faculty, and strong sense of purpose have not only helped me grow academically and professionally but also personally. I’m incredibly grateful for the relationships I've built and the values I've learned during my time here.

ai_summary: At the University of Delaware, students often praise the wide range of academic opportunities and strong professor support , particularly in programs like engineering and business, which have solid reputations. The campus itself is described as beautiful and well-maintained, with a mix of historic and modern architecture, enhancing the overall student experience. However, some reviews note challenges with connecting to campus life, especially for freshmen, and while many enjoy the local dining options and vibrant student organizations , others express concerns about the quality of food and housing. Additionally, critiques about mental health resources and campus safety were mentioned, indicating room for improvement. Socially, the university is known for its Greek life, though some feel it can overshadow other activities. Overall, while the University of Delaware offers a welcoming environment and a community spirit, experiences can vary significantly based on individual preferences and expectations.

reviews.text: My experience with UD has been up and down. At first, it was really daunting to find my way through such a big campus. Finding friends however, was easy. There is truly a place for everyone at UD if you look around for it. It has allowed me to find my friends for life. It allowed me to learn so many diifferent things, and also to find my passions in my Wildlife Conservation and Ecology major. I have discovered internships and gotten them thanks to recourses UD has provided for me. I am so thankful. The worst thing about UD is probably being a person of color, LGBTQ, or other minority groups. The school is prodominantly white, straight, and conservative men have done hurtful, harassing behaviors. This however, can happen in any University. I feel overall safe and protected on campus grounds, but there are always bad apples. The school is very expensive to attend, but mostly for valid reaons. Their parking security is overwhelming and can be invasive and disrespectful towards students.

Great! I loved the format for the psychology courses I took for my minor. I took many exams through the local prometric center in Newark, DE. I would like to see career services change. I am currently unemployed since February 2024.

My overall experience at the University of Delaware has been very positive. The campus has a great atmosphere and there are always activities, clubs, and events happening that help students get involved. I especially like how easy it is to meet people and build a community, whether through classes, organizations, or just spending time on the Green. The campus itself is walkable, but biking’s definitely more convenient (just avoid times between classes, when there are masses of people, because then biking through is pretty inconvenient). Academically, many professors are knowledgeable, passionate about their teaching, and always accommodating if you are on a varsity team and travel a lot. When you put in the effort, you can get a lot out of the classes and learn useful skills. There are also good resources available for students, such as tutoring and career support.

I am a student athlete at the University of Delaware and can confidently say that this is an amazing school with great social scenes and also so many opportunities to grow as a person and towards a career. Being a student athlete on the womens lacrosse team has defineatly separated my personal experience from that of the general student body. The athletics department is something I am extremely grateful to have that resource wheter it be for career services, academic advising, or general support.
Campus is beautiful and the homey feeling is what initially drew me to UD in the first place. My favorite thing about it is main street where all of my favorite restaurants are and places to study. The social scene here is also super lively and fun.
UD is a great school with challenging courses and labs. I’ve enjoyed my time here and would do it all over again.
I like easily being able to find clubs I am interested in. The Caesar Rodney dining hall is not great for picky eaters like myself.

As a current freshman, at University of Delaware I love the experience of meeting different people from different backgrounds, celebrating other cultures, and sharing ideas with others. University of Delaware does a great job with their academics by providing free tutoring, office hours, and resources that are accessible. I have made a lot of friends that came from different clubs and have gained a bunch of knowledge that is useful for the real-world. I am a biochemistry major that seeks to achieve becoming a lab technician one day in the future. The University of Delaware has given me the opportunity to seek this goal by attending college. The Biochemistry and chemistry department offers a lot of resources to help us succeed. These resources are what I like about what University of Delaware has. In my college, I would like for the University of Delaware to maintain important research about history from disappearing. I would like the University to protect this important information.
Overall I loved the University of Delaware. It truly set me up for success in the Delaware teaching world as well as taught me so many lessons on becoming an adult.
I love the classes and professors! Im in the honors college and had a great experience. I also went random with my roommate and love her!

Overall, University of Delaware is a good school. Its strongest aspects are in academics and supporting resources. The University provides students with free access to the tutoring center, career center, and counseling center. I recommend for students take full advantage of these resources. It also has a good selection of on-campus and off-campus food. Weaker aspects are expensive housing and a loud/busy environment. There is also a pretty big party culture which can either be a positive or negative depending on what you are looking for.

The University of Delaware has provided me an outstanding, positive college experience, as I am able to maintain my academics alongside extracurricular activities and spending time with my newfound friends.

I really love the University of Delaware. The campus is beautiful and has such a welcoming feel, which makes it a great place to live and learn. Academics here are definitely rigorous, but they are manageable if you stay organized and put in the effort. Professors expect a lot, but there are plenty of resources to help students succeed. The food is okay overall, but it has been improving, especially now that the renovated Trabant Student Center is open. Trabant has become a major hotspot on campus, and it’s always lively. One of my favorite things about UD is how active campus life is; there are so many school events happening every week, so it’s easy to get involved, meet new people, and feel part of the community.

I truly love the University of Delaware and my experience here! The professors in my major (kinesiology) are incredibly knowledgeable, supportive, and genuinely invested in their students’ success. UD also offers countless opportunities for internships and research, allowing students to gain meaningful, hands-on experience. In addition, with over 400 registered student organizations, there is no shortage of ways to get involved. There truly is something for everyone, and I believe anyone can find a strong sense of community at UD!

Party scene for women is great, but for men, you HAVE to rush a fraternity otherwise you won’t be allowed into any parties. Math professors have a reputation to lack in their teaching skills, leading to many students struggling (especially in higher level courses). No one really cares about sports here, and our teams aren’t great.
I liked my classes and my professors, everyone on campus is very welcoming. There are many ways to get involved which is nice. I just wish the dorms and food was better.

ai_summary: At the University of Denver, many students appreciate the small class sizes and dedicated professors who offer personalized support, creating a strong academic environment. The campus is frequently described as beautiful and well-located , providing access to both city and nature. However, some students express frustration with diversity, feeling that the student body lacks representation and inclusivity. While there's a noted vibrant student life with various activities and sports, particularly hockey, critiques point to the quality of campus food and dining options. Additionally, the administration faces criticism for communication issues and the handling of financial aid. Overall, students seem to find value in their education and community, though there are significant areas they hope to see improved.

reviews.text: A very beautiful campus. I loved almost all of my professors and they have been so supportive and helpful in my academics and graduate school applications. I wish it was more diverse, I guess it depends on the classes.

My experience with the University of Denver has been very positive overall. I like the welcoming campus environment, the smaller class sizes, and how approachable professors and staff are. DU feels supportive and focused on helping students succeed both academically and personally. I also enjoy the campus location, which offers a good balance between city life and a close-knit community. One change I would like to see is more affordable housing and dining options for students. Expanding academic support resources and increasing hands-on learning opportunities would also be great. Overall, DU feels like a place where students are encouraged to grow and explore their interests.
The University of Denver is a pretty good school from my experience so far, but I will say it is rather expensive, and I'm not sure if the price is worth it.
Overall, I had a positive experience with the University of Denver so far, with many supportive professors who help you stay engaged with classes and great people on campus.
I enjoyed the community and location. Something that everyone wants to change is the food situation because it is through Sodexo and is usually inedible.

As a current sophomore, I have had a generally positive experience here at DU. The size of school and the average level of involvement create an interconnected community and a fairly strong sense of purpose on campus. As an INTS major at Korbel, I feel particularly confident in my education as I believe I am learning from the best of their fields and I believe that I am being well prepared for a career in politics/international relations despite the uncertainty in the current geopolitical climate. I personally received a large deal of need based and merit based financial aid to mitigate the high cost of attendance to something manageable.

DU is a very wonderful community to be a part of. I truly have loved my time here I believe that it is a very balanced school on a gorgeous campus. The student life could be better it is not a party school if thats what you are looking for but it still has really fun events and lots of things that are available to all students easily because it is a smaller school. 100% would recommend at least applying and coming to see DU as it really is a very amazing balanced university.
While visiting the campus, overall there was just so much to do and everything was open. The athletics are amazing and the overall vibe is good
The application process is simple and the form easy to complete. Staff are available and attentive to candidates. Information is accessible at all levels.
The University of Denver (DU) campus nis surrounded by the City and County of Denver neighborhood and provides a fun city campus life and top notch academics. Enjoying my college experience so far.

I've really enjoyed my experience at the University of Denver. It has offered me a wonderful community and I am so grateful for everyone here - the students, the professors. Everyone cares about your success.

The University of Denver graduate programs are very welcoming and built on community. The diversity and different points of views are all taken and everyone has something special to offer. All voices are heard and professors learn from us as much as we learn from them.
I am just starting, but this school is extremely well-rounded and there is so much room for student development and growth. I am excited to earn my education here.

The University of Denver has given me a great experience. It's diverse community as well as its structure has made me feel like I was at home. It was very creative with including students to celebrate its culture as well as respecting the history that occured before the university was created. I feel a sense of worth and I'm able to connect with my resources very easily.

Attended during covid years - I know they have made some advancements since, however, the party scene is subpar. The only academics that are useful are when you reach advanced classes that are within departmental honors programs. Most students don't have the drive to succeed and are usually "just mailing it in." This is a tuition grab school, borderline highway robbery if you don't have a scholarship to attend.

ai_summary: At the University of Detroit Mercy, students appreciate the small class sizes and the close-knit community that facilitates strong connections with professors and peers, many of whom are described as supportive and dedicated to student success. Academically, the university is noted for its rigorous programs , particularly in nursing and engineering, which prepare students well for their careers or graduate school. However, students also express concerns about high tuition costs and the quality of campus food. While the campus environment is often described as welcoming and inclusive , some reviews highlight limitations in student life and activities, noting that the social scene can be quieter compared to larger universities. Overall, UDM is regarded as a good choice for students who value personalized attention and strong academic foundations, despite some areas needing improvement.

reviews.text: The academic buildings and facilities are great, always clean, plenty of places to relax and study. I would like to see more options for food as a student living on campus

My overall experience with the University of Detroit Mercy has been a mix of positive support and areas for improvement. My campus visit was excellent; the staff were friendly and truly helpful, giving me a clear picture of the academic environment. The admissions process for the Graduate program (Fall 2026) was also quite smooth and the support with credit transfer was a major plus for an international applicant.
What I liked: The clarity of the admissions and credit transfer process.
What I'd like to see change: While I received an admission letter with a minor amount /academic year scholarship, it was exclusively for the Undergraduate level. I was disappointed and surprised that this scholarship was not applicable to my desired Graduate program. I strongly urge the university to increase financial aid and scholarship options specifically for Graduate students to make advanced degrees more accessible and competitive.
I have enjoyed the community I built here, I have met people all over the world, from differernt majors, and even different backgrounds.

I enjoy that university of Detroit Mercy is near downtown Detroit, where I like to spend time going out and trying new restaurants. I also enjoy how small the classes are because everybody gets to know each other. Each professor I have had so far also really cares for each and every student and ensure we are all successful.
Love the school, Professors, and students. The food is the only thing that I don't like. Although, Detroit mercy is one of the very few Universities to have Five-year programs

Food was horrible! I’m a highschool student who came here to tour and check out the university along with other colleges near the area.
Did not taste good

As a student at the University of Detroit Mercy, I appreciate the small class sizes and the personal attention from professors who genuinely care about our success. The curriculum is challenging but prepared me well for my career. Campus life is quieter since many students commute, but there are still clubs and activities to get involved in. The tuition is high, but scholarships and financial aid help. The location offers internship opportunities, though safety can be a concern in some areas. Overall, it’s a solid university with a strong academic focus. The only thing I would like to see change is the safeness of the surrounding area.
I like the academic programs. I moved off of campus because living on campus was not a great experience due to the food and lack of activities to do on weekends. I enjoy my peers and professors.
As a university of detroit mercy student, so far this past months it has been a good ride and also a stressful on but God saw me through it and he will continue to.

I've really enjoyed my time at UDM. It's a wonderful school with great professors and people are always willing to help you out. I love the beautiful campus and being at a relatively small college is definitely an experience that I would suggest.

What I like most about Detroit mercy is the diversity we have. You get to meet many people with different ethnicities and backgrounds. It really helps broaden your perspective and feel more compassion towards others. Apart from the diversity, I also like the academics part of it. I feel like it’s definitely challenging, but it really helps bring out the most and best out of you. Seeing how far you grow and come makes you feel successful and happy. I’m not sure what I’d like to see change. Maybe something could be the events hosted on campus. Making more people aware of them. I feel like as a commuter, sometimes we aren’t made aware of certain events, but we would like to be. Overall, this is a great university and I would definitely recommend it!
I will start taking classes in January. I am so excited to be returning to college. I am happy to get excepted for the opportunity to get my masters degree in psychology.

Detroit Mercy is definitely a great University for STEM major students. There are several accelerated programs like the 7-Year Dentistry and 5-Year Physician Assistant program, that can help you get ahead. The education and professors are great and genuinely want you to succeed. They do a great job at being there for students. The social life on campus is not as great though but we are near Downtown Detroit and Ferndale which always have fun activities going on!
University of detroit mercy professors all strive to guide you and see you succeed. The environment is unmatched and the course work helped stregthen many of my weakness
The school environment, from professors and academics to infrastructure and facilities. I have not had a poor experience at the school that can in any way be attributed to the institution itself.

ai_summary: The University of Findlay offers a close-knit campus atmosphere where students appreciate small class sizes and the ability to form personal relationships with professors , who are often described as supportive and dedicated to student success. Many students enjoy hands-on experiences , especially in animal science and health-related programs, which are frequently highlighted as strengths of the university. However, some students express concerns about the lack of nightlife and extracurricular activities in the surrounding town, leading to a quieter social scene . The dining options receive mixed reviews, with some finding the food lacking. Additionally, while the campus environment is considered safe and welcoming, some students have noted that the tuition can be high and that more resources for international students and diversity initiatives would be beneficial. Overall, those who thrive in small, personalized educational settings may find the University of Findlay to be a fitting choice.

reviews.text: The University of Findlay is a decent university, and your experience depends heavily on your major. If you are a Pre-Vet, Pharmacy, or Physical Therapy major, you'll have done just fine. Anything else? It depends. While I had a good overall experience, the constant turnover of faculty in my specific major was difficult to work around. The faculty, other than that, are amazing, and I truly do appreciate them for everything. The school definitely needs some things figured out, but part of that is private school dramas.
Professors did a good job of communicating clear expectations. Friendly campus and in a great location with a lot of activities nearby including on-campus.

The school is good, but there isn’t much social life unless you are part of a popular group. The town is also nice, but almost everything closes at 10 pm, so there's not much for night owls to do. Overall I like it here and I am glad I chose this school.

Although most of the professors go above and beyond for the university, the administrative decisions that are being made seem to be benefitting pockets rather than the students. Funds that were allocated by the professors for things like lab equipment, supplies, and research are now gone and the administration is not being forthcoming as to where this money went to in the budget.
The University of Findlay did a wonderful job of making everything feel very at home and safe. If you were on campus you felt included and like you fit in no matter what.

The University of Findlay is a great college, but if you're looking for a party school, I would say it doesn't fit that criterion. I personally enjoy the quiet there is during the weekend since I am an introvert.

I am part of the English Equestrian Program. Love the trainers, they push you to make you better. Many levels of horse to learn on. Opportunity to work in the barn and meet the farriers and vets. The learning never ends.

It's a pretty small University so you have a better chance of knowing more people, and I feel like, with the exception of some, professors and advisors actually tried to help you and were better able to help you since there were less students admitted than at larger universities.

College is going broke. Do not attend. I don’t imagine they’ll be running much longer. Students are getting egregious fees added to their accounts, including athletes. We were going to merge with Bluffton University but we can’t afford to take on that debt.
Quality of housing is poor. Dealt with bug infestations and hazardous electrical issues. Again, people are getting slapped with new huge [summer] housing fees with little to no time to pay them off.
Academics are great depending on major. Departments are small but you can really connect with faculty in your field.
Budget cuts are rampant, however. Faculty are getting cut and club budgets are slashed. Heard about a club who went from a 10k budget to a 2k budget per semester.
Student working hours are getting cut and departments are getting consolidated to reduce the amount UF needs to spend on them.
DO NOT GO HERE. Even the equine program, which is supposedly our best one, is terrible as well and the barn conditions are inhumane.

There is a very small community that allows for a more comfortable college experience. No matter where you are on campus, it will be less than a ten minute walk to your next destination, which is extremely helpful when classes are back to back. Everyone is pretty friendly and most teachers want to see their students exceed in their academics.

I have enjoyed my time as a student at the University of Findlay as I have learned a lot under many great professors. However, coming from the big public school of Bowling Green State University to the small private school of Findlay was not a fun transition for me as I feel I have less opportunity and people to network with.

The dining hall isn’t great. The food is edible, but there’s few options at stations and some of them will just be randomly closed. Campus life is okay. A majority of events happened during the week though, so campus tends to be dead on the weekends because everyone goes home.
schools great for someone who's not ready for a large school. Professors care about the students and there are many ways to get help academically

The University of Findlay is a private college on the small side. Findlay, Ohio is better than the surrounding areas but is still lack luster when it comes to entertainment, night life, and other forms of enjoyment. However, the academics and Professors at The University of Findlay are great!

I really enjoy the professors and the environment of the school. I really enjoy being at UF, but the only thing I would change is cheaper tuition and overall lower costs. I would also like to recommend more parking spaces for residents. I feel like there are too many commuter spots and not enough resident spots.

ai_summary: The University of Florida offers a vibrant campus life with a strong sense of community , boosted by active Greek life and numerous extracurricular activities. Students appreciate the diverse population and the welcoming atmosphere , although some find the prominence of drinking culture off-putting if they prefer quieter activities. Academically, UF is well-regarded, with challenging courses and dedicated professors who genuinely care about student success, though some students note a few professors may not be as engaged. The campus has extensive resources for academic support and personal well-being, like the "You Matter We Care" initiative. While the campus is beautiful, students frequently cite parking availability as an issue. Overall, UF is seen as a place where students can find their niche and thrive, surrounded by a competitive yet supportive environment.

reviews.text: Overall I enjoyed my experience at UF due to the friends I made. I will say UF was sort of diverse but not as much as I would have liked it. It is easy to find communities to get involved with.
The diversity of everything was quite nice and I enjoyed my experience there. I made good connections and the professors were understandable.

I liked the community here and how hardworking and disciplined everything is while also being able to make time for fun. I really enjoy all the athletic events on campus as well as the well connected alumni association and professional and research opportunities. I wish the quality of food was better on campus.

I am enjoying my experience with the University of Florida, particularly for the campus atmosphere. Despite the rigour of everyone's coursework, there is almost always something new, exciting, and student-run happening on campus to lift everyone's spirits and encourage us to keep pursuing greatness. My only major complaint about the school is its bus schedule, as many buses have been cut, making getting to and from campus, or even just getting around campus increasingly challenging. Additionally, with late-in-the-day exams, these cut bus schedules make it difficult for students who live off-campus to guarantee that they have a safe means of getting home.
I started my undergrad at UF during COVID, which led to an unusual first year. However, I had a great experience overall, especially my junior and senior year.
I love this school and I am having the best time. It is so well-rounded, and everyone has such a good balance of academic and social lives.
As a new freshman, it has been a great experience overall. The communication from both the school and professors is clear.
Great academics, very clear professors are passionate about what they teach. Lots of clubs/orgs available to join, makes finding community easier.
It’s not what everybody makes it out to be. The classes will challenge you. The people aren’t the best, it feels like a constant competition. This makes it hard to even chat with someone.
UF is an perfect option for someone looking for a university with athletics, academics, and and a social. Additionally, it is a top-notch, highly respected school and extremely affordable.

If I'm being honest it is a really great school that i'm thankful for getting into, but the whole campus and scenery, and people makes it to where i'm lonely. I have not fit into one group yet so its not that fun of an experience going there for me. If I were to win this scholarship money it would be a load off of my family's back making the experience even a bit more enjoyable.

Enjoyed the courses I took here for my major and the class environment. I had very helpful professors that helped me in my professional goals. I also found a great group of friends and very welcoming environment. Enjoyed the campus and weather since it is warm all the year. There is only limited things to do in Gainesville.

It was cool, nice place. Middle of no where but it is quite in the city of Gainesville. I never thought I would be able to actually make it here but I worked hard and got accepted. This was a great decision by me to decide to come here.

After spending my first undergraduate semester here at the University of Florida, I can say with full confidence I am having an absolutely astounding college experience. Every professor has been lovely and all my classes have invested me in learning.

The sheer size and amount of students was a big adjustment, but the connections that they have to offer are immeasurable. Even though I'm one semester in, I've already made connections with several professors to help further my career interests who are more than eager to help. The student government situation and safety on campus are probably the main drawbacks.

ai_summary: The University of Georgia offers a vibrant campus life characterized by strong school spirit , a rich array of clubs and organizations, and notable athletic traditions , particularly in football. Academically, students appreciate challenging courses and supportive professors who genuinely care about their success, despite some concerns about large class sizes and specific teaching methods. Many reviews highlight the welcoming atmosphere and sense of community, with numerous opportunities for social networking and personal growth. However, common frustrations include the limited parking availability , the high cost of tuition, and some perceptions of a lack of inclusivity for minority students. Overall, UGA presents a dynamic environment that balances rigorous academics with an active social scene.

reviews.text: The University of Georgia was the perfect place to get my bachelor's degree. It was the perfect mix of academics and fun. From Saturday's between the hedges to Sunday's at the library, I was able to have the perfect balance. Athens itself has really developed over the past four years and has opened many new and trendy restaurants and retail stores. Downtown Athens is even currently undergoing construction to give downtown a new and improved look. As for nightlife, Athens has over 80 bars, ensuring everyone has a place to have fun. There is nothing like supporting the sports teams from getting a jersey replica at women's soccer games to a Saturday night football game with eight overtimes, to beating Auburn in basketball, UGA has one of the most vibrant student sections. The only downside that I have encountered in my four years at UGA and living in Athens, is the crime rate and homeless population downtown. Athens has a high homeless population and high crime rate that impacts safety.
This university has everything that you look for when applying to a college. Whether its academics, athletics, greek life, college town, UGA has it all.

My first semester at the school was extremely enjoyable for a number of reasons. If you are a fan of sports, I doubt there are much better environments for an athletic event than Sanford Stadium for football games. As far as academics goes, I have been extremely satisfied with how well the coursework has challenged me yet managed to feel rewarding at the same time. I love the school and would definitely recommend it to others.

My experience at the University of Georgia has been the most eye-opening and experience giving out of all the colleges which I have considered. Their diverse programs, lively student life, and university spirit have proved that I made the right choice choosing this school as my top choice.
