There are endless opportunities presented to the students, and the atmosphere is inclusive and supportive.

I had a great experience at UM. My counselor was helpful and always worked with me to make my schedule what I wanted and needed it to be to meet prerequisite requirements for graduate programs. Every professor I had during my time here made a lasting impact on me and I still think about them now as a grad student. I was an IPAT student and I loved my peers. As an older student with children it was amazing that I could still find common ground with students and was able to make some life long friends in this program.
The only thing I would change is the financial aid office. There were issues with disbursement EVERY semester despite “being on top of it.” There was a lot of misinformation and the financial aid office employees are never able to adequately assist you or answer your questions. I am not the only person who has experienced it. This is a campus wide issue that really put a damper on my experience. Despite the financial aid office I did love my time here as an undergrad.
Campus has a lot of good study spots inside and outside, professors want students to succeed, and there are plenty of coffee/snack spots around campus

I had a good overall experience with the University of Montana as a student. I mainly focused on my classes and did homework/studied in the same buildings. The professors are really cool for the most part.
Professors are amazing, yet food, housing and most financial aid and housing employees very uncollaborative.

It was a fun location to be for undergrad, yet the resources and online processes are so limited and difficult. So many times my information literally disappeared and I had to resubmit information or need to go through an entire new additional process to get it back. For example, every student has about 5 different email possibilities that are all different depending on the interface you are logging into, but there is no clear explanation of which one to use. It would like nice to have more technologically savvy systems and consistency.

As a current undergraduate student at the University of Montana, I have greatly enjoyed my experience both on campus and within the Missoula community. UM offers a wide range of student resources and opportunities for involvement, making it easy for students to feel supported and engaged. There truly is something for everyone, whether it’s joining clubs, participating in campus events, or finding a community with shared interests. Academic advisors have been especially helpful, and the requirement to meet with an advisor before registering for courses each semester ensures students stay on track academically. One of the best aspects of attending UM is Saturday football gameday. The energy and excitement on campus are unmatched, creating a sense of unity and school pride that makes these days unforgettable. Overall, the University of Montana is a welcoming and vibrant campus that offers students a well-rounded college experience, strong academic support, and long-lasting memories.

The University of Montana is a great school if you take your academic career seriously and love spending time outdoors. There are endless opportunities to be involved in student organizations and in research.

Every advisor and professor I have met seems to be pleased to be doing their job; rare these days! Everyone is helpful, and students are held to a high standard. The academics are top-notch, and every class I've taken has been beneficial to my degree

My experience at UM has been incredible. If you are a person planning to study anything regarding the outdoors or the environment and just enjoy being outdoors overall, this is the place to be. The amount of world class professors here within the environmental science/forestry/wildlife biology realm is impressive. And Missoula has so many opportunities to get outdoors, and even if you aren't somebody who recreated outdoors at all before college (such as myself), it is so easily accessible and easy to get into in Missoula.
I love the availability of professors, the quality of the nursing program and the atmosphere of the college. Diversity could be upped.

The best college i have ever been to in the united states. Faculty and professors are the best. The way they teach is the best way of learning. They make the syllabus easy with explaining each and every detail concepts and they make things clear even if you ask them n number of times.

There are a lot of great opportunities available including the Davidson Honors College and Frankie Global Leadership Initiative. Frankie GLI gives an opportunity for students to study abroad or at a partner school domestically. I've had a lot of great professors at UMT as well, and the honors college has offered me a lot of support. I also had a great experience with the Office of Disability Equity when I was applying for accommodations.
My main issue is the lack of diversity. As an African American student I am often isolated and surrounded by people who are discriminatory and say derogatory things everyday. It makes the learning environment very challenging and the black faculty consists of two people (that I know of) so there is limited support.
I am in the Black Student Union, however, which has existed for 58 years!! I hope that it can grow again, as it has shrunk in recent years, so if you are a prospective black student, weigh the opportunities vs. cons to decide what is best.

ai_summary: At the University of Nebraska - Lincoln (UNL), students appreciate the vibrant campus life and diverse opportunities, both academically and socially. Many reviews highlight the supportive and approachable professors , with a solid educational experience dependent on the specific major and course. The campus is described as welcoming, with a friendly atmosphere that encourages student involvement in a variety of clubs and activities; however, some students feel that making connections can be challenging without engaging in Greek life. While students enjoy the range of food options and the communal environment, complaints about aging dorms , parking issues, and tuition costs resonate often. The city of Lincoln adds to the overall experience, with many noting it offers plenty to explore, though some express a desire for more diversity and inclusivity on campus. Overall, UNL is seen as a place where personal growth and academic achievement are highly feasible.

reviews.text:
The college offers good academic support to students. There is an office specially designed to help students get help if they are struggling in class. Some classes also have support from TAs. I was a Computer Science student, and found the TA program they had set up for comp sci classes especially helpful. There was also a TA program dedicated to math classes. Professors can be a hit or miss. I've had some really bad ones, and only a few I really enjoyed having. Most were just ok.
If you want to get involved, there are a decent number of clubs to join, although I wish there were more clubs involving interests/hobbies. The advertising for some clubs is also non-existent, so I felt like I might've missed out on a club I would've enjoyed. There are club fairs, but it's usually always the same clubs with booths at those fairs and other campus events where clubs are present.
Overall pretty good, there’s a lot of opportunities to grow academically and professionally. I wish there was more diversity and inclusivity, but I’m hopeful that will change soon.
i love the area of the campus its super easy to get around. everyone is super friendly and the husker community is amazing
The environment on campus is rewarding in the friendships and community you find. However outside of Lincoln is dull and there is not much to do.

I have really enjoyed the University of Nebraska - Lincoln. I have grown up in Lincoln and was very grateful to have such a great campus just down the street from my house. The education that I am receiving is good and if I had to choose to come here again, I would.
The school might seem big at first but after a while you start to feel familiar faces. I like the size of the school, it’s not too small or too big.

I really like the environment of UNL. The people are so friendly and the campus is beautiful. I will say the whole meal plan system is not the greatest, not great food options and expensive stores. Other than that it's a great place!

As an in-state student, I am not a fan of UNL because I feel trapped. It is a great school with amazing professors, opportunities, and classes but I am constantly surrounded by people that I grew up with. It makes it difficult to grow around new people when I don't have a single class without someone I went to high school with.

I really enjoy the pride for the school and how being a Husker truly makes the experience feel meaningful. The Husker staff do a great job of making students feel welcome and providing opportunities to meet new people and find where you fit on campus. There are also many resources available to help ensure academic success. If I had to change one thing, it would be how glamorized Greek life is on campus.
Campus is nice, a lot of different statues to look at, student life looks good, and I feel like its a good school.
I like how much diversity there is between people and how open everyone is with each other. My professors so far have been very good as well.
I really like the overall vibe on campus. I feel safe when I'm walking around, the campus is lively, and I have decent professors that seem invested in my future.

My experience at the University of Nebraska–Lincoln has been really meaningful so far. I’ve always loved the atmosphere here in Lincoln, and hearing my parents talk about their own time at UNL inspired me to follow in their footsteps. I wasn’t completely sure where I wanted to go after high school, but choosing UNL feels like the right decision.
I appreciate the welcoming campus environment and the sense of community you can tell people genuinely want you to succeed. The opportunities for growth, both academically and personally, have been great.
If there’s one thing I’d like to see improve, it would be continued support for students when it comes to advising and communication. Clearer guidance can make a big difference, especially for students navigating college for the first time.
Overall, UNL has been a great place to learn, meet new people, and build a future I’m excited about.

I chose Nebraska because when I was deciding on what school I’d like to attend I didn’t quite know what I’d like to major in yet I knew I wanted to do something in the graphic design, creative field, along with business communications and marketing. When I started looking further in to Nebraska, I found the advertising and public relations major, and it was truly everything I wanted in a major wrapped up into one thing. And with already having every part of all those majors combined into one it left me being able to minor in finance like I wanted to. This was all just from looking into the college, but when I actually got to take my unofficial visit to Nebraska, everything was 100 times better than I ever could have wished or asked for or even anticipated myself wanting in a college.

My experience at the University of Nebraska–Lincoln has been both academically and personally fulfilling. The university fosters a culture of curiosity and innovation, allowing students to engage deeply in research and critical thinking. The faculty members are approachable and genuinely invested in student success, offering valuable mentorship and guidance. Beyond academics, the campus community is welcoming and diverse, providing opportunities to connect with peers from around the world. Overall, UNL has provided me with the resources, support, and inspiration to grow as a researcher and as an individual.

ai_summary: At the University of Nebraska at Omaha (UNO), students generally find the academic environment supportive, with many praising the helpfulness and engagement of professors . The diverse community is a highlight for many, as it enriches both academic and extracurricular experiences. However, it is often mentioned that UNO primarily serves as a commuter school, resulting in a less vibrant social scene, where students tend to keep to themselves. The campus is praised for being safe and well-maintained, yet parking can be a significant issue for commuters. While many appreciate the affordability and available resources , some wish for enhancements in student life and social opportunities. Overall, UNO is seen as a solid choice for students focused on academics, though it may not suit those seeking an active campus social life.

reviews.text: So far, as a first year student, it has been a great experience. I feel guided well in how this scene is operated. They have great job opportunities, and the professors and the community is incredibly helpful.

I like how it is open and chill, you get your work done and be able to meet people on campus. It is great to experience a new team and people that like the same things as you do. I hope for future success that more parking near popular buildings.
UNO was a good school overall; I had some problems with microaggression from a few professors, however.

I really like the school as a whole. The classes and resources are very good, like in the lab there’s a lot of access to new equipment. However, since it is a commuter school there isn’t so much of a good community or student social life.

My overall experience at UNO is average I don't spend too much time on campus due to having to work anytime I am not in class since I have to pay for school out of pocket. So, I haven't been able to fully experience the college life on campus. I can tell you that the professors at UNO have been awesome most of them will listen to you and will offer advice to get through their class. They offer office hours as well to ask additional questions and even to go over the test and what you missed. There is a lot of amenities at UNO I am often in the H&K building which is where they have the weight rooms, basketball court, ping pong, and even rock climbing. These are some activities I have been able to use throughout my 6 years at UNO. I love the school but I have been here long and would love to graduate. I am on track to graduate in 2027. I would really appreciate being accepted so I can further my education just a little less stressed to keep my grades up. Thank You!

I love philanthropy life, my first semester I didn’t do anything but school and it was dreadful. No one in my classes wanted to be my friend, I lived off campus, UNO unfortunately does not do a good job of getting students to really make friends with one another. Its more serious than that. I want to see people getting involved with one another in classes, clubs, and volunteer opportunities.
I honestly had an amazing year so far. My only issue is my payments. Otherwise I have had a great semester.

My personal experience at UNO has been really nice. I like my student life here. it feels easy to meet new people and make friends. Joining clubs and getting involved on campus has also been simple. Overall, I feel happy and comfortable being a part of the UNO community.
The students and professors are all very nice, and the facilities offer a lot of great opportunities to improve your academic abilities.

College experience has been great for me. There are a couple of campuses, and the Scott Campus is where I go to take engineering classes in the PKI. Great atmosphere, everyone is friendly and the place inside is very neat. Food options is limited and can get crowded near the cafe. Study areas need to be improved.

Even though I have lived here for a couple of years before attending university I still feel like I am discovering the city of Omaha. There is so many activities on campus it makes it really easy to get involved, explore Omaha and make meaningful connections with other students. I also enjoy the challenging classes and the extensive labs that help further my learning.

My experience at UNO has been largely positive. The university offers a welcoming environment with a strong sense of community, and I’ve appreciated the accessibility of professors and the relatively small class sizes, which allow for more personalized instruction. The campus is modern and well-maintained, with great resources like the library, wellness center, and student success services. One of the standout features of UNO is its commitment to affordability and flexibility, especially for non-traditional and commuter students. The online course offerings and evening classes made it easier to balance school with work and other responsibilities. The only issue I have had is finding parking close to class, even in the large garages. Overall, UNO is a solid choice, especially for students looking for quality education in a supportive, community-driven atmosphere. I’d recommend it to anyone who values practical, career-oriented learning and wants to stay connected to the Omaha area.
When I first started attending Uno I have met new people and made more friends, I got into more activities
I have enjoyed studying at UNO and am excited to continue going. I haven't lived on campus yet, so I can't speak on that, but mostly everything else has been pretty cool.
Smaller campus in a city but the size of community does not overwhelm the feel. The faculty and staff also care greatly about performance.

ai_summary: At the University of Nevada, Las Vegas (UNLV), students often highlight the strong community and support network provided both by peers and faculty. Many reviews mention the welcoming atmosphere and the university’s commitment to diversity , fostering an environment where students from different backgrounds feel included. Academically, while professors are generally viewed as approachable and invested in student success, some students express concerns about the quality of instruction in specific programs, particularly in STEM. The campus is noted for its size and accessibility, though challenges such as parking and safety—especially in the surrounding areas—are areas for improvement. Student life is described as vibrant, with a plethora of clubs and events, although some wish for more engagement opportunities, particularly in the later semesters. Financial considerations come into play for many, with tuition seen as somewhat high, but many students appreciate the availability of financial aid. Overall, UNLV offers a unique blend of academic opportunities and cultural experiences , particularly attractive for those interested in hospitality and business due to its Las Vegas location.

reviews.text: I am in my 1st semester and have had nothing but great experiences so far. My professors are personable and provide constructive feedback on my assignments. I have 2 classes in person and 1 online, but they all feel like intimate small group classes. The road construction around the campus is a nightmare, but that’s not UNLV’s fault. I’m excited to pick up a class or 2 over the summer to keep advancing towards my degree.

My experience with UNLV was mostly positive, although there were some things that made campus life a little difficult. To start, the parking lot was always full, and most times you had to park in the furthest parking garage and walk to the other side of campus, which is like a ten-minute walk, and in the Las Vegas heat, it can get pretty exhausting.
the school is very nice and diverse. a lot of people are friendly and it isn't hard to make friends.

For me, the University of Nevada, Las Vegas is a really interesting place to go to school. I like that it’s in Las Vegas because we have a lot of opportunities here with tourism, business, and entertainment. Its the type of school where you could meet a lot of different kinds of people and have many new experiences. I also think the campus looks very nice and collected; they definitely have a lot of programs and activities for students. Overall, UNLV seems like a place where someone could learn a lot but also grow as a person and figure out what they want to do in the future.

So far, the professors have been on point with their syllabus and I like how most have the materials online through MindTap. It was all a little overwhelming with all the different website that I had to keep track of but you get the hang of it fast, I personally found it helpful to keep a book of websites, what they are for, and passwords because it was a lot. I would like to see the Sona website accessible from anywhere, and for more of the staff at school to be more knowledgeable about that program.
UNLV is very much a commuter school now. People come in and get out. It is very hard to make connections there. No social outlets there.

I like how the campus buildings are big and in a closed off area. I like the people there as I have a lot of friends that go there. Its very accessible and easy going. Its a very safe and chill environment where people have a chance to meet others and build friendships. The clubs there are great and the class room sizes aren't too big but also aren't too little. They have good food and the dorms are nice too but aren't as close as most people would like. The only main part I don't like and would encourage to change is the location. It isn't as close to my family as I would like and there are a lot of homeless people around in the entire area. Not in the buildings but by the streets close to it. The construction is also very annoying as there is always traffic and construction going on which blocks traffic. It is a hassle and an annoyance when there is always something needing to be fixed and always having to plan to be 5-10 mins late because of traffic.

University of Nevada, Las Vegas offers a diverse, energetic college experience shaped by its urban location near the Las Vegas Strip. Students often mention the wide range of majors, strong hospitality and business programs, and access to internships with local companies. Campus life includes clubs, sports, and cultural events reflecting a multicultural student body. Because many students commute, the social scene can feel quieter than traditional residential campuses, but there are still plenty of ways to get involved. The desert climate is hot, and summers are intense. Overall, UNLV is known for opportunity, diversity, and practical career connections today everywhere.

So far, my overall experience with the University of Nevada, Las Vegas has been positive. There are, of course, pros and cons to any university, and even though this is the only college I have attended, I have found it to be an overall satisfactory college. To begin with, the pros, UNLV is a large and exceptionally diverse college that provides equal opportunities for people of all backgrounds. I enjoy the diversity as it allows students to connect with backgrounds that they may not been familiar with before attending UNLV. UNLV also provides many study resources, which have helped me through numerous difficult classes. These resources include an extremely large library with private study rooms and designated quiet areas, along with a tutoring center. There are, however, many cons to being a UNLV student, but I want to stay positive and focus on the pros. My only major complaint includes professors who are very hard to understand and lack the passion for teaching.

UNLV overall is a very welcoming university. It is a very diverse school that offers many opportunities for all students regardless of any differences. They also have great amenities for almost anything you could possibly need. UNLV has splendid professors who genuinely care about your education. Most professors try to get to know their students on a personal level and keep their office open in case students need to talk about school, or anything at all. Not only do the professors want you to succeed, they teach like they want you to as well. Although the campus is on the older side of town, it is a pretty safe campus. Students are nice, security is always around, and nothing bad tends to happen. Overall it is a pretty great school with lots of fun school events, great education, and a safe space for those who need it.

The University of Nevada, Las Vegas is a very interesting college to attend. It is surrounded by the Las Vegas Strip, but also is strict when it comes to academics. There are a wide variety of clubs and organizations to join to stay involved in campus life. Professors are not bad, it depends who you have.

Everyone remembers their first semester of college. It's either full of fond memories or a tough time to reminisce on. My first semester, I was required to take a first-year seminar course. This course introduced me to what UNLV has to offer, and it helped me fight the difficulties that I was facing during my transition. I began to really take advantage of advising, tutoring, academic coaching, and even the gym. All of these things have really kept me structured during my first year of college.
The online experience did give me time to do other things I needed so it was rarely easy to come back to it and get started.

Given the fact that I live off campus, I believe my personal experiences with the school has been rather hindered. I know little of what goes on in terms of "party scenes" and bonding exercises that occur outside of class and perhaps that's why I don't feel particularly sentimental when it comes to my university. Although I consider myself lucky to be in the presence of such brilliant professors and studying a degree I regard with outmost respect, there simply seems to be a void when it comes to connecting with other students. I would like to see more efforts being made within classes to empathize and relate with one another. I walk by students I've shared a class with and never give a thought to look back. I don't want that, I want to look back and recognize this fellow student and bask in the shared pleasure that is education. I wish my university would make more of an effort to unite students in their shared experience of being human.

My experience here at University of Las Vegas Nevada has been very eye opening and life changing. I am a third-year transfer student at UNLV about to start my second semester, and I sure have learned a lot. I am truly sad in my decision in transferring to UNLV because I believed this institution was going to make a big impact on my life and education. As much as I dislike change, this university taught me not to always believe in the good thing in mind compared to what you have in front of you. Prior to transferring I was ahead at my prior university and could have started my master's early, but I believed that I needed a change and took it. Now I am in more debt than I was before, and I want that to change here at University of Las Vegas. I notice they are actively increasing the tuition year by year and this does not help struggling students that chose to attend a public university for a supposed "cheaper" price. I like the updated classroom buildings, such as the hospitality hall.

ai_summary: The University of Nevada, Reno (UNR) presents a mixed bag of experiences. Many students appreciate the beautiful campus and supportive atmosphere , noting that the campus is easy to navigate and is home to various events and activities that foster a sense of community. Academically, most students find the professors to be knowledgeable and dedicated, though experiences vary widely, with some expressing frustration over large class sizes and teaching effectiveness. The student life is vibrant, highlighted by accessible extracurricular opportunities , a solid gym, and a lively college environment, though opinions on nearby entertainment options vary, especially for those without a car. Concerns about parking , tuition costs, and food quality were frequently mentioned, with many students wishing for improvements in these areas. Despite some challenges, many express a strong connection to the university and a positive overall experience.

reviews.text: I am currently a sophomore here at UNR , and as someone who immediately fell in love with the campus, I am still incredibly happy with my choice. My courses are very interesting and related to my major which is more motivating than taking the general education/required courses from freshman year. One thing I would love to see change however, is the majors offered for criminal studies. I would have loved to major in criminology, or forensics, or along those lines instead of criminal justice. It doesn't necessarily have to be a major either, just having the option to do so and take those specific courses would be beneficial.

It sounds like you've had a really positive experience at the University of Nevada, Reno! It's great that you've found the campus to be welcoming and appreciate the balance it offers between city amenities and outdoor adventures, especially with Lake Tahoe being so accessible. It's also wonderful to hear that you've found the professors to be knowledgeable and approachable, which can make a big difference in your learning. The availability of clubs, organizations, and campus events is a fantastic way to get involved and enhance your college life. The sense of community you mentioned is a key aspect of a good university experience, and it's fantastic that you found it easy to meet people and build connections there.

The dorms honestly really aren't that bad (other than Nye), but the food in the dining hall is awful. As a science major, I'd say that academics all around are very mid. The campus itself is very nice and walkable. There's an active greek life community and a decent party scene. Some of the campus events are fun and there is generally a good community on campus. The mindset here is very "only UNR students can talk badly about UNR."

My time at UNR was overall pretty great and I absolutely do not regret going here for my undergraduate degree. It is very affordable and if you are willing to live with roommates the cost of living is not terrible either. There are a plethora of clubs and programs that I would encourage prospective students to look into joining. This is what really made my experience great. Additionally, if you are someone interested in research, specifically STEM-related, UNR has incredible opportunities to engage with high quality and rigorous research. The campus is beautiful and well maintained and the classes are only as hard as you make them. My advice, have fun and take extracurriculars if you need to meet a credit requirement! Intro to acting and Intro to ceramics were phenomenal!

University of Nevada-Reno is a college for people. My first semester I met a lot of people and I realize how enjoyable college can be. The professors are good but there are some who shouldn't be teaching especially a higher class. I do like how walkable everything is. I do think university Nevada Reno is a good college.
I am enjoying my first year here. There are lots of great clubs to join. I feel safe on campus and around town. I feel accepted for who I am here.

The University of Nevada, Reno (UNR) is a strong school overall and provides a very positive college experience. The campus is attractive, well maintained, and close to downtown Reno, which makes it easy to enjoy both campus life and the surrounding area. There is a strong sense of school spirit, and students have many opportunities to get involved through clubs, events, research, and internships.
Academically, UNR offers solid programs, and many professors are knowledgeable and engaging. When classes are taught by passionate professors, the learning experience is rewarding and motivating. The university encourages hands-on learning and provides opportunities that help prepare students for careers or graduate school.
However, advising is one of UNR’s weaker areas. Advisors can be difficult to reach and are not always as helpful as expected, which can make planning classes stressful. Overall, UNR is a good university with far more positives than negatives.

I really like UNR. I don't mind the walking distance from classes. I especially love the scenery all over campus. Being able to enjoy the fresh air outside is great! I just wish there were better ways to get to campus and how to use the busses.

My overall experience here have been good so far, as in campus food and housing, and courses. Something I’d like to see change is less professors being rude. And someone you can talk to that is actually willing to help?

I loved most of my teachers and felt seen. Everyone is very welcoming and I feel at ease there. The only thing I can say is I didn't like the dinning hall food. It wasn't horrible, but could be better.
It’s a good school professors are understanding for the most part and student life is so fun even when on campus lots of goodies and swag overall an 8/10 school!!
Unr is a great place. They have so many majors that don’t seem to exist at other schools anymore. Great location in a great city. Everyone is kind and cares.
I wish the university would fund more parking and also closer parking. Everything is super expensive and we still have to walk a few miles.
I really have enjoyed how supportive all the staff from all the departments are and have made my educational journey easy and supportive.
My overall experience at the University of Nevada, Reno is excellent. I am a transfer student from TMCC.

ai_summary: The University of New England (UNE) offers a close-knit community experience amplified by its beautiful coastal location , which many students appreciate. The small class sizes foster strong relationships with professors, who are reportedly helpful and invested in students’ success, particularly within health-related programs . However, several reviews express concerns about high tuition costs , problematic housing situations, and unsatisfactory dining options. While some students find the campus ambiance friendly and inclusive, others describe it as cliquey, with a limited social scene and challenges in securing jobs on campus. Overall, UNE stands out for its strong health science curriculum, but students seeking a vibrant social life might find it lacking.

reviews.text: Herbert and Lauder are in the Epstein files. Herbert sent his four children to Lauder’s mansion and received scholarships for all four.
I do enjoy my time here, however for the amount of money I'm paying I wish there was more stuff in or around campus.
Terrible school, no diversity, not welcoming. Provost Mahon says black students are "not good” and creates a toxic and racist atmosphere. Stay away!
I like the smaller community and the location. There are many things to do outside of campus such as going to the beach or walking around Portland.

Coming to this university was one the best decisions I have ever made in my life. I love every friend I have met here. The campus is gorgeous and the classes are amazing. It is very hands on and you get to learn and participate in labs after your lectures. The faculty is very inclusive. I will never transfer or drop out of this school. The only thing I would change is the people that also attend this university, some are weird and some are mean and disruptive. That is the only thing about about this place but UNE can not control that.

My overall experience is connecting with staff and faculty who are open to help give resources when you need help. The curriculum is easy to follow and adjust to learning. I am able to enjoy as I learn the different topics in my master of social work program and meet people from different walks of life. I am able to be flexible with my work schedule while also completing my schoolwork.

I absolutely love it here. As someone who grew up in a town by the ocean, UNE is absolutely beautiful. The beach is a five minute walk away with a variety of wildlife to explore for those who enjoy tidal pooling. Student life and events are very fun and they make sure people meet new people and have new experiences. The food is good, but I have a poultry allergy so I can’t eat a lot of stuff they have but they are very accommodating. The professors are very supportive and there are smaller class sizes so it’s a very comforting environment.
Professors call students ‘stupid'. When we complained to Provost Gwendolyn Mahon, this racist bully laughed at us and berated us. Such a toxic environment! Many students have already transferred out.

It's a nice campus and its fun during the summer and spring months since you can hang out at the beach or walk in the woods, but the social life is not it. I've only made 2 friends since I've started and both of which have left and now I'm lonely. Other students are extremely cliquey, and it feels humiliating to approach anyone. It feels exactly like middle and high school where everyone has a group and you'll be an outcast if you don't. When I toured, the MAR center had tons of fish, but due to regulations, there hardly any anymore. These restrictions have made it near impossible for student led research. Lots of clubs are inactive and the one I'm in was actually disbanded over the summer and were trying to get it started again, but the school wont fund us till then. Tuition is extremely high and it has me questioning how and if I'll ever pay it off. I strongly believe I will die with this debt. I really hope my future semesters are better because I'm at my lowest low right now.
Unsafe. President James Herbert is a pervert who got fired from Drexel for inappropriate behavior with female students. He always brings young students at his house late at night. Disgusting.

My experience at UNE has been really positive overall so far. The small class sizes made it easy to connect with professors, and I always felt like they genuinely cared about helping students succeed. The university’s focus on hands-on learning, especially in the health sciences, has given me valuable real-world experience that I know will help me in my future career. Even though I didn’t live on campus, I still felt included in campus life and appreciated how beautiful and well-kept the Biddeford campus is. One thing that could make the experience even better would be improving transportation between the Biddeford and Portland campuses, since that can be a bit of a challenge. I’d also love to see more events and activities to help commuter and transfer students feel more connected. Overall, UNE has been a great place to learn and grow. It’s a friendly, close-knit community in a stunning location, and I’m really glad I chose to study here.

Overall I believe UNE is a good school. The student life and clubs are very interactive and there are many things to do and join on campus. I know some colleges on the weekends is slow and many people leave but at UNE many people stay on campus to stay for the events hosted by many clubs. Like all colleges UNE is quite expensive, even though there are many benefits for going there I think the cost is definitely something that I wish could be changed.
University of New England is very welcoming and diverse no matter who you are. The students and staff are very friendly and will do anything in their power to make sure you are where you want to be.
I think the tuition is a bit much and has increased a few times since enrolling but they really care about their students and have great programs.

ai_summary: At the University of New Hampshire, students appreciate the strong academic offerings , particularly in hands-on and experiential learning , with many highlighting the supportive and engaging professors . The campus is described as beautiful and safe, fostering a welcoming community despite concerns about its lack of diversity. Student life features numerous activities, clubs, and opportunities for involvement, although some feel that campus life could benefit from more structured community-building initiatives . The food has received mixed reviews, with some praising its quality while others express disappointment about the variety and availability. Financial concerns , especially relating to tuition and financial aid processes, are also a recurring theme, reflecting broader worries about the budget and administration's focus on revenue. Overall, students find UNH to offer a rich college experience, though opinions on campus engagement and administrative support vary widely.

reviews.text: So far, my experience at UNH has been very positive. I have met many new people with many different backgrounds and stories to tell. The education and staff quality, for the most part has been good. I'm really excited to spend the next 3 years of my life here at UNH.

I am a freshman, electrical engineering major UNH. I really enjoy my classes and several student activities. My favorite is the entrepreneurship center. I never thought of myself as an entrepreneur, but this place encourages me to think differently and see possibilities.
One of my professors offered me an opportunity to do research with him next year. I’m very excited about this!
I am very happy with my decision to attend UNH.

My experience at the University of New Hampshire was very positive overall. The program gave me the flexibility I needed while still challenging me academically. Since my program was online, I was able to balance school with other responsibilities, but the coursework still required discipline, critical thinking, and strong time management.
The classes helped me develop a deeper understanding of healthcare systems, leadership, and communication. Many of the assignments focused on applying concepts to real-world situations, which made the material feel practical and relevant. I also appreciated how the program encouraged independent learning and problem solving rather than just memorizing information.
Overall, my time at UNH helped me grow academically and professionally. It strengthened my ability to manage a demanding workload, think critically, and communicate ideas effectively. These skills have continued to help me as I move forward in my education and career.

From the moment I started to research about university, the university of new Hampshire captured my my attention due to it exponential learning, innovation and opportunity to participate in researches.
Beyond academics, the campus life appears vibrant and diverse. With numerous student organizations, athletics, and leadership opportunities, students have many ways to build friendships, develop teamwork skills, and explore interests outside the classroom

I think its a great campus the people there make you feel like its a second home and the amount of nature, like college woods is a great way to relaxed as well as exercise. The rec its really great to and welcoming and a sufficient amount of space personally I really enjoy the rock wall.
Even though it's a college in New Hampshire, there was so much less diversity than I expected. For professor quality, it really varies by department.

After transferring to UNH for my last two years of undergraduate school, I found myself more involved in campus life. My advisor was the best, and I recommend that every student have conversations with their advisors regularly; they are extremely helpful.
The social life on campus is unmatched. From Ice Hockey to Football to club sports, UNH students, faculty, and staff are proud to wear blue and white.

The curriculum counselors are horrible. When my daughter started, they put her on a path for her degree. Now after a year they say the degree is no longer available and changing to another degree they say they offer will extend here time at school by at least another year. Makes me feel like they are just trying to get more money out of her. When she transferred in over a year ago, they should have known that the degree she wanted wasn't right. Anybody looking to go there, be sure they actually have the degree you want, and it you will be able to finish it before it changes.

I did not enjoy my time here at all and transferred out after my freshman year. I found the campus to be cliquey and there's really not much to do around there due to the rural location. Additionally, classes were so big it was hard to feel like more than a number, and I found minimal support in advising when I expressed that I would be transferring. I left for a smaller school in Boston and had a significantly better experience.

UNH has a gorgeous campus with a coastal New England vibe, great academics, and fun social scene. In the 25 years since I graduated, my appreciation for the academics & overall college experience at UNH only continues to grow. UNH prepared me very well for my chosen career. It is accessible to Boston by train and in close proximity to both the coast and mountains. Toured several schools along the East coast and could not be more thrilled that my own child is choosing UNH as an OOS student! She felt the school had just the right balance of academics & rah-rah culture set in a beautiful environment.
I have always seen myself going to college and when I decided it was time for me to go, I knew that UNH was the right fit for me right when I stepped on campus.

The administration running the University is terrible. They do not care about the students or the faculty. All they care about is money.
It's very possible to have a good time at UNH. Some professors are excellent, and some are downright awful. You will get from your life here what you put into it. Many people here are friendly and kind.

Going to UNH has been the worst decision I have made. The promises of good academics, a diverse student body, a good location, and a good party scene are all complete lies. The police on campus are overbearing, the parties are boring, social scene nonexistent unless you're 21+, no one talks to one another at parties or anywhere else unless they already know each other. The classes I have taken are all jokes. They aren't taught well and I have hardly learned anything yet I feel like I am never free from constant homework. The town is tiny and getting to more worthwhile towns is a chore, the transportation is hard to navigate. The funds are mismanaged and most campus services are severely lacking as a result: for one thing, there is only one dining hall on weekends, yet you must pay a fortune for an unlimited swipe meal plan. Please do not fall for the lies. Please, if you value your education and college experience, go elsewhere.

Overall, the University of New Hampshire is a great school. I enjoy most of the proffessors, they genuienly care about their students and their academic success. I am a commuter student, so I don't know much about other things like the dining hall or dorm life. I think however that they have a great variety of clubs and organizations. Everyone I have met has been nice too, which is something I really appreciate.

I think UNH is a great school for students wanting the feel of being on a bigger campus while also being connected to other students and professors. I came from a small high school in New Hampshire and wanted a college with more people and opportunities, UNH definitely provided that for me! As someone who is more on the quiet side, I was nervous I would get lost in the crowd here. But once I put myself out there and started joining clubs and meeting with professors I felt like I was right where I belonged! There's truly something for everyone here if you take the time to look for it.

ai_summary: At the University of New Mexico, many students appreciate the supportive and friendly atmosphere both among peers and faculty, contributing to a welcoming campus culture . Academically, there are diverse programs and engaging professors , although experiences can vary widely depending on the specific course and instructor. Students frequently mention the abundance of extracurricular activities and the opportunity to meet people from various backgrounds, enriching their social life. However, there are concerns about bureaucratic processes, particularly regarding advising and financial aid, which some find frustrating. Additionally, while the campus itself is perceived as safe and beautiful, the surrounding area raises safety concerns, particularly at night. Overall, UNM is seen as a solid choice for those seeking a diverse, community-oriented college experience .

reviews.text: Horrible professors in CS, I had to do tutor sessions for free after school because the tutors didn't know what they were doing. I had to leave because my account had a glitch that kept giving me holds and nobody would fix it, therefore I couldn't register. My Environmental Science teacher taught our class the wrong course for the whole semester, instead he taught us planetary science. I sent so many checks to the Bursars office and yet their mailing system was broken so all were lost and I ended up with a bunch of late fees because of this, I eventually had to pay in person. The bursars office also doesn't have support that speaks English from my own experience. My advisor was useless, I got kicked out of the don't give up on math class because she gave our class way too much work. They kicked me out of my email and kept sending important emails to me while I was kicked out of it. They miss handle their money, the president literally resigned halfway through the fall semester.
I really like the Commodore and the push to always want to do good and be more. They are always promoting good health and remembering mental health as well.
UNM is a great school with lovely professors, a wide array of classes, and many clubs/activities for student life outside of class.
I appreciate the university for allowing such flexibility with the class schedule to allow people like myself, a full time employee, to complete my bachelors program.

UNM is an amazing experience. The culture and area of UNM are so beautiful, and the people are even better. I have made so many new friends, and the people in general, including professors and other UNM staff, are so kind. Academic life is so amazing. Last semester I got all A's and one B. The teachers are so understanding when it comes to assignments, and they really know how to teach.

I meet some really good people and had some really good professors. They were all very helpful and would work with me when I had to miss a class or test due to military obligations with the Army Reserves. Only thing is that there were often many homeless wandering around due to the location.

Chairmen do not care if their professors are disrespectful even after meeting. I had multiple issues with being discriminated against for my appearance and identity. Horrible safety guidelines homeless everywhere, campus police could care less, parties extremely dangerous.

I don't live on campus, so I am not really too involved in student life and activities. There are tons of resources all around campus for academic success, safety, along with a bunch of other stuff. There is nothing in particular that I would like to see differently around campus.

My overall experience at the University of New Mexico can be explained as a good in between to the categories of social life and school life. The campus is beautiful and the perfect size for walking and biking around campus. Staff are overall sweet and help in the best way they can. Athletics is a major thing in regards to college life at the University of New Mexico, we are well known to have a football and basketball rival which makes those specific games far more entertaining to watch. Clubs and faculties offer students suggestions of places they can get discounts at local diners, restaurants, cafes and more, but as well as any tourist spots that seem inviting. My overall experience can be explained as peaceful, collaborative, and fun.
Just go and travel, get your school work done and make sure you are in class. Everything else can come later especially if you’re on scholarship.
Overall UNM has been really great and exciting. I did have trouble getting started , including some wrongful holds on my student account, but was able to get them solved with the help of loborespect.

I enjoy the people, and they psychology courses. The things I would like see change is the housing and food. some of the dorms are literally falling apart. We have great food in the SUB but a lot of complaints of the LAPO food.

I came to UNM from Louisiana, which was only possible thanks to a scholarship exclusive to out-of-state students. My family and I have been in love with New Mexico ever since our first summer vacation spent there.
I loved the duck pond, how close the cafeteria was to the dorms, and the breadth of potions at the gym. I do genuinely think the school could benefit from an in-house arcade.

My first semester has been, overall, very well. I‘be been able to immediately start in my major curriculum. However, if I had a vote, I would just skip Gen Ed. Courses altogether and immediately start my film studies. I’ve met and become friends with an eclectic group of people. Food is good. One major problem with UNM is parking. They require all Freshmen to live on campus, but does not have enough parking to accommodate the students. So we have to park off campus, which is 20 minutes from the school! The parking passes are expensive and the don’t hesitate to give out tickets if you park without a parking pass.

Campus police is completely disorganized and inadequate. They willingly allowed the body of a 14 year old child to be left undiscovered for 3 hours because they were "busy." DO NOT SEND YOUR ADULT CHILDREN HERE.

ai_summary: The University of New Orleans (UNO) offers a unique blend of an urban environment and a supportive academic atmosphere , making it appealing to students seeking a diverse and friendly community. Many reviews highlight the strong commitment of professors to student success, with accessible faculty helping create close-knit relationships in smaller class sizes. Students appreciate the vibrant cultural backdrop of New Orleans, which enriches their educational experience with opportunities for networking, internships, and community engagement. However, some reviews note challenges such as bureaucratic issues, a lack of extensive student life activities, and outdated facilities. Though the campus is often described as welcoming and clean, students desire more robust support services and an expansion of extracurricular activities, especially for those not living on campus, which can feel quiet at times. Overall, UNO is seen as a solid, affordable choice for students looking for personal growth and academic rigor in a diverse setting.

reviews.text: I’ve been here for almost three years now and I can say that the experience is going down to be something I would like to go back to. You don’t always get the help you need in any category. It’s a place where people would probably recommend community college instead of here.

The University of New Orleans is great. I enjoy the food, events, and the diversity! As an LGBTQ person of color, it's refreshing to see so many people like me. Even though my experiences are positive, it doesn't mean the university is perfect. We have a couple of places to choose from food-wise, but I do wish that they had more of a variety. I also feel as though some buildings, such as the Liberal Arts building, are run down and need some repairs.
My overaly experience was okay, it had its ups and downs. School was good but the campus it self is boring, and the food was okay.

I had a pretty good experience. The cost compared to other colleges was fair. There were people from many differing backgrounds. As a transfer student, I was navigated into my program well. Overall 4 out of 5 experience.

I am a full time student at the University of New Orleans. My experience so far is good. I like the campus and the helpfulness of most of the professors, but struggle a bit academically sometimes. I would like to see change in the majors the university offers.

My experience so far on campus hasn't been bad. When I first got on campus it was a little confusing because I was and still am struggling with paying my tuition and the financial aid office was very confusing and not that helpful.

Overall my experience at UNO has been positive during my five years on campus. The school’s diversity is incredible, with men, women, and non-conforming students from all walks of life. It’s a space where students are welcome to be themselves around their peers as well as professors, who encourage inclusivity. What really makes UNO special isn’t how diverse and accepting it is during the 99%, but its response during the 1% of time when it matters. Bullying, hazing, and discrimination is not allowed, and it shows. This goes for students and professors—if action must be taken, it will be. That, along with the opportunities that the school allows for students to freely protest their opinions on campus truly shows how much the university cares about our voices. The most recent example of this has been the allowance of the Class of 2024 to walk and have a commencement ceremony even though it was out of budget.

This is a perfect university for those looking for a smaller campus with staff that truly care to get to know you and help you grow. Staff have also been very accommodating regarding name changes, and such changes are reflected within the school system quickly.
This is my first year at UNO and I want to say I love it. The support from the faculty and staff is incredible. They really treat you like family.
It was beautiful lakefront campus and caring professors, but buildings and classrooms need updating.
UNO offers a great, affordable education in diverse classes and subjects. It is mostly a communter school with an older student demographic.
I only had a tour. I liked the housing and the students I did socialize with while I was there were super sweet !

There is a wide range of activities around campus. The campus holds a variety of student events. The university of new orleans is a small university which allows more one on one time with professors. The learning experience is made easier through the tutoring center as well as attentive professors. Overall, the campus allows students to flourish in academics. Although the criculium can be difficult it pre pairs pre professional students for upper level classes.

My experience at [School Name] has been truly rewarding. The professors are knowledgeable and passionate, especially in the psychology department where I’ve felt constantly supported. There are plenty of student resources and wellness programs available, which shows the school values mental health. Campus life is vibrant, and I’ve made real friendships through clubs and events. I’d definitely recommend this school to anyone looking for a strong education and supportive environment.

Things I Like About UNO:
I love UNO, the people are great and the teachers are amazing. I love working out and playing basketball in the gym, and the extracurricular events they have are sometimes fun too.
Things I Would Like To Change About UNO:
The food there is not the best and sometimes over seasoned or not seasoned at all. Campus life is okay if you have friends, other than that it’s pretty boring. There’s only like 2 parties throughout the whole year and they are the worst parties ever.

school_name: University of North Carolina at Charlotte
ai_summary: The University of North Carolina at Charlotte presents a diverse and welcoming campus environment , with a beautiful setting and ample resources for students. Many reviews highlight the supportive faculty and community , which contribute to a positive academic experience , particularly in fields like music, architecture, and various sciences. However, some students express concerns about the food quality, campus spirit, and the commuter nature of the university, which can affect campus life and engagement. While there are numerous extracurricular activities and opportunities for socialization, the general sentiment is that there is room for improvement in promoting school spirit and providing better dining options. Overall, UNC Charlotte offers a unique blend of academic rigor and community support, making it a notable choice for prospective students.

reviews.text: I’ve been thoroughly enjoying my time here at UNC Charlotte. I love the opportunities that the school provides to students and the ability to explore and learn more about your desired career outside the classroom. Being at UNC Charlotte has truly opened my eyes to many opportunities and taught me a lot.
I thoroughly enjoy this campus and the student life. It’s very easy to find jobs and employment after graduation.

I found the staff and the teachers to be less than helpful. There’s not a lot of communication between departments, leaving students to have to do a lot of of the work on finding solutions their own issues. This did not seem like a professional place to earn a degree. I found that professors were often unprepared and their lessons and classes did not align to the overall goal or curriculum at the Cato College of education. Additionally, the people at Niner Central as well as at Teal or a lesson helpful with coming up with solutions, and in fact, we’re a big reason that I almost did not receive my degree because of a mixup and poor communication on their end. It seems that a lot of people there do not know what their role exactly is and there’s not a lot of people with long vested experience there.
If you would like a seamless college experience, prioritizing academics or working a full-time job and going to school part-time, this college will only add stress and frustration.
I like UNC Charlotte as it is a beautiful campus with an excellent Computer Science program. Additionally, it is also 20 minutes away from home.

I enjoy UNC Charlotte a lot, I don't plan on leaving/transferring. I've met so many amazing people, although our football team isn't the best the cheering is always at 100%. The professors themselves aren't all that bad I mostly search them up and see what works best for me. I will say they need more advisors as I've had her ignore my emails multiple times, and when she didn't have answers instead of directing me, she kept quiet but as long as I was consistent, I would end up getting a response
It’s been awesome! The best decision I’ve ever made so far in my life!!!Everything is just great here!
It is an all around great school. They are adjusting for the new popularity but are handling it very well.

My overall experience at the University of North Carolina at Charlotte has been positive and growth-oriented. One of the things I like most about University of North Carolina at Charlotte is the diversity of the student body. Being surrounded by students from different backgrounds creates an environment where you can learn not just academically, but socially and culturally as well.
I also appreciate the supportive professors. Many of them are approachable, willing to answer questions, and genuinely care about student success.

I really like how UNCC prioritizes students and does its best to provide what students may need. I really like the professors and they have all been so amazing. The only thing that I would want UNCC to do better with is keeping its programs alive. The history and arts departments keep getting defunded or dropped, and a lot of students are suffering because of that.

I that the students and staff are friendly and helped a lot when during orientation to ensure you knew where you were going and next steps. The campus is big with lots of parking. The transition from the early college campus (high school) to main college campus was a breeze.

Overall, the campus is very nice with newer buildings and facilities. The university has many different groups and clubs available for one to explore and meet new people. The recreation center is top-notch and always busy. The food is good, but it may not be the healthiest option so be aware of what and how much you are eating. Also, make sure you are connected to know what times they are open. They have a bus system, but the campus is very walkable if you are ok with a lot of stairs. Weekends are often quiet because most upperclassmen live off-campus. Parking can be an issue if you commute. Professors are very approachable and want to help you succeed. You just have to put in the effort and stay on top of your assignments.

I've had a great experience at UNC Charlotte so far! With a very diverse community, over 400 student organizations, and a wide variety of academic programs to choose from, I feel as though UNC Charlotte has helped me discover my true identity and passions. Its a perfect mix of a big college life, while also not being overwhelming and "in your face". You can get a big "southern school" experience if you'd like, or a less intense, more casual experience.
I enjoy the University of North Carolina at Charlotte. I am in the ABSN program which is a 16 month program and I feel well supported. Professors are great !

My experience at UNC Charlotte has been one of growth, learning, and self-discovery. The campus community has provided opportunities to develop leadership skills, connect with diverse peers, and explore my academic interests. Through classes, student involvement, and campus resources, I’ve gained confidence and a clearer sense of my goals.
I love it. There is so much to do in the city, and the university provides so many fun events and resources.

ai_summary: The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill offers a dynamic and welcoming environment for students, highlighted by a strong sense of community and school spirit, particularly during sports events like basketball games. Academically, the university is recognized for its rigorous coursework and dedicated professors, with students noting the engaging and supportive teaching staff . While many appreciate the variety of majors and extracurricular activities available, some express challenges with particularly difficult programs, especially in STEM fields like chemistry and computer science. Students feel a mix of pride and stress, with many seeking ways to balance academic pressures with an active social life. The campus is described as beautiful and conveniently located within a vibrant college town , though there have been concerns about parking and the need for improved diversity initiatives. Overall, students seem to value their experiences at UNC, feeling equipped for future endeavors amid a rich academic and social landscape.

reviews.text: Attending the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill offers a top-tier "Public Ivy" experience that skillfully balances rigorous academics with a lively, world-class social scene. The campus beauty, centered around landmarks like the Old Well, creates an inspiring environment where students develop a lasting connection to the "Tar Heel" family. However, the intense academic pressure highlights the need for more accessible mental health resources and updated student housing to match the university’s prestigious reputation. While administrative transparency remains a concern, the vibrant energy of Franklin Street and the university’s focus on public service make it an exceptional place to grow. Ultimately, UNC provides a transformative education that prepares you for the world, as long as you’re willing to embrace its high standards and rich traditions.

The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill is more than a university—it’s a place with personality. From its strong sports culture to its academic intensity, UNC can feel overwhelming, especially for students from different backgrounds or those who did not grow up speaking English. I have felt that challenge myself, but I have learned that UNC is where you grow into who you are meant to be.
There is space at Carolina for everyone. You don’t have to love sports to belong—you just have to show up, work hard, and stay open to growth. The academic pressure is real, but it builds resilience.
What I value most is UNC’s sense of family. At the same time, we must continue to support students. Many face decisions that impact their mental health and need guidance—not judgment. As a future social worker, I am committed to being part of that support system and helping students grow through their challenges.

UNC-Chapel Hill is a one-of-a-kind university. It's one of the only places where you can find elite academics, competitive athletics, thriving student life, and a beautiful campus all in one place. Sure, the courses here can be difficult, but most professors truly care for their students and want to help them succeed. Something that UNC does a phenomenal job with is giving you connections for the rest of your life. Need an internship, a job, or a recommendation letter? A fellow Tar Heel will always be happy to help! The town of Chapel Hill is beautiful as well, providing many places where students can go and enjoy some well-deserved rest. Then, there is the price. Especially for in-state students, there is no better value for your money. My only complaints about the university would be the underwhelming on-campus dining halls and that there is limited, affordable off-campus housing. However, these things are just small blemishes on an overall beautiful university! Go Heels!

Very talented professors who are experts in their field, though the difficulty of classes differs by department. Beautiful campus but things like the gym and dining hall are too small for the increasing number of students.

I transferred into UNC Chapel Hill as a junior. I would say this has significantly affected my overall experience with the school. Coming in at the time I did, everyone my age already had their grounds, their friends, their clubs, and more. It was hard to integrate into these groups because they were pretty much set in stone and weren't looking to add anyone new. I also transferred here during one of the hardest academic years for most undergraduate students. This made it difficult to enjoy the "college scene" because I had to focus so much on my work that I couldn't have a social life. As a senior, it has gotten better now that I am able to be plugged into certain groups and know more people. The only thing I would say I don't have, or have missed out on, is having a core group of friends that I could do things with. I have different friends in different social circles, but I don't have my own friend group, which makes college very difficult.

I enjoyed most of my time at UNC, but I have to acknowledge how COVID added a sour spot for all college students. I will say campus life did the best it could despite that and I had mostly excellent professors. I would definitely say it’s a school you have to visit before you commit to because its environment encompasses the “college town” feel perfectly in my opinion. That’s not the atmosphere everyone is looking for though. Additionally if you value partying then we’re not the ideal place. It’s not horrible in that regard but I would not say it’s a strong part of the community.

The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill was a clique-y, snobby school with professors who did not really seem to care for their students. I have 1-2 memorable professors that I enjoyed class with, and that is because they did not care what the school thought of them. Many graduate requirements are superfluous and the students are lacking diversity. I took myself throughout all my class registration, even when seeking help from an advisor, they did not help me. I transferred in from Appalachian State so I was disappointed with this so-called "prestigious school," environment. Professors at my previous institution cared much more about students and their well-being.

The campus is beautiful, the courses are also rigorous, but there is a great sense of community. The growth over the past four years have allowed me to form connections that will inspire me in the future.

I loved my time at UNC! I lived off campus in Carrboro my junior and senior year and could not have asked for a better housing situation. The professors in my department were phenomenal, and I thoroughly enjoyed all of the upper-level classes I took. I wish the lower-level classes hadn't been so demanding - intro to chemistry and physics were brutal in particular. However, they are considered "weed out" classes for a reason! I had time to take ~15 credit hours each semester, work a part-time job, and still spend plenty of time out and about with my friends. I gained experience at UNC as an undergraduate learning assistant, undergraduate research assistant, physics tutor, and waitress at our local sports bar. My time as a student in Chapel Hill was exactly how I always imagined the "college experience" would be, and I feel amply prepared for the next step as I pursue a Doctorate in Physical Therapy from the Medical University of South Carolina!
Best school ever! If you attend UNC Chapel Hill you are a tar heel for life. The school spirit is unmatched!

I absolutely adore UNC. It is truly in my opinion the best place on earth. The people are so amazing and you can tell that everyone truly wants to be here and be successful. The campus is beautiful and the events and athletics are awesome.

Overall, my experience at UNC has been typical, I'd say. I have made new friends, tried new things, and I have been able to accomplish things I had never done before. I have grown quite a lot, and I'm only in my second semester as a sophomore. I haven't had any complaints so far. The school has handled most situations correctly and has acted quickly when it mattered.

From my experience as a student—especially in the business side at UNC Kenan-Flagler Business School—classes are collaborative, professors are accessible, and there’s a big culture of career preparation, networking, and internships. At the same time, UNC isn’t only about academics: sports, clubs, and campus traditions are a huge part of daily life, so it’s easy to build community if you’re willing to get involved.
I really enjoyed my time at UNC, but I would like to see the upper administration change. The classes and faculty are excellent, you won't find better elsewhere.

I come from a very small high school where I was able to know every person, their quirks, their stories, their issues, etc. It has been hard moving to UNC with the large student population. The academics have not been challenging thus far, but rather networking and competing with your fellow student is what makes it a challenging and rewarding university. P.S. dont be afraid of studying the humanities, we need them more than ever.

ai_summary: At the University of North Carolina - Greensboro (UNCG), students frequently highlight the vibrant and diverse campus community , which facilitates a welcoming atmosphere for individuals from all backgrounds. Many reviewers appreciate the supportive faculty , noting that professors are genuinely invested in student success and often go out of their way to provide help and engage with learners. Student life is described as dynamic, with numerous activities, clubs, and events, ensuring there is something for everyone. While the campus is praised for its beauty and accessibility , some reviewers expressed concerns about the quality of food options and the condition of certain dorms. Overall, UNCG stands out for its inclusive environment and commitment to providing a well-rounded educational experience, though it has room for improvements in specific areas like campus safety and weekend social engagement .

reviews.text: I went to Uncg for band camp before starting highschool. The experience was amazing with campus life, the food, as well as the diversity I was immersed into. This campus makes me feel wanted and opens new doors for me.

Attending University of North Carolina at Greensboro was a valuable experience for me, especially in terms of personal growth and exposure to different perspectives.
What I appreciated most was the diversity and sense of community on campus. It felt like a place where people from different backgrounds could come together and find their lane. The professors were generally supportive and willing to help, which made a difference when navigating coursework and figuring out my direction.
That said, one thing I would like to see improved is more career alignment and real-world preparation. While the education is solid, having clearer pipelines into jobs, internships, and post-grad opportunities would make the experience even stronger.
Overall, UNCG is a good school for students who are still figuring things out and want a supportive environment to grow, but it requires you to take initiative and really use the resources available.
I have been at UNCG for 3 years in the Social Work program. All of my social work professors have supported me, and provided me with the education I need.

UNCG is an amazing school, and it's truly the student body that makes it great, along with the people you learn to love along the way. I haven't had any bad experiences at the school so far, except for the housing situation. I genuinely dislike having to share a bathroom in the dorm hall. Other than that, it's a fantastic school. I also believe they could offer more programs in behavioral or forensic science.
Great diversity and so much academic support for students to succeed in all different classes. Students are able to find their place in college with all the different student organizations

I had a very nice and grateful experience at UNCG. The campus was well cleaned, many students and staff were nice, and the academics were good and up to date. Campus life was great as I was able to participate in different clubs such as Black Student Union, Prepping Advancing to Careers in Healthcare (PATCH), and even Biology research. Next, the campus did have its problems such as campus food not being the best, housing had problems such as mold, bugs, and thin walls. Also, some of the professors there did not prioritize students as they are supposed to, which caused some problems amongst students.
It’s only my freshman year so I’m still getting familiar with the campus. I am enjoying my time here thus far and finding activities I can be involved in.

My experience at UNC Greensboro has been positive. The campus is very welcoming and diverse; many professors are willing to help you succeed. They really care about students' success and also help outside of class time. I like that there are plenty of academic resources and student organizations to get involved in. However, I think communication through departments could be improved, specifically when it comes to housing and administrative processes; parking could also be frustrating at times. Still, UNCG provides a supportive environment for growth if you take advantage of what's offered. Another thing I like about the campus is its nice and walkable, and it feels like a safe environment overall. UNCG also does a great job at offering support services, which help balance academics and personal life. Overall, UNCG is a solid school with strong academic support and a welcoming atmosphere. While some issues could be improved, students who are motivated and take advantage of help do well

I have thoroughly enjoyed UNCG. I love the diversity, there are many helpful resources on campus, and lots of student groups/clubs to join if you want to make friends. The campus is beautiful and I love sitting out by the fountain during the warmer months.
