I was a transfer student from another university and it was the best decision I’ve ever made. I love UC

The University of the Cumberlands has a Christian background which aligns with my personal beliefs, however they do not force it onto other people. Plenty of multi ethnic people groups in my online classes. The system at UC is amazing where you are placed with a Student Coordinator who helps you choose your classes and keeps you up to date with the courses you need to fulfill your degree. They are very available and same for the professors. However, the one thing that I would like to see change is for them to introduce online student groups to cater toward the variety of online students. This would give students a connection point online, which many do not have in person. UC equips students with a purpose to better themself with the four short years you have there. Lastly, you can not beat the online price for UC. It is such a blessing to go to a college that is affordable, and caters towards the student experience while caring for the overall health of the individual.

One thing I like is that all of my instructors have been really great especially with my accommodations and their understanding. I feel like when there's lecture videos and when there's weekly videos with the instructor space on the screen going over content or explaining content to us that that creates more of an inclusion type feel and not all professors do that. There's been many that I've not even seen their face except for their picture beside their name on their email. I feel that lecture videos should be a requirement. A lot of people can read the material and get it and understand it but then there's a lot of people who like the interaction and I'm one of those people. Also financial aid that's another story. They make sure that they take their money right off the bat but you're stuck waiting on yours and waiting and waiting. There's been many times that I've had to get on the phone with financial aid or get a problem worked out do too issues with financial aid.

I am so grateful for the chance to earn my degree on line. I'm a stay at home mom, so it's perfect. I hope sometime soon I can gain employment to help out with the bills. My boys are both enrolled in public school system so I can work 1st shift somewhere. I've not yet figured out how the student pay to study program works but I'd like to find out more about it also.

University of the Cumberlands opened doors for me that I never thought would be possible. I was able to graduate with my associate degree sooner than expected because they allowed me to do everything online and take more classes than originally planned so I could get everything done before graduation date.

My experiences with the University of the Cumberlands have been overall very positive. The staff and instructors have always been helpful in answering questions and assisting with any problems related to registration, financial aid, or understanding the class material.

Campus is beautiful, but not a lot going on at all around town. Not much to do in Williamsburg you have to usually drive to Knox or Lexington to find something fun. professors and classes are great, but honestly sometimes there's really nothing to do. Food is okay, campus events are fun, night life is barely there

My experience at the University of the Cumberlands has been truly rewarding. I really appreciated the small class sizes, which allowed for meaningful interactions and one-on-one guidance from professors who genuinely cared about my success. The diverse range of programs offered helped me explore my interests and find my passion.
However, I believe there are areas for improvement. While the online learning platform is convenient, it sometimes lacks an intuitive design that would make navigating courses easier. Additionally, I would like to see more opportunities for student engagement outside the classroom, including clubs and events that cater to various interests. These changes could enhance the social aspect of campus life, making it even more vibrant. Overall, I am grateful for my time at the University of the Cumberlands and would encourage others to explore it for their educational journey.

Straightforward enrollment process. Everyone at the university is willing to help at a moment's notice. They listen to your circumstances, and it's almost like they tailor the situation so your problem becomes an instant solution. It was one of my easiest and most pleasant college experiences!

It is one of the cheapest schools you could find yet still very nice.
It's clean and we'll kept up. There is great community here.
It's a perfect school for those who aren't into the party scene.
It’s been very good, the people are friendly but it is a smaller campus, and not many parties. The food isn’t anything special but it’s been a good place to learn and met friends plus it’s cheap
I have loved every minute of studying with this school. They are most of the time helpful and always want the best for each student.

I have attended online classes through the Cumberlands for my entire undergrad degree, and now my Master's program as well. I have enjoyed my experience here and would absolutely recommend the school to anyone looking! They have a great support system for their students and I have had nothing but positive interactions with all of my professors.

ai_summary: The University of the Incarnate Word (UIW) is noted for its close-knit community and personalized learning experience , where smaller class sizes foster strong connections between students and professors. Many reviews highlight the supportive faculty , who are described as approachable and invested in students' success, which enhances academic engagement. While the campus offers a variety of activities and events to help students socialize and build relationships, some students feel the social scene could be improved, particularly in terms of fostering community among different majors. However, the university's commitment to diversity and its welcoming atmosphere are frequently praised. On the downside, reviewers often mention the high tuition costs and limited financial aid options , urging prospective students to seek scholarships. Overall, UIW stands out for its supportive environment, academic focus, and the unique experiences it offers to students.

reviews.text: I love the campus, staff and professors, everything is really good and the classes are small so you can have a better experience in classroom for learning

I am a high school senior considering UIW for college. From what I’ve researched, the school offers strong academic programs and a supportive environment for students. The campus seems welcoming, with lots of clubs and activities to get involved in. I like that UIW values diversity and community, which makes it feel inclusive.
It is a wonderful school at all levels. I recommend to anyone to join us. Every aspect of the quality is outstanding.

The University of the Incarnate Word is a very very expensive school. Academically, the professors have been great and look out for the best interest of the students. It’s worth the money but not everyone has it.

My experience at the University of the Incarnate Word (UIW) has been incredibly positive. The small class sizes and supportive professors have made learning personal and engaging, and I’ve felt truly valued as a student. The campus community is welcoming and diverse, creating a comfortable environment to grow both academically and personally. UIW also offers great opportunities for involvement, from service projects to student organizations, which helped me build strong connections and leadership skills. Overall, my time at UIW has been rewarding, inspiring, and a major part of my personal and professional growth.
The University of the Incarnate word has been a wonderful experience so far. The professors are incredibly helpful, the students feel welcoming, and the campus is extremely beautiful.

I've had a great time at Incarnate Word; the campus is lovely, yet it's small, so finding buildings and classes is simple. Because the classrooms are smaller, it's also easier to establish friends, which is great if you're a bit introverted because it may be a little challenging to work with or be in a large school. The amount of work is acceptable, but this is true at all universities. The instructors are really kind and supportive. On campus, there are a lot of events and activities that promote social interaction and the development of relationships among students. For us students, the majority of the activities are free, which is convenient if you're a penniless college student. The food on campus is decent; there are a couple restaurants and a dining hall, but entry requires a meal plan card, which is a little inconvenient. I would recommend that you look into it when searching for universities to apply to because it is a pleasant, soothing, and supportive institution.
Being a student at UIW is great. Everyone is very welcoming and it’s easy to make friends. The setting is nice and very well taken care of.

I have enjoyed my experience so far at UIW, during high school I participated in the blended program which allowed me to experience college life while still in high school. I believe this helped me sustainably because I was able to get familiarized myself on what to expect prior to being full time college student.
As a current student of UIW I have it to be very exciting and diverse when it comes to activities and classes.

I am a transfer student who will be attending UIW in the fall. I was accepted into the ABSN nursing program. So far, my experience with UIW has been great. The admissions process has been very smooth. I also have an advisor who is assigned to be who has been very helpful, involved, and encouraging in the process thus far.

I greatly enjoyed the growing environment that is diverse and beautiful. The campus itself is gorgeous and the dorms are fairly beautiful as well and carry all of the accommodations. The students as well as professors are kind and willing to help. The community you get here is something so amazing.
It’s a great place great people great community and awesome team of leaders could have not asked for a better place to have gone.

My first year at UIW was a great experience. The supportive community, caring professors, and beautiful campus really stood out. I made great friends, had a strong track season, enjoyed the dining hall food, and overall had a positive start. I’d love to see more student events and chances to connect outside of class.

My experience at the University of the Incarnate Word has been delegating. As a Fashion Management major with a Business Administration minor, I’ve developed my creativity and strategy, which are important in the fashion industry. I appreciate the hands-on projects, supportive faculty, and connections with professionals that have shaped my goals and boosted my confidence.
As a Latina, I appreciate how UIW makes me feel like I belong. It encourages me to celebrate my background and highlights the value of diversity and representation.
I believe we should create more chances for students to connect with industry professionals early in the program. This would help them gain practical experience and build their networks sooner, giving them a head start in their careers.
Overall, I’m proud of my progress & excited for the future. UIW has provided a strong foundation, and I’m motivated to continue learning and making an impact in the fashion world.

ai_summary: At the University of the Pacific, reviews emphasize a mix of strong academic support and community, particularly through small class sizes that foster personalized relationships with professors. Many students appreciate the accessible and friendly nature of instructors, which contributes to a supportive learning environment . However, there are contrasting views on administrative efficiency, with some students reporting difficulties with services like housing and financial aid. The campus itself is noted for its beauty, though there's criticism about the social scene and limited extracurricular activities. While some students feel the tuition is high for the experience offered, many still find value in the connections and opportunities within their specific academic fields, particularly in pre-professional programs . Overall, the University of the Pacific presents a unique environment that blends academic rigor with a close-knit community feel, although there are areas for improvement in student services and campus life.

reviews.text: Good and straightforward instructions as a new graduate student. Welcoming grad program personnel and happy people to talk to about the Master of Education department. Easy online access to the student portal. Interesting campus with an inclusive environment with diversity in a good, which makes me feel comfortable as a student.

The University Campus is beautiful and very well kept by staff. For the most part, professors are helpful and approachable, wanting students to have their best foot forward, though there are a naturally a few outliers as there would be anywhere. Students are generally kind and respectful as well and advisors are very helpful.

I love the diversity, and all the programs here. Students are generally happy and professors are very accessible and rooting for you to succeed.
The housing is ok, the biggest drawback about UOP is the food is not good and very expensive. Especially if you're vegan.

Beautiful school, beautiful campus. Every professor wants you to succeed. There are so many ways the school helps you succeed, the truly want the best for you. Every class I've taken at that school has been very interesting and organized. I love everything about being a Tiger!

My experience at the University of the Pacific has changed the way I've seen other people. Whether it is in the classroom or on the athletic fields/courts, everyone seems to have their own way of going through their lives.
Honestly, I grew up here so I may be a kittle bit biased, but honestly this place is extremely nice and so beautiful compared to most other campuses.
Great school with small classes and very personable professors. It is in Stockton so the surrounding area can be sketchy, but overall, UOP is a school of great opportunities.
My program offers a lot of opportunities and chances to put ourselves out there, but I wish the cafeteria food and housing is better.
It was a difficult time being at the school. It is so competitive and hard to adjust to, students are very competitive.

I really like the overall vibes of the campus, full of friendly people and lots of beautiful trees. I would like to see changes in the food options and housing quality. Not many halal choices and some dorms are looked after less than others.
My overall experience at the University of the Pacific was great! The pre-pharmacy program that I'm in is managed well and the campus life is awesome.

University of The Pacific is academically a great school. These are small classes and most professors are passionate about what they teach and want to help you achieve your goals. However, if you are looking for a school that has that party scene, big school spirit, and athleticism you won’t find that here. If you just want access to a good education and don’t care too much for student life it’s a good fit. Stockton itself is very lacking on things to do so you might want to invest in a car so you can explore other places.
I feel as if the professors do not care about the students and lack any empathy towards the students, especially those who wish to pursue dentistry or pharmacy

I love the University of Pacific. During my three years there I really developed into the young women I am today.
My career path of Speech Language Pathology and the professor I had inspire me to do more for the world. I had such a supportive community in my academics and outside.
The students and people I met were always so kind and welcoming. The University for the most part does what it can for their students and always try to meet their students needs.

My time at UOP was good until it became traumatic. Hard to get ahold of anyone on campus except the police. Admissions, Registrars Office and Financial Aid office either would not pickup the phone or went in circles emailing. I got tired of being so stressed out at such a young age from this place. I wish I had a more positive experience but I would be lying. The food was good, the professors were very hard/strict. The communication department was very organized and it felt like they cared about you. Everything at this school was directed straight towards athletes at my time of being there. They solely focused on them.

ai_summary: Utah State University (USU) offers a supportive and vibrant academic environment , where many students appreciate the approachable and caring nature of the professors . The campus is praised for its beautiful setting in Logan and the proximity to various outdoor activities. Student life is lively, with numerous events, clubs, and a strong sense of community that helps students connect with one another. While the university provides a range of resources , including mental health services and academic support, some students note challenges like limited parking, issues with housing, and a desire for more diversity and event options. Overall, USU seems to foster a friendly atmosphere conducive to a well-rounded college experience , despite some logistical hurdles.

reviews.text: I loved Utah State! The campus like was great, I loved my classes, and all the professors seemed invested in my future and helping me succeed.

I'm a real big fan of the university in general. The student life and campus are exceptional. The professors are the kindest people I've met, and my fellow students are the cherry on top for my experience. Overall, I couldn't ask for a better university experience than this, and that is why Utah State University was, is, and will always be my first option.

I have absolutely loved my time here at Utah State. This is my second semester, and I have been absolutely amazed by how involved and helpful my academic advisor and professors are. I would recommend this university to anybody I know.
USU is a great environment for people who are interested in getting to know the local community. I would recommend it to mainly agriculture kids, but anyone is welcome!
Utah State has been really good. The professors have always been very helpful and are willing to work with you. There are also many opportunities to get the help that you need in your classes.

I was concurrently enrolled in Hist. 1700 American Civilizations, it was a broadcast class and the professor knew extremely little on her subject and was absent a large majority of the time. The course was incredibly shallow and offered extremely little student involvement/engagement.

I liked about 50% of my professors who seemed to be fair in grading, balanced in their methods, and relatively unbiased in their theoretical perspective. I do think there were too many woke initiatives on campus and in the name of diversity, many students, professors, and programs were openly racist towards white and south east asian people at the school. I also struggled in my program in particular which I won't say, but it was in the Humanities college. Some of the curriculum had nothing to do with the profession and wasn't valuable, but rather very distracting. A few professors were terribly incompetent and didn't appear to actually know the things they were teaching. Over all a good experience, but the constant political brainwashing from certain elements on campus and in the curriculum, made it a deeply frustrating experience. College should make you uncomfortable and should challenge you, much of which was accomplished as expected at the school, but there is a line.
Good people and professors. Lots of campus activities and life. Lots of nice people with good food. Campus is well kept and there are lots of new amenities.

Utah State is a great University! I love going here, and I'm excited to continue!
There are multiple events throughout the year on campus. The professors I have had so far have taught well.

I think Utah State University is an incredible school for those who want a full four-year quality education on a safe, family friendly campus. I often see children and infants with their student parent/parents on campus in between classes or while they study. I personally believe USU’s family friendly activities and community, as well as the stunning natural scenery and outdoor activity options, make it perfect for athletic, outdoorsy, family, friendly type students who prefer smaller class sizes and more focused attention from the professors. On the flip side, it would be an understatement to say that the social scene is lacking. Many students, including myself find ourselves attending activities we have no interest in just to meet and interact with other students in a social setting. I would be thrilled to see more diversity on campus as well as more diverse events to encourage social interactions across larger groups and interests.
I have several great professors at Utah State University and am receiving a valuable education at this university.

Utah State has been a great fit for me! Campus is surrounded my towering mountains and breathtaking views. It a great place to be for a nature lover! I have enjoyed working with all of the staff, especially my professors, they are all so understanding and accommodating. The safety and vibe of campus and the surrounding town makes me feel like i'm in a hallmark movie.

Utah State University is a great school. I have loved my professors in my degree area, and they are all very knowledgable in their respective areas. They are willing to work with their students and really care for your success. USU is also very well-known for having one of the greatest students sections at all of their athletic events!

I love the community here at Utah state. It’s definitely not the typical go to “party” college, but I definitely think it’s a great community to be in. Full of familiar and friendly faces. A small town college at best!

Utah State University is situated on a beautiful campus, with lovely greenery everywhere you look. Applying to the college is fairly simple, and even applying for classes has been somewhat streamlined through an online process. That being said, their online presence could use some work. Some professors for the lower-level classes are also clearly overworked, meaning that they do not keep up very well with grading and the like. Ideally, instead of taking higher-level professors and adding more classes for them to teach, Utah State University leadership could hire more qualified staff so that students can get their money's worth from their learning.

ai_summary: Valdosta State University offers a welcoming environment with a strong sense of community , largely thanks to its diverse student body and supportive faculty. Many students appreciate the smaller class sizes , which foster closer connections with professors who are described as friendly and dedicated to student success. Academic programs, particularly in Health Science and Psychology, receive positive feedback for their quality, while campus life is enriched by a variety of extracurricular activities and events. However, some students report issues with housing conditions and parking challenges. Overall, VSU is seen as a place where students can build lasting friendships and enjoy a unique college experience , though the surrounding town may offer limited entertainment options.

reviews.text: I never attended this school before i'm just a high school student but i am more interested in the football program because i seen the coaches there and how the team plays and i also seen what other programs they have their for academics and i founded great enough for me to attend there.

For me personally I like this school. They have many opportunities for undergrads research and a counseling program for graduates which I plan on attending. I also love that they have lots of abroad programs for all classes. And all of my teachers were very nice and pretty understanding and taught me well. And it is nice to have an advisor that actually cares and allows you to pick your own classes but still helps you to structure your academic life. Also, with housing though it may not be the best it is quite fun to live in the dorms with a roommate. And though the girls may not be super clean the janitors do an excellent job with cleaning it up. And lastly as for as the student life which I truly appreciate because they have things to do all the time for the students and teachers. Typically, everything is fun and entertaining as well as you can win things from the events. I also love how VSU is open but provides a lot of protection always there when you call to help.

I love it. So excited to be at Valdosta State, such a great college to attend. My father passed away June 30, 2017, from a stroke. So my mother is a 62 yr old widow. I need all the scholarships I can get. If I cant get a scholarship or loan. I will have to go back home. Trying to do everything to stay.

Valdosta State University is a very diverse school with a rather safe environment. It is filled with various students organizations and clubs giving students an plenty of opportunities to be involved on campus.

As a sophomore at Valdosta University, I've stepped outside my comfort zone by joining clubs and trying to be more involved but it still doesn't feel like I'm at "home." In some ways, it felt like high school all over again. That realization showed me I need an environment that pushes me further—socially, academically, and personally. Valdosta has ignited my curiosity to branch out and apply to Georgia Tech in Atlanta. This has always been my mindset: I feel capable of more, and I want to be challenged. That drive has only grown stronger, and I believe I am ready for the next step. As a pre-med student, I can see myself learning at the intersection of engineering and medicine, gaining research habits I’ll carry into medical school. I am drawn to an environment that challenges me, connects me with innovative thinkers, and helps me grow into the person I aspire to become. I am ready to become a Yellow Jacket because I know Georgia Tech is where I belong and that's thanks to VSU.
So far I’m having a good time making friends and exploring the campus and area around me. The campus is constantly offering new events and resources for the sudents and I really appreciate it.
Amazing college!! Very friendly , warm, and welcoming!! Food is always on point . The staff are very helpful.
it is a very friendly environment, however certain days the food in the dining hall is sub par and some of the dormitory buildings have problems like mold and bugs

I worked hard and did what I was supposed too. I watched numerous professors overlook and judge me. I watched literal bullies be enabled and supported. This university experience showed how judgemental some "doctors" can be. I literally went to my classes and was continuously held to different standards than my classmates. I did not go and sucker a Professor into changing grades so they can get a job. To be honest, go to school online otherwise they may belittle you and destroy your confidence as a student. The amount of judgement was horrible. The lack of support and acknowledgement was worse. This is a exact example of why the university system is a joke these days because of all yhe private businesses. I literally was NEVER even given a chance to succeed. I am low key embarrassed to be from Valdosta State and REALLY wish I did not go there. That is now past thinking, but I literally have not been able to settle into my past jobs due to judgement of my university.
It was harder to connect as a commuter student so a person that lives on or around campus may have different experience.

I thoroughly enjoyed Valdosta State. It was my first time moving far away from my home but I loved the environment so much that I was not even home-sick. I loved the campus life and all the clubs and activities students can do. It was definitely a place where you always have something to do! I enjoyed it and I would 100% go back to live my undergraduate life again. I would choose Valdosta State in every lifetime.

Valdosta State University is a good school. The advisors are really great and helpful so you can strive for greatness. It is a more decent, affordable school unlike my last college I transferred from due to too much financial stress.
The college itself is very strong academically; however, the location and student life are definitely subpar and need work.

My overall experience at Valdosta State University was good and fun. My favorite part was the campus life at VSU it was always active, with a range of clubs, organizations, and sports events. As a student at VSU it gave me opportunities to socialize, develop leadership skills, and build relationships with others. Another great experience for me was the parties, VSU party life is a lively mix of on-campus and off-campus events, offering something for everyone. From high-energy gatherings at local bars and clubs to themed parties hosted by Greek life and other organizations, there's always something exciting happening. Overall, Valdosta State University aims to provide comprehensive college experience. By balancing academic rigor with social engagement, VSU prepares students for future success in their chosen fields while fostering a sense of community and belonging.
I haven't gone yet but I will in the fall and have heard great things from current students. I am looking forward to the future that is there.

ai_summary: Valparaiso University offers a mix of academic strengths and community spirit, highlighted by its small class sizes and dedicated professors who genuinely care about their students' success. Many appreciate the supportive environment and close-knit community , where students can easily form friendships and get involved in various clubs and organizations. The university is recognized for specific programs like Nursing, Engineering, and Meteorology, which have received positive mentions for their hands-on experiences and dedicated faculty. However, concerns about tuition costs, limited social life, and inconsistent food quality have been common themes, with several students expressing discomfort with the increasing expenses and the university's management of resources. Overall, Valpo is viewed as a welcoming place with a beautiful campus , though some students have voiced issues regarding diversity and administrative responsiveness.

reviews.text: It has good professors who mostly actually care about you. Dorms are so expensive and not worth it. The food is okay but there's only 1 dining hall and it gets boring after a while.
I love Valparaiso, the professors are extremely personal and attentive to each student. I feel so much safer here than I did in high school.

I took a tour of the Valpo campus, and I loved it. My tour guide told me all the secrets of campus life like we were long-lost friends. Valpo seems like the perfect fit for those who are academically focused.

Freshman at VU and couldn’t be happier with my choice. Support from faculty and staff has been excellent. Small class sizes with professors want you to succeed. There is always something going on at the campus, so many organizations and clubs to get involved with. Love it here!

Absolutely disgusted by a recent event in the PA program. Complete lack of communication and common sense. I definitely would encourage PA students to go elsewhere since this program can’t be bothered to do their jobs as administrators.

Amazing experience as a student and came back as a professor. Faculty really care about the students and the graduates make a significant impact in there fields. Known for their interdisciplinary majors along with engineering, meteorology, nursing, etc.

I'm currently pursuing my MBA at Valparaiso University, and my experience so far has been excellent. The program is flexible, thoughtfully designed, and led by professors who are incredibly supportive and responsive. I appreciate how the curriculum balances core business fundamentals with real-world application, especially in areas like leadership, strategy, and ethics.
Classes are offered in an online format (as well as in person), which has made it easier to manage my coursework alongside a full-time job. The small class sizes also mean more interaction with faculty and peers, which has added a lot of value to my learning.
If you're looking for a program that offers personal attention, practical skill development, and a strong sense of community, I highly recommend Valpo’s MBA.

I've been working at Valpo for about two years and I absolutely love it. Our campus community is the embodiment of "Midwest nice"; every department across campus works together to give our students a unique college experience, and finds great joy in doing so. Between a variety of academic programs, research opportunities, student clubs, a bustling downtown, and proximity to landmarks like Chicago and the Indiana Dunes National Park, there's a place for every student here on our campus.

As both a Valparaiso University alumna and a current MBA student, I’ve experienced firsthand how welcoming and supportive this community is. From my undergraduate years to now, faculty and staff have gone out of their way to help me succeed and feel a true sense of belonging. The application process was straightforward, and communication was always clear — which makes such a difference for students navigating their next steps. Valpo truly invests in its students, both academically and personally, making it a place where you can grow, thrive, and feel at home.

Working at Valparaiso University has been such a rewarding experience. The campus is beautiful year-round—whether it’s the vibrant fall leaves or the peaceful walking paths in spring, it’s a lovely place to come to work each day. The town of Valparaiso itself offers a welcoming, tight-knit community with great local shops, restaurants, and a charming atmosphere. What stands out most, though, is the kindness and authenticity of the faculty and staff. People here genuinely care about one another and, above all, about the students we serve. It’s a place where meaningful work and meaningful relationships go hand in hand.

I loved my time at Valpo. The people there are so kind, warm, and truly want the students to succeed. My relationships with my professors helped shape my career goals and I value being able to reach out to them to this day. I love the idea that Valpo is preparing students for a vocation – even if your career path doesn't follow the plan you originally had yourself, you'll have experience with leadership, service, and community that will shape how you live your life post-graduation.
Christ College was a life-changing learning experience. Even though I didn't end up working in the humanities, I'm constantly applying the philosophies and concepts I learned about through CC in my everyday life. Being a CC Scholar was probably the best part of my experience at Valpo.

As a recent graduate and now a proud member of the university staff, I can confidently say that Valpo offers a one-of-a-kind experience. The small class sizes and engaged faculty made my academic journey incredibly personal and hands on. Professors knew me by name, encouraged me to explore my interests, and helped me develop both hard and soft skills that directly prepared me for my future career. But Valpo is more than just academics. It’s a place where I found community, leadership opportunities, and lifelong friendships. From student organizations to campus events, I was constantly supported and encouraged to grow as a leader and as a person. Some of my closest friends to this day are people I met at Valpo, and that speaks volumes about the kind of connections and support system this university fosters. Valpo isn't just where I went to school, it's where I found my voice, my people, and my path.

My time at Valparaiso University has been transformative — both academically and personally. As a biology major on the pre-physical therapy track, I’ve been challenged in the classroom and supported every step of the way by professors who genuinely care about my success. The academic environment here has pushed me to think critically, manage my time effectively, and develop a deeper understanding of the human body and its potential to heal.

Overall, my experience at Valparaiso University has been positive and meaningful. One of the things I appreciated most was the strong sense of community—both professors and classmates were generally supportive, and I felt like I mattered as a student. The smaller class sizes made it easier to build connections with faculty and receive personalized attention, which was a big plus.
Academically, the university offers a solid curriculum, especially in accounting, and I appreciated the focus on ethical leadership and service, which is woven into many programs. There were also plenty of student organizations and activities, and campus life felt engaging and welcoming.
Overall, I’m grateful for my time at Valpo. It’s a great fit for students looking for a smaller, values-centered university with a strong academic core and supportive environment.
I like it here it’s really for the ones who like medium size schools everyone gets along but I don’t really care for some of the professors who act like they care about your academics

ai_summary: Vanderbilt University offers a vibrant college experience characterized by a strong academic reputation and an engaging social scene . Many students appreciate the collaborative atmosphere fostered by friendly peers and dedicated professors, who often maintain open-door policies. The campus itself, located in the lively city of Nashville, is frequently noted for its beauty and the multitude of activities available, from cultural events to sports. However, some reviews highlight concerns about the dining options, housing quality, and the influence of Greek life on the social environment, which can sometimes feel cliquey or exclusive. While a majority find the academics to be challenging yet rewarding, some mention a lack of support for underprivileged students and a perceived disconnect between administration and student needs. Overall, students seem to value the unique blend of rigorous academics, supportive faculty, and a thriving social life that Vanderbilt provides.

reviews.text: My four years at Vanderbilt University have been an incredibly enriching academic and personal experience. As a student in Peabody, I have been grateful to have learned from professors who are not only deeply knowledgeable in their fields but who also genuinely invest in the success and growth of their students. Beyond the classroom, Vanderbilt offers a whole variety of student organizations that cater to every interest imaginable. This variety has allowed me to grow well beyond my major and connect with peers who share my passions and hobbies.

Attending Vanderbilt University was the best decision I have ever made. I would not be where I am today without the mentorship and opportunities the university has provided me. Vanderbilt is also where I made lifelong friends and memories I will never forget. At Vanderbilt, I was able to live a balanced work hard, play hard lifestyle, which I think is a testament to the kind of community the university works to foster. I could not recommend this university more. Anchor Down!

I would not choose to attend Vanderbilt University again. Financial aid is generous compared to many schools, but packages can change yearly and may require appeals. Academics are rigorous and you will learn a lot, but some professors are not very supportive of students from weaker academic backgrounds. Housing is decent for underclassmen and better for upperclassmen if placed in a residential college. The culture is “work hard, play hard,” with strong academics and a big party scene, which may not suit everyone. I also noticed social cliques and very limited inclusivity. Grade deflation, especially in STEM, can hurt GPAs and graduate school prospects. Overall, I believe college fit and support matter more than prestige. Vanderbilt University appears focused on achieving T10 or Ivy status, and in my view, that ambition may be hurting the student experience.

This school was great. I went there all four years of my undergrad. I made great friends and had great experiences with all my professors. The night life is great and being in nashville there is so much opportunity. 100% recommend.
I have loved my experience at Vanderbilt. I love how there is a balance between having fun and being a student while also taking academically rigorous courses.

I have only been at Vanderbilt for one semester but I love it here. The professors truly want you to succeed and I have made incredible relationships here. Can't wait to see what the next four years bring.

Would not change a thing. I could not be happier with my decision to end up here. It's not easy to find this good of a school or this fun of a city. Vanderbilt is the unique balance very other schools can truly compete with.

I am an online doctoral student of Vanderbilt's Peabody College. While my program is primarily online, we meet a few times in person on campus. Vanderbilt is a beautiful intersection of history and tradition while blazing a trail into the future. The campus itself is stunning, the students are wonderful and the professors are outstanding.

As an online learner that lives near campus, the university goes out of their way to be accessible and accommodating. I have been able to access libraries to study, in-person meetings with professors, and consistent help from administration. I have enjoyed my time so far and am excited to complete my degree, and hopefully apply for a J.D. with this school as well.
Vanderbilt is amazing! The academics are top tier with great research as well. Also, the social life is really underrated as well.

While on campus, I really enjoyed the classes I have taken and the professor do a great job of passing on the content. It is close to outside amenities and it is easy to get around using the bus system even though it takes a while to get used to it. All in all the staff is really helpful when a student comes to them with a concern. I am very grateful I can join this school in the fall.

Overall, Vanderbilt is a great school and has provided me with so many great opportunities. If I had to go through the college application process again, I would choose Vanderbilt. The academics are hard but there is a lot of support on campus. One big setback for me was the lack of diversity on campus and trying to find where I fit in. After a semester or two, I finally found my place through clubs and organanizations on campus.
Vanderbilt focuses on the wellbeing of its students, and they provide us with various resources to get through any challenges. It is up to the students to take those opportunities and utilize them.

Wonderful rigor and community; I initially wanted to transfer out but now I don't feel as inclined to do so anymore! Some professors can give you a hard time, and course registration is quick and competitive, but just be organized and you'll be fine!
Honestly my time at Vanderbilt has been amazing. The education, lifestyle, and overall experience have been eye opening and definitely extremely enjoyable.

ai_summary: Villanova University is praised for its strong academic programs and supportive faculty , who are often described as accessible and eager to help students succeed. Many reviews highlight the close-knit community atmosphere , where students feel a sense of belonging and camaraderie. While there are plenty of extracurricular opportunities available, including clubs and community service, some students note that the social scene can be challenging for those not involved in Greek life, leading to feelings of isolation . Additionally, reviews frequently mention the need for improvement in campus food options and highlighted concerns about diversity and the elitist social environment , which can make fitting in difficult for some students. Overall, Villanova offers a rigorous academic experience paired with a welcoming community, but students should be prepared for certain social dynamics and the overall costs associated with attendance.

reviews.text: A pleasant experience once I found my community within the greater population. Starting in 2020 (COVID era), the university and Father Peter did a good job of maintaining a sense of community and a commitment to staying on campus. The core curriculum classes that are required for CLAS students were not that bad in hindsight. Overall campus vibe was mainly upbeat and positive with something always going on either at the Oreo, Conn or other campus green areas.

Great people, great place, great overall school. As a civil engineering sophomore, I have some things to look forward to in my life post graduation, as Villanova has already helped me land my 2nd internship this upcoming summer. The people are really friendly, there's so many activities, clubs, and organizations to get involved with on campus. People who give it the Villa "no-fun" label simply haven't put themselves out there enough, if you try hard enough you will 100% meet your people. School is no joke, you got to put focus on your academic, but with enough effort I'm sure anyone talented enough to get in is more than capable of having success. The basketball team is finally good again too, Villanova to the moon!

My experience at Villanova University was truly outstanding. I have been to the campus twice; one for a campus tour and another for Junior Preview Day. The environment had such a positive and welcoming feeling. I really hope to see myself there as a student one day.
terrible. i cannot believe that i wasted so much of my precious time at this university. the majority of people are undeniably clicky and miserable with a long long long stick up thier rear end.

I valued my experiences at Villanova. The professors were great and engaging. The staff throughout the university cares about making your time there memorable. Diversity was the biggest downside, but I did notice it improve throughout my years there.
Villanova is such a great school with a strong tight nit community and student life. The only downside is that it has low diversity and we are required to take religion class.
I have not transferred to Villanova yet. I went on a tour. Everyone else's nice and welcoming. I wanted to go there because it has a strong accounting program.

Villanova has been the greatest gift I've received. It is rare to experience such strong community which supports a person not only during their undergraduate experience but throughout life. My connections to alumni remain strong and the spiritual lessons I gained from professors continue to enrich my life regularly. Villanova is more than an education, it is a home.

If you are not preppy, rich, and from the general area you will not fit in here. i’m from the midwest and a wealthy enough family to afford full tuition and sometimes i even feel like an alien in this community. professors have been a mixed bag but i appreciate the small class sizes. greek life here is prevalent and parties are limited and lame. the campus is nothing special, most buildings are nice on the outside but pretty outdated and ugly inside with a few exceptions. there’s nice green spaces but they go almost entirely unused by students. food and dorms is notably bad but some upperclassmen housing seems nicer. the catholic culture isn’t too in your face but it’s there if you want it. the area is nice but there’s nothing really to do unless you wanna take a 10-20 minute train/go to philly. there’s no diversity both in important issues like race background etc but also diversity of opinion and self expression-everyone dresses and talks the same and it’s suffocating and boring.

Villanova has been a fun and empowering experience with lots of challenges when it comes to academics and balancing workload. I have taken away a lot from all of my experiences and wouldn't change them for anything.
Taking Summer classes and the atmosphere is amazing and the student body is awesome. Great professors and advisors. Ease of appointment and accessing resources is easy
Great University with many opportunities for students to be involved with academics and extra curricular activities as well.

Now that I’ve left Villanova, and attended multiple other colleges for prerequisite courses for graduate school, I realize just how much about it I took for granted. The campus is extremely safe, besides being beautiful and well-maintained. The faculty are the best, they genuinely care about your success, are intelligent, and the classes are extremely digestible (minus occasional classes like organic chemistry). The students are pretty preppy, vineyard vines and having summer houses out east are relatively common. There aren’t many creative spirits, but people are overall very polite – though some can be a bit snobby. I was involved with a lot of clubs as well as volunteer work, which the school is very big on. Nova has a reputation for not being very diverse, but my friend group was at least half international students, so I can’t say that that was my experience. If you’re Christian, it’s a great place to get closer with your faith, but it’s not shoved down your throat if you're not.
School is great. Tons of activities to supplemental academic work. Expensive tuition, difficult courses, but great education.

Excellent education at Villanova with outstanding professors and peers who created a thriving community. I turned down two Ivies for Villanova (not a positive reaction from the parents at the time). Joining this community with foundational Augustinian principles was right for me. High expectations but not an extreme Pressure Cooker so I had time to enjoy college life and not just a college grind. Professors care. Villanova courses are top tier and set you on a path for success. I am projected to graduate from the one Ivy with my JD. Go NOVA! Be a Wildcat!

ai_summary: Virginia Commonwealth University stands out for its vibrant, diverse atmosphere and urban campus situated in the heart of Richmond. Students often highlight the inclusivity of the community, making it a welcoming place for everyone, particularly first-generation and low-income students. Academically, many appreciate the variety of programs offered and the supportive nature of most professors, although experiences with faculty can be mixed; some instructors are well-regarded while others may pose challenges in their teaching methods. Student life is enriched by the blending of campus and city culture, providing countless extracurricular activities , clubs, and dining options, though there are notable concerns about the quality of food and housing shortages. Overall, VCU is characterized as a place where students can explore their interests and thrive in a dynamic and friendly setting.

reviews.text: I really like that my professors have experience in the fields they are teaching in. I would like if there were less online class options and more in person options.

I had a wonderful experience at Virginia Commonwealth University. Though I originally struggled to find friends, I attended many clubs and campus events, which eventually allowed me to form the relationships I sought. I had good experiences with my professors, they all seemed to want to provide a good educational experience. I also benefited a lot from the free peer tutoring that VCU offers. Further, the Richmond area is bustling and there was always something to do, which I really enjoyed. I loved my time there.

VCU is in a beautiful city with lots of access to nature, restaurants and fun activities. The university has a great reputation for being a top school for research, and it has a lot of opportunities to students to get research experience. The classes are taught by professors who care about their students academics. The school also cares about students mental health. VCU Basketball is also very fun part of the school social life.

VCU is an urban campus and your experience as a student since it has a large commuter population but is also fully within the city and not sectioned off as some urban campuses are. I found that by involving myself in campus, I was able to have a much better experience than those who did not. The living learning communities are a great way to get volunteer hours, meet other students, guarantee housing to avoid the upperclassmen lottery but also get the choose classes first. I can't speak for other majors but in the psychology and gender studies programs I found that most professors were on the younger side, flexible, and willing to meet for office hours. Overall, I found my experience at VCU to be well rounded and the coursework to be fairly rigorous.
Very accepting and homey. It's fairly easy to get around, and it offers plenty of study opportunities and spaces.
Great diversity where everyone fits in. The classes are perfect for my field of interest. I finally feel at home there.

I enjoy the ease of walking around campus, particularly in normal weather and when living in a dorm. The free RVA bus system is also a plus if you want to go downtown. I would like to see more food options or cheaper/lower meal swipe plans for those living in a first-year dorm.
I would say that it was a great college for my major which was Biomedical Engineering. Good clubs and social life.
I like the fact that the school is in the city. Some would disagree, but everything is close by and a walkable distance.
The best part of VCU is the location.I love exploring the city when I'm not studying. I am always able to find something new to do.

It’s been my first week here and I absolutely love it! I’ve gotten the amazing chance to get an early glimpse of the campus, and I can only say, I am extremely excited to live my first year here. I really only worry about the community/ students not properly taking care of this environment we have, to me, this space will be a form of escapism. Other than that, there’s nothing else to mention, besides the bomb food!
I have enjoyed my experience at VCU so far. The atmosphere is nice, good community, resources, and plenty of clubs and activities.

I have really enjoyed my first semester at Virginia Commonwealth University. My experience at VCU has been both challenging and rewarding. I have enjoyed the diversity on campus, as it allows me to meet people from different backgrounds and perspectives. The professors are knowledgeable and supportive and academic advising has been very helpful. I like the location and access to real-world learning opportunities. However, coming from a small town into a city atmosphere was a little challenging but overall, VCU has helped me grow academically, personally, and professionally.
Not too bad just expensive and some professors could be a little better with their teaching styles. Y

Virginia Commonwealth University is the perfect college for you if you value student diversity, engaging campus life, as well as amiable staff. There's always something going on at campus whether it be a block party, social event with the dean or even a basketball game. There are plenty of opportunities to meet new people and build connections.

ai_summary: Virginia Tech offers a vibrant college experience with a strong sense of community , attractive campus, and a variety of dining options that many students rave about. Students appreciate the wide array of extracurricular activities, including sports and clubs, catering to different interests, although some note that the social scene can lean heavily toward partying. Academically, faculty care about student success, with many professors being described as supportive and accommodating, especially in smaller class settings. However, there are concerns about the efficiency of administration and larger class sizes in some programs, which can affect individual attention. While the campus and surrounding Blacksburg are praised for their beauty, some reviews mention limited off-campus diversity and social options, alongside high housing costs . Overall, students express a strong sense of belonging , often referring to Virginia Tech as a second home, filled with opportunities for personal and academic growth.

reviews.text: My experience at Virginia Tech exceeded my expectations, I learned endless lessons that went beyond the classrooms.
I love Virginia Tech. It is a great school for students who are strong academically, but love to have fun. The Dining halls are unmatched with so many different options that youll never get sick of.

Overall, my experience at Virginia Tech has been very positive. The sense of community stands out the most—there’s a strong “Hokie spirit” that makes it easy to feel connected, whether through classes, events, or campus traditions. The campus itself is beautiful and well-maintained, and there are plenty of opportunities to get involved in clubs, research, and internships. Many professors are knowledgeable and genuinely care about student success, especially within major-specific programs.
That said, there are a few areas that could be improved. Some larger classes can feel impersonal, making it harder to get individualized support. Additionally, campus dining and housing can be expensive, and availability isn’t always ideal. Expanding mental health resources and reducing wait times for counseling services would also make a big difference for students.
I'm excited to attend because the Biochemistry department looks to be really rigorous, engaging, and uplifting.

Virginia Tech was definitely a lot for me when I first started school there, just because of how big the school and student population are. But after the first month, I felt very well adjusted to the large campus life and began enjoying myself. I stayed so busy in the past year, and it has been a blast. There's so much to do here with academics, interest clubs, athletics, and there's usually always a party going down. It's been a great past year, and many memories were made, and I can't wait to spend more time at VT. Go HOKIES!!!

Virginia Tech is a beautiful campus with access to a variety of activities to fit anyone's interest. On campus food selections are great - but get in line early cause there isn't enough staff to accommodate the students. Off campus there are a variety of places to purchase premade meals or groceries stores using your Hokie card.

Virginia Tech's sense of community is unmatched. From the moment I stepped onto campus, the "Hokie Nation" felt like a genuine family. The mentorship opportunities, like my LEAP program experience, were transformative—allowing me to support younger students and build real connections. The service trip to Appalachia through my school's program opened my eyes to the power of community engagement beyond campus. Navigating a predominantly white institution as a biracial student came with moments of isolation. While many peers were welcoming, I occasionally felt the weight of being an "other" in spaces where my identity wasn't immediately understood. The social scene sometimes felt segmented, making it harder to find community across difference. Virginia Tech gave me invaluable experiences in mentorship, service, and navigating diverse perspectives. It wasn't perfect, but it taught me how to build bridges—a skill I'll carry everywhere.

I loved everything about Virginia Tech I would stay forever if I could. Academics were great, plenty of opportunities to get involved as a student, surrounding area is a small town but very welcoming.

Virginia Tech is THE school - Enter Sandman, it has it all, a science major with excellent academic research opportunities. The social scene in a small town is awesome – tons of sports and clubs has something for everyone. Working around campus, you are surrounded by Hokie stone….

Attending Tech was the best decision. The community spirit is so unique and everyone is supportive and friendly. There is a club or organisation for everyone so you’ll not be bored. Blacksburg is a small college town but there are always events happening and the surrounding area is truly beautiful!
I loved my time at Virginia Tech. The town is really cute and has everything you need. Campus is accessible and the food is great!

As a student at Virginia Tech, I genuinely feel like I’m in a place that pushes me to grow in every direction. The academics challenge me to think deeper and work harder, especially in engineering, where hands-on projects and real problem-solving make what I learn feel meaningful and practical. Beyond the classroom, the atmosphere on campus is something special — there’s a strong sense of pride, community, and tradition that makes being a Hokie feel like being part of something bigger than myself. Whether it’s walking across the Drillfield, going to games at Lane Stadium, or just studying with friends, there’s an energy here that keeps me motivated. I also appreciate how the university connects students to internships, research, and career opportunities, making me feel like I’m not just earning a degree, but building a future.
Living on-campus at Virginia Tech is a great experience. Dining halls are easily accessible and the food is good. Its a short walk to downtown Blacksburg and the people are very friendly
I am enjoying my time on this beautiful campus. Definitely something every undergrad needs to experience at least once.

the experiences of classes and the outside life really speaks to me, I really love the diversity, I love being able to share experiences with people from all around the world, from the sunny sides of Dubai to the bright city of Tokyo

ai_summary: Wake Forest University garners a mix of opinions from students, particularly around its campus culture and academic environment. Many appreciate the rigorous academics and small class sizes , highlighting the accessibility and support from professors, which fosters a collaborative learning atmosphere . However, the dominance of Greek life in social scenarios is a point of contention; students with varied interests may feel excluded, especially those less inclined towards the party scene. Reviews frequently mention the campus's beauty and the quality of facilities, though some students express dissatisfaction with the lack of diversity and inclusivity, feeling it caters primarily to affluent, predominantly white students. While there are strong career support resources available, some feel there's a disconnect when it comes to accommodating lower-income students and first-generation college students. The experience at Wake Forest can thus vary significantly depending on individual backgrounds and social preferences.

reviews.text: Wake is a pretty odd place. The academics are tough, grade inflation doesn’t keep up with other universities of the same caliber, and it’s very much a work hard play hard kind of place. Some people here are interesting but a huge chunk of campus doesn’t do much aside from school and their fraternity/sorority. The student body is very close because outside of campus winston salem is pretty dead. I’d say if you’re thinking about applying here, make sure you have connections in a frat/sorority and/or a whole lot of money. Otherwise the social scene is pretty bad. You’ll meet people like you, but you just can’t get the same variety that you can at a big school. If I was to pick a college again, I personally don’t think wake would be the best fit for me, but I’m not disappointed in my choice.

Greek life is driving all social life on campus. If you have a student who does not want to participate, or does not get accepted into sorority or fraternity, it is really difficult to find something else on campus.

Wake Forest University is a small university, but due to its small size, you get a lot of benefits. The small class sizes allow you to get to know the teachers well and ask questions if you need help. There are also lots of opportunities that are really accessible if you make use of them. I would say the biggest positives of going to Wake are the academic opportunities, the professors, research, and ability to get involved in a variety of different activities. The biggest negatives are the small undergraduate student body, the cost, and the housing requirement.

Wake Forest is an amazing school academically full of resources that are really catered to help students thrive. When I struggled with my classes, I had professors who reached out because they noticed a decline in my performance and they set me up with free services around campus and would work with me and tutor me on difficult topics. The student-professor relationships are very rare, however the turn around and that the school is dominated by Greek Life and seriously lacks diversity.

Wake Forest has provided me with endless resources and amazing rigor to master and succeed in the subjects that I am interested in. Although there is a steep language and liberal arts requirement, the school preaches and enacts holistic student development and truly does a good job of teaching its students.

Please choose another school if you are unwilling to follow a specific social norm set by this campus. Otherwise you will likely stand out very uncomfortably and experience a lot of social isolation, especially when winston-salem is in the middle of nowhere. The degree will not open doors for you, and the overall environment is very superficial, image-driven, and vanity-based. The diversity component rated as 'A' is such a joke.
It’s a beautiful site to see food is amazing business class is amazing as well it has set me up for life

The academics here at Wake are exceptional; however, if you are a guy who is unsure about Greek life, I highly encourage you not to come to Wake Forest. You have to be fully committed to it from the start to have a chance of getting into a frat. There is NOTHING to do on Friday nights besides going to the frats. If you get "cut" from their email lists (which 70% of you all will, and some cut the day before Halloween, leaving you completely skrewed over), you will have nothing to do socially on Fridays or Saturdays. It feels like 80% of the student body are wannabe southerners from the northeast. However, if you're from a private high school and you want to come to an almost fully white school whose student body is cliquey, snobby, and pretentious, this is the place for you!

Wake Forest has a lot going for it, a strong motto, beautiful campus, strong academics and strong athletics. But, there is a glaring issue around social life, particularly for boys/men, that makes a lot of people leave or regret their decision. Despite how Wake talks about it, "dirty rush" for boys starts the minute you step on campus and creates a lot of stress when you are still acclimating to college. There are only a few frats even considered worth your time and there are not a lot of spots. If you're not part of the New England private school scene already it is a real challenge to get on and stay on the "lists". The frat parties are exclusive and there is not much else going on so the whole thing can be depressing. Most males are not happy here, except the few chosen frat boys. There is a real lack of diversity that has hurt Wake in the rankings and the Connecticut run Greek scene, that is anything but inclusive, will not help them is this regard.

I seriously cannot stress this enough: if you are not from the northeast and know 10-15 people already here and in your freshman class you will have a very difficult time socially. The school is already extremely difficult and if you don’t already have connections within Greek life odds are you won’t get a bid, and even if you get one you’ll likely still feel left out. No bars and the frat parties are nothing compared to most other schools. Known for having a party scene but it’s mostly just sheltered kids from prep schools who are just excited to be able to drink for the first time. Overall not for most people, and if you are seriously considering it but don’t know anyone here, look elsewhere because you probably won’t be able to make many friends.
WFU is very PWI, whereas if your mindset is not strong enough to go to a PWI, I'd suggest go somewhere else.

I graduated in 2016, I’m not sure why I’m writing this review now but I felt the urge to review it. Wake Forest has a beautiful campus and Winston-Salem is a decent college town with just the right amount of activities.
It was wonderful being surrounded by intelligent, well-rounded, and motivated people. Academic life is challenging and I remember struggling a lot. They don’t provide a lot of career help and there are not that many majors to choose from. Socially, it can be really hard to fit in because Greek life is so prevalent and if you don’t fit into that it’s hard to find a group.

I loved Wake Forest so much - I felt so cared for by the university, I made wonderful friends and enjoyed being in the company of bright people, and my academic life was forever (positively!) changed.

I graduated in 2020, and Wake Forest was one of the worst experiences of my life. It's sold as a top-30 school that guarantees success, but the reality is harsh. Academics are extremely intense—my first freshman class required a 15-page paper. There’s no time for balance or social life. The grading feels unfair, and departments are small with limited professors. If you’re from a public school, you’ll likely feel out of place socially. Greek life dominates, and diversity is almost nonexistent. The name didn’t help me post-grad; employers cared more about my GPA, which suffered here. Wake demands a lot but gives little in return. I wouldn’t attend again, though others should decide for themselves.

My experience with Wake Forest University has been both enriching and transformative. The university fosters a close-knit community where professors are genuinely invested in students’ growth. The small class sizes encouraged meaningful discussions and personal connections. Wake’s commitment to the teacher-scholar model provided numerous opportunities for research and mentorship. Beyond academics, campus life was vibrant and inclusive, with diverse organizations and service initiatives. The Pro Humanitate spirit was evident in every aspect of the university, instilling a lifelong commitment to service and leadership. Wake Forest challenged me to think critically, act compassionately, and emerge prepared for both career and life.

ai_summary: Walsh University presents itself as a supportive and community-oriented institution, particularly appealing to high school seniors looking for a small college atmosphere . Reviewers frequently highlight the strength of the faculty , noting their accessibility and dedication to student success, which is enhanced by the small class sizes that foster personal connections. While many appreciate the beautiful campus and resources available for both academic and personal development, there are shared concerns about limited extracurricular activities and dining options on campus. Students also mention the high tuition rates, with encouragement to seek external scholarships, especially for those who do not identify as Catholic or are not athletes. The atmosphere is described as welcoming, but some feel there is room for improvement in diversity and inclusivity. Overall, Walsh University stands out for its personalized education approach within a nurturing community, making it a good fit for students seeking those values.

reviews.text: When I first started at Walsh, I was somewhat nervous. Everyone was very helpful and kind. I was able to overcome how nervous I was and settle it quickly. They are prompt in helping with whatever situation it is. Campus, dining and activities are all top-notch.
Mediocre but there could be some better things like more diversity better party scenes there’s no parties because it’s a catholic college and it’s extremely strict so there’s no campus life at all

Do NOT go to Walsh University. The tuition cost is a rip-off especially compared to what you get; disengaged professors, terrible dorms/dining, ugly campus, etc. They use make believe sports ("sprint football") to boost attendance and take advantage of students

Walsh University is a small school, but I view that as its greatest asset—the professors are extremely personable and make a strong effort to help students on an individual basis. I have had a fairly easy time getting help during office hours, and it's not uncommon to find students with special research projects. Student interactions are similar, as most of them are very friendly. I was also surprised by how quickly they learned my name. A strong effort is made to plan events, but this has slowed since COVID-19.
Courses for STEM and Business majors are well constructed, so education is excellent in that sector. Unfortunately, I have heard from peers that the University is struggling to hire for computer science and art courses.
As for needed changes, the curricula for majors change too frequently and present confusion for both students and advisors. Bureaucracy can be inefficient, making it unnecessarily challenging to resolve issues with professors or to correct system errors.

Overall enjoyed my time at Walsh. Definitely made it feel like home while I was there. In the end, college experiances are whatever you want them to be. Just depends what you decide to put your time into.
It was cool. Not much to do but that’s out of the schools control. Needs to be some regulations on whose coaching teams.
Walsh University offers a wonderful faith-bassd education that greatly sets one up for a successful future.

Walsh was so warm and inviting and really felt like a tight knit community. The new school president made it even more so! As an adult student with children and a full time job, I felt like the faculty were very accommodating with my daily professional and personal life. They still provided a very balanced and robust liberal arts experience, even online.

Walsh University, located in North Canton, Ohio, is a private Catholic university known for its strong academic programs, particularly in business, nursing, and education. The campus is beautiful and the community is welcoming.
One of Walsh's strengths is its small class sizes, fostering a close-knit environment where students can build strong relationships with professors and classmates. The sense of community at Walsh is palpable, with students and faculty readily supporting each other.
However, I believe Walsh could benefit from expanding its course offerings to encompass a wider range of disciplines, particularly in STEM and the arts. Additionally, promoting diversity and inclusion on campus could further enhance the learning environment.
Overall, Walsh University provides a valuable educational experience. Its small class sizes, strong sense of community, and dedicated faculty make it a great choice for students seeking a private university setting.
I felt at home as soon as I stepped foot onto campus. I consider it my second home. I was nervous but I found my people almost instantly that I wouldn’t meet any other way.

Walsh University offers a unique blend of academic excellence and Catholic values, making it an excellent choice for students seeking a well-rounded education. One of the standout features is the university's strong programs, particularly in fields like nursing, business, and education. The Gary and Linda Byers School of Nursing, for instance, has received notable recognition, ranking #345 nationally in the Bachelor of Science in Nursing Programs category by U.S. News & World Report
Students also appreciate the small class sizes, with a student-faculty ratio of 13:1, allowing for personalized attention and mentorship from experienced professors ². The campus itself is situated in a small urban setting, providing easy access to amenities and cultural attractions in Canton
Walsh University has been nothing but supportive and encouraging during my transition as a transfer student.
So far, I love Walsh! The professors are experts in their fields and are extremely helpful if I have any struggles!

My Experience at walsh has been great, walsh really feels like they care about the student my Admissions counselor helped me with my transition from high school into college and without her caring about me going to walsh i never would have made it. The people here are amazing and Faith based in catholicism and other religions.

Change the food, the grill and cafe were good but overpriced
The dining hall was hit or miss with food, you had to be careful of some foods and people did get food poisoning every year

ai_summary: Washington State University (WSU) offers a supportive academic environment , with many professors dedicated to helping students succeed. The coursework is generally manageable, and while some classes may feel crowded, students appreciate the variety of majors and hands-on learning opportunities . The campus fosters a strong sense of community , where students often feel like part of a family and enjoy a vibrant student life filled with clubs and events. However, the surrounding area may feel limited during downtime, and the cost of attendance can be a concern for some. Overall, WSU is recognized for its welcoming atmosphere and the balance of social and academic life, making it a solid choice for many students.

reviews.text: I am a Freshman at Washington State University. So far my experience has been good but at times I feel like the school is not setting me up for success as much as it could.
n/a I do not want to write a review so I will not. My overall experience was I was a student at washington state university

I had the opportunity to spend time at Washington State University for a campus experience, even though I am not full-time student there. During my visit, I was impresses by how welcoming and friendly the environment felt. The campus was clean, well-organized and had a strong sense of community. I appreciated seeing the facilities and learning more about the academic programs and student life. The staff and students I interacted with were helpful and enthusiastic, which made the experience enjoyable and informative. It gave me a better understanding of what the university offers and the overall atmosphere on campus. Overall, my experience at Washington State University was positive and left a strong impression.

WSU is a very welcoming school, it's unlike anyother school I've been to. Coming from a very small school, with a graduating class to Pullman was scary at first, but once I found my way, I fell in love with the school

I was very hesitant about going to WSU as a transfer student after my time at EWU, but attending WSU was the best decision I made in my life. The academics are competitive and on par with other top universities, I also like the campus life that is present all throughout the university. I would recommend WSU to anybody interested in going to a 4 year university.
